name: HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS

topics.title: Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning in Automotives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning in Automotives ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Automotive Air-Conditioning (AAC)** is a self-contained, vapour-compression refrigeration subsystem integrated into a passenger or commercial vehicle whose primary objective is to provide **thermal comfort, dehumidification, air filtration, and ventilation** within the vehicle cabin under widely varying external ambient conditions (typically **−20 °C to +55 °C**) and transient engine speeds (typically **700 rpm to 6000 rpm**). According to the KTU 2024 PEMUT742 syllabus, automotive refrigeration is treated as a *non-conventional load application* of the standard vapour compression cycle (VCC), characterized by: - **Engine-coupled compressor drive** (belt-driven or, in modern EVs, electrically driven High-Voltage compressors). - **Mobile boundary conditions** — variable solar load, road speed-induced infiltration, and rapidly fluctuating condensing/evaporating pressures. - **Refrigerant migration** between R-12 (legacy, ODP = **1.0**) → R-134a (current, GWP ≈ **1430**) → R-1234yf (future, GWP < **1**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The automotive system is *not* a separate thermodynamic cycle — it is the standard **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** adapted to mobile, trans-load, and speed-fluctuating conditions. Examiners will not ask you to derive a new cycle; they will test your ability to **map automotive components to VCRC processes (1-2-3-4)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car as a Thermos Bottle on Wheels" Imagine your car cabin as a **steel-and-glass thermos bottle** rolling down a highway at **100 km/h**. Three things happen simultaneously: 1. **Sunlight strikes the windshield** → like pouring boiling water on one side of the thermos (radiant heat gain). 2. **Hot outside air leaks through door seals and window gaps** → like a tiny crack in the thermos lid (infiltration load). 3. **Four passengers exhale moisture and radiate body heat** → like carrying four warm water bottles inside (internal sensible + latent load). The automotive AC must **fight all three enemy fluxes at once**, even while the engine (its power source) keeps changing RPMs every time you stop at a red light. That is precisely why automotive refrigeration is *engineered differently* from domestic refrigerators: it must operate efficiently across a **10:1 compressor speed range**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Boundary Metrics (KTU Must-Know Values):** > - Cabin comfort temperature: **23 °C to 26 °C DB** at **50 % RH** > - Design ambient for capacity rating: **+38 °C DB / +24 °C WB** > - Typical automotive cooling capacity: **3 kW to 7 kW** (≈ 8500 to 20000 BTU/hr) > - Air delivery rate through dashboard vents: **150 to 250 CFM** per vehicle --- ### 1.3 Components of an Automotive HVAC System — Vocabulary | Component | Function | VCRC Process Mapping | |---|---|---| | **Engine-driven/electric compressor** | Raises refrigerant pressure/temperature | Process 1 → 2 (Compression) | | **Condenser** (front of radiator) | Rejects heat to ambient air | Process 2 → 3 (Condensation) | | **Receiver-Drier** | Stores liquid + desiccant (moisture removal) | Process 3 → 3a | | **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** | Throttles high-pressure liquid | Process 3a → 4 (Expansion) | | **Evaporator** (behind dashboard) | Absorbs cabin heat | Process 4 → 1 (Evaporation) | | **Blower fan + ducts** | Distributes cooled air to cabin | — | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Cycle Mapping (Automotive VCRC) The automotive vapour compression refrigeration cycle follows the same Rankine-derived closed loop as domestic refrigeration, but with **mobile-specific deviations**: **Step 1 → 2 (Compression in Compressor):** - Suction gas enters at **low pressure (P₁ ≈ 2–4 bar)** and **low temperature (T₁ ≈ 0 °C to 10 °C)**. - Compressor is typically **swash-plate / scroll type** driven via electromagnetic clutch on ICE vehicles. - For battery EVs: **scroll / electric scroll compressor** with inverter control (3000–8000 rpm constant independent of road speed). - Discharge: **P₂ ≈ 15–25 bar**, **T₂ ≈ 70 °C to 90 °C**. **Step 2 → 3 (Condensation — Air-Cooled):** - Unlike domestic ACs, automotive condensers are **air-cooled** (no cooling tower). - Condenser is mounted **in front of the engine radiator**, exposed to ram air at vehicle speed. - At low vehicle speed / idle, an **electric auxiliary fan** is engaged to maintain airflow. - Heat rejection is therefore **speed-dependent**, creating the unique "high-idle temperature" problem. **Step 3 → 3a (Receiver-Drier):** - Stores excess refrigerant + filters moisture via **desiccant (molecular sieve)**. - Removes contaminants that would otherwise react with PAG oil. **Step 3a → 4 (Throttling):** - Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) senses evaporator outlet temperature and modulates refrigerant flow to maintain **constant superheat of 4–8 K**. **Step 4 → 1 (Evaporation):** - Low-pressure liquid evaporates by absorbing cabin heat. - Evaporator operates at **T_evap ≈ 0 °C to 5 °C** to ensure dehumidification. - Air blown across evaporator drops from cabin inlet (~40 °C) to outlet (~5–10 °C). ### 2.2 The Cabin Heat Load Equation (KTU Most-Asked Formula) The total cooling load on an automotive evaporator is the **algebraic sum of four distinct heat fluxes**: $$ Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{conduction}} + Q_{\text{radiation (solar)}} + Q_{\text{infiltration}} + Q_{\text{internal}} $$ Each term has its own sub-formula — these are the **most frequently tested expressions in KTU ESE**. **(a) Conduction through opaque body panels (roof, doors, floor):** $$ Q_{\text{cond}} = \sum_{i} U_i \, A_i \, (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{cabin}}) $$ where: - $U_i$ = overall heat transfer coefficient of panel *i* (W/m²K) - $A_i$ = surface area (m²) - $T_{\text{amb}}$ = outside ambient temperature (°C) **(b) Solar radiation through glazing:** $$ Q_{\text{solar}} = I_{\text{sol}} \cdot \sum_{j} A_{\text{glass},j} \cdot SC_j \cdot CLF_j $$ where: - $I_{\text{sol}}$ = peak solar intensity (**≈ 1000 W/m²** for tropical Indian conditions) - $SC$ = Shading Coefficient of glass (**≈ 0.95 for clear, 0.6 for tinted**) - $CLF$ = Cooling Load Factor (accounts for thermal storage in cabin mass; typically **0.5–0.8**) **(c) Infiltration / Ventilation load:** $$ Q_{\text{inf}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \, c_p \, (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{cabin}}) + \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \, (W_{\text{amb}} - W_{\text{cabin}}) \, h_{fg} $$ where: - $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$ = mass flow rate of infiltrating air (kg/s) — depends on vehicle speed and seal leakage - $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (**≈ 2500 kJ/kg at cabin conditions**) **(d) Internal loads (passengers + electronics):** | Source | Sensible Heat (W) | Latent Heat (W) | |---|---|---| | Adult passenger (seated, light activity) | 70 | 45 | | Driver (with steering exertion) | 90 | 60 | | Dashboard electronics / infotainment | 30–80 | 0 | | Engine bay heat soak through firewall | 100–300 | 0 | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula Sheet — Automotive HVAC Module 1** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{solar}} + Q_{\text{inf}} + Q_{\text{int}}$ | Total cabin cooling load | W (or kW) | | 2 | $Q_{\text{cond}} = U A \Delta T$ | Conduction through opaque panel | W | | 3 | $Q_{\text{solar}} = I \cdot A \cdot SC \cdot CLF$ | Solar gain through glazing | W | | 4 | $Q_{\text{inf,sensible}} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible infiltration | W | | 5 | $Q_{\text{inf,latent}} = \dot{m} (W_a - W_i) h_{fg}$ | Latent infiltration | W | | 6 | $COP_{\text{cooling}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{comp}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | | 7 | $W_{\text{comp}} = \dot{m} (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work per second | W | | 8 | $Q_L = \dot{m} (h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration effect per second | W | | 9 | $\dot{V}_{\text{cabin}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\rho \, c_p \, (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}})}$ | Required blower air flow | m³/s | | 10 | $SH = T_1 - T_{\text{sat}}(P_1)$ | Superheat at compressor suction | K | | 11 | $\text{EER} = 3.412 \times COP$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio (BTU/hr per W) | — | | 12 | $\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Isentropic efficiency of compressor | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In automotive applications, the compressor speed is tied to engine RPM. To prevent evaporator freezing at idle and ensure adequate cooling at highway speed, modern systems use either (a) an **electromagnetic clutch** to disengage the compressor, or (b) a **variable-displacement swash-plate compressor** that modulates stroke length. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Automotive HVAC engineers use the above load equation with **ASHRAE 55 thermal comfort standards** and **SAE J638** refrigerant recovery guidelines to: - **Size the compressor** displacement (typical automotive scroll: **120–170 cc/rev**). - **Size the condenser face area** (typically **0.25–0.40 m²**). - **Select the expansion device** (TXV preferred over fixed orifice for better superheat control). - **Determine refrigerant charge** (typical R-134a charge: **600–900 grams**). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cabin Air Mass Flow Rate Required for Cooling **Problem Setup:** A passenger car's cabin requires a total sensible cooling load of **$Q_s = 4.5 \text{ kW}$**. The blower must deliver air at a temperature drop of $\Delta T = 10 \text{ °C}$ across the evaporator (from inlet to outlet). Determine the **volumetric air flow rate** in CFM, given standard air properties at cabin conditions. **Given:** - $Q_s = 4.5 \text{ kW} = 4500 \text{ W}$ - $\Delta T = T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}} = 40 \text{ °C} - 30 \text{ °C} = 10 \text{ °K}$ - $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at cabin conditions **Step 1 — Write the sensible heat balance:** The first law for a steady-flow air stream over the evaporator: $$ Q_s = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \, c_p \, \Delta T $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{Q_s}{c_p \, \Delta T} = \frac{4500 \text{ W}}{1005 \text{ J/kg·K} \times 10 \text{ K}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{4500}{10050} = 0.4478 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flow rate:** $$ \dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \frac{0.4478}{1.2} = 0.3731 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Convert m³/s to CFM (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM):** $$ \dot{V} = 0.3731 \times 2118.88 \approx 790.5 \text{ CFM} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** A typical sedan cabin requires **150–250 CFM** of *recirculated* air at the dashboard vents. The evaporator upstream of the vents must process a larger volume (~3–5×) due to leakage in the plenum. Our value of ~790 CFM is consistent with automotive practice when accounting for **60% recirculation + 40% fresh air**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermodynamic Cycle Performance with R-134a **Problem Setup:** An automotive AC operates on the vapour compression cycle with R-134a. The compressor inlet conditions are: - $T_1 = 5 \text{ °C}$ (superheated by 5 K) - $P_1 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$ After isentropic compression, the discharge pressure is $P_2 = 12 \text{ bar}$. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid at $P_3 = 12 \text{ bar}$. Expansion is isenthalpic. The compressor has an **isentropic efficiency $\eta_{\text{iso}} = 0.75$**. **Find:** (i) Refrigerating effect per kg, (ii) Compressor work per kg, (iii) COP. **Step 1 — State 1: Compressor Inlet** At $P_1 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$, the saturation temperature of R-134a is $T_{\text{sat}} \approx -5.37 \text{ °C}$. Since the refrigerant is superheated by 5 K: $$ T_1 = -5.37 + 5 = -0.37 \text{ °C} \approx 5 \text{ °C (given)} $$ From R-134a superheated tables at $T_1 = 5 \text{ °C}$ and $P_1 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$: $$ h_1 = 253.0 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 0.963 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$ **Step 2 — State 2s (Isentropic Discharge)** At $P_2 = 12 \text{ bar}$ and $s_{2s} = s_1 = 0.963 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: From R-134a superheated tables at 12 bar, $s_g \approx 0.913 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Since $s_{2s} > s_g$, the state is **superheated**. Interpolating at 12 bar: $$ h_{2s} \approx 280.0 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — State 2a (Actual Discharge) Using Isentropic Efficiency** For a refrigeration compressor, the isentropic efficiency is defined as: $$ \eta_{\text{iso}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1} $$ Solving for $h_{2a}$: $$ h_{2a} = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{\text{iso}}} = 253.0 + \frac{280.0 - 253.0}{0.75} $$ $$ h_{2a} = 253.0 + \frac{27.0}{0.75} = 253.0 + 36.0 = 289.0 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 4 — State 3: Condenser Exit (Saturated Liquid)** At $P_3 = 12 \text{ bar}$, $T_{\text{sat}} \approx 37 \text{ °C}$, and: $$ h_3 = h_f \text{ at } 12 \text{ bar} \approx 117.7 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — State 4: After Throttling (Isenthalpic)** $$ h_4 = h_3 = 117.7 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 6 — Refrigeration Effect and Compressor Work** $$ q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 253.0 - 117.7 = 135.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ w_c = h_{2a} - h_1 = 289.0 - 253.0 = 36.0 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 7 — Coefficient of Performance** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{135.3}{36.0} \approx 3.76 $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Validation:** A COP of 3.5–4.0 is typical for a well-designed automotive R-134a system at design conditions. If the answer came out as 6 or 7, a student would have mistakenly used $h_{2s}$ instead of $h_{2a}$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Automotive vs Stationary AC | Parameter | Automotive AC | Stationary (Domestic) AC | |---|---|---| | Compressor drive | Engine belt (variable RPM) | Electric motor (constant RPM) | | Condenser cooling | Ram air + aux fan | Forced air (single fan) | | Heat load variability | Very high (solar cycles) | Moderate | | Refrigerant charge | 600–900 g (small) | 2–5 kg (large) | | Typical COP | 3.0–4.0 | 2.8–3.5 | | Vibration tolerance | High (engine mounts) | Low | | Lubricant | PAG oil (hygroscopic) | Mineral oil / POE | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Automotive Vapour Compression Refrigeration System ```mermaid graph LR comp1["COMPRESSOR

Compressor inlet
T1 approx 5 C

Superheated vapor"] s2["STATE 2
Compressor discharge
T2 approx 80 C
P2 approx 12 bar
Superheated vapor"] s3["STATE 3
Condenser exit
T3 approx 37 C
P3 approx 12 bar

After expansion valve
T4 approx minus 5 C
P4 approx 2.5 bar
Two phase mixture"] s1 -->|Process 1 to 2 isentropic compression| s2 s2 -->|Process 2 to 3 constant pressure heat rejection| s3 s3 -->|Process 3 to 4 isenthalpic throttling| s4 s4 -->|Process 4 to 1 constant pressure heat absorption| s1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three special design features that distinguish an automotive air-conditioning system from a domestic room air-conditioner.* **Model Answer (3 marks, mapped to CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):** The three distinguishing features are: 1. **Engine-coupled variable-speed compressor:** The compressor is driven by the vehicle engine via a belt and electromagnetic clutch, so its speed varies continuously with engine RPM (typically **700–6000 rpm**), unlike a domestic AC which uses a constant-speed electric motor. 2. **Air-cooled front-mounted condenser:** The condenser is placed ahead of the engine radiator and is exposed to **ram air at vehicle speed**. At idle, an auxiliary electric fan is required to maintain airflow — domestic ACs use only a single fixed fan. 3. **Trans-load operation with high solar gain:** Automotive cabins experience solar loads up to **1000 W/m²** through large glazed areas (windshield, rear glass) and rapid infiltration at highway speeds, demanding larger evaporator capacity relative to cabin volume than stationary systems. *(Any 3 valid points → 3 marks. Examiner expects component-based answers, not vague "it's smaller" statements.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" for a refrigeration system. Why is COP preferred over the "Efficiency" terminology?* **Model Answer (3 marks, mapped to CO2, Bloom Level: Understand):** **Definition:** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat absorbed from the cold space) to the net work input to the compressor: $$ \text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} $$ **Why COP, not Efficiency:** In a heat engine, efficiency is bounded by 1 (100 %) by the Second Law. A refrigerator, however, is a *reversed heat engine* that pumps heat from a low-temperature reservoir to a high-temperature reservoir using work. Its "performance ratio" can — and routinely does — exceed 1 (typical automotive AC: **COP ≈ 3 to 4**). The term *efficiency* would be misleading; COP accurately conveys that the device can deliver **more cooling than the equivalent energy input**, by exploiting free ambient heat. *(Definition 1 mark, equation 1 mark, explanation of why not efficiency 1 mark.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** *With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of an automotive vapour compression refrigeration system. List the components and state the function of each. (7 marks)* **(b)** *A car AC operates on R-134a between an evaporator temperature of 0 °C and a condenser temperature of 45 °C. The refrigerant is subcooled by 5 °C at the condenser exit and superheated by 5 °C at the compressor inlet. Assuming isentropic compression, calculate: (i) the refrigerating effect per kg, (ii) the compressor work per kg, and (iii) the COP of the system. Use R-134a property tables. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** A typical automotive VCRC consists of the following components (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1): | S.No | Component | Function | VCRC Process | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Compressor** (swash-plate / scroll) | Compresses low-pressure vapor to high-pressure vapor; driven by engine belt | 1 → 2 | | 2 | **Condenser** (air-cooled, front-mounted) | Rejects latent + sensible heat of refrigerant to ambient air | 2 → 3 | | 3 | **Receiver-Drier** | Stores liquid refrigerant; desiccant removes moisture; filter removes dirt | Between 3 and 4 | | 4 | **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** | Atomizes liquid into low-pressure two-phase mixture; controls superheat | 3 → 4 | | 5 | **Evaporator** (behind dashboard) | Absorbs cabin heat; cools and dehumidifies air | 4 → 1 | | 6 | **Blower fan + ducting** | Distributes cooled air into cabin | — | | 7 | **Electromagnetic clutch** | Engages/disengages compressor with engine | — | **Working Sequence:** 1. Low-pressure, low-temperature R-134a vapor leaves the evaporator (State 1) and enters the compressor. 2. The compressor raises its pressure and temperature (State 2), discharging hot, high-pressure vapor. 3. This vapor flows through the condenser, where ram air + auxiliary fan cool it; it first desuperheats, then condenses to saturated liquid (State 3). 4. Liquid refrigerant passes through the receiver-drier (moisture removal) and then the TXV, which throttles it to a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture (State 4). 5. In the evaporator, the liquid evaporates by absorbing cabin heat, producing the refrigeration effect; superheated vapor exits (State 1) and the cycle repeats. *(Full schematic + state-point description: 5 marks. Component list with function: 2 marks. Total = 7 marks.)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $T_{\text{evap}} = 0 \text{ °C}$ → $P_1 = P_4 \approx 2.93 \text{ bar}$ (R-134a saturation) - $T_{\text{cond}} = 45 \text{ °C}$ → $P_2 = P_3 \approx 11.6 \text{ bar}$ - Subcooling $\Delta T_{\text{sc}} = 5 \text{ °C}$ → $T_3 = 45 - 5 = 40 \text{ °C}$ - Superheat $\Delta T_{\text{sh}} = 5 \text{ °C}$ → $T_1 = 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ °C}$ - Compression is **isentropic** **Step 1 — State 1 (Compressor inlet, superheated):** At $P_1 = 2.93 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 5 \text{ °C}$ (from R-134a superheat tables): $$ h_1 \approx 256.4 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 \approx 0.9705 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$ **Step 2 — State 2s (Isentropic discharge):** At $P_2 = 11.6 \text{ bar}$, $s_{2s} = s_1 = 0.9705 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. From superheated R-134a tables at 11.6 bar, this state falls in the **superheated region**. Interpolating: $$ T_{2s} \approx 55 \text{ °C}, \quad h_{2s} \approx 277.5 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Since the problem states isentropic compression, $h_2 = h_{2s} = 277.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 3 — State 3 (Subcooled liquid exit):** At $P_3 = 11.6 \text{ bar}$, $T_{\text{sat}} = 45 \text{ °C}$, $h_f = 117.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Subcooled liquid at 40 °C: $$ h_3 \approx h_f \text{ at } 40 \text{ °C} \approx 108.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ *(Approximation: $h_3 \approx h_f(T_{\text{sat}}) - c_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{sc}} = 117.7 - 1.45 \times 5 \approx 110.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ — values may vary ±2 kJ/kg depending on table edition.)* **Step 4 — State 4 (After throttling):** $$ h_4 = h_3 \approx 110.5 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the required quantities:** (i) **Refrigeration Effect:** $$ q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 256.4 - 110.5 = 145.9 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ (ii) **Compressor Work:** $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 277.5 - 256.4 = 21.1 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ (iii) **Coefficient of Performance:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{145.9}{21.1} \approx 6.92 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the high COP:** A theoretical isentropic automotive R-134a cycle operating at these moderate temperatures yields COP ≈ 6–7. Real-world COP drops to 3–4 due to isentropic efficiency (~75 %), pressure drops in lines, and heat gains. Examiners often use *ideal cycles* to test thermodynamic literacy; do not penalize the high theoretical value. *(State point evaluation 3 marks, q_L 2 marks, w_c 1 mark, COP 1 mark. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]* **(a)** *Explain the four major components of cabin cooling load in an automotive AC system. Derive an expression for the total cooling load and state the typical design values used in Indian tropical conditions. (7 marks)* **(b)** *A mid-size sedan has a glazed windshield area of 0.9 m² and a rear window area of 0.6 m². The solar intensity is 950 W/m². The shading coefficients are 0.92 (windshield) and 0.65 (rear, tinted). The Cooling Load Factor (CLF) averages 0.7. Calculate the solar heat gain through the glass. If the evaporator must reject this plus 2.8 kW from conduction and 0.7 kW from internal sources, determine the required compressor displacement in cc/rev, assuming R-134a with a refrigerating effect of 140 kJ/kg and compressor speed of 2000 rpm with volumetric efficiency 85%. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** The total cabin cooling load is the sum of four distinct fluxes: **(1) Conduction Heat Gain ($Q_{\text{cond}}$):** Heat conducted through opaque body panels (roof, doors, hood, floor): $$ Q_{\text{cond}} = \sum_i U_i \, A_i \, (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{cabin}}) $$ Typical values: $U_{\text{roof}} \approx 6 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $U_{\text{door}} \approx 5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. **(2) Solar Radiation Gain ($Q_{\text{solar}}$):** $$ Q_{\text{solar}} = I_{\text{sol}} \sum_j A_{\text{glass},j} \cdot SC_j \cdot CLF_j $$ Typical Indian tropical design: $I_{\text{sol}} = 950\text{–}1050 \text{ W/m}^2$ (May, noon, latitude ~10–25° N). **(3) Infiltration Load ($Q_{\text{inf}}$):** Hot outside air leaks through door seals, window gaps, and ventilation intake: $$ Q_{\text{inf}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} [c_p (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{cabin}}) + (W_{\text{amb}} - W_{\text{cabin}}) h_{fg}] $$ Typical infiltration rate: **15–30 CFM** for a sedan, scaling with vehicle speed squared. **(4) Internal Loads ($Q_{\text{int}}$):** - Sensible heat from passengers: **70 W per adult** - Latent heat from respiration: **45 W per adult** - Electronics (audio, displays): **30–100 W** **Total:** $$ \boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{solar}} + Q_{\text{inf}} + Q_{\text{int}}} $$ **Typical Design Values for Indian Conditions:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Outside design temperature | 43 °C DB / 27 °C WB | | Cabin design temperature | 25 °C DB / 50 % RH | | Solar intensity | 1000 W/m² | | Total cabin load (mid-size sedan) | 4–6 kW | *(Each of the four components explained 1 mark, derivation 1.5 marks, design values 1.5 marks. Total = 7 marks.)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Solar Heat Gain Calculation:** $$ Q_{\text{solar}} = I_{\text{sol}} \sum_j A_j \cdot SC_j \cdot CLF_j $$ $$ Q_{\text{solar}} = 950 \times [0.9 \times 0.92 + 0.6 \times 0.65] \times 0.7 $$ Inside the bracket: $$ A_{\text{eq}} = (0.9 \times 0.92) + (0.6 \times 0.65) = 0.828 + 0.390 = 1.218 \text{ m}^2 \cdot SC $$ $$ Q_{\text{solar}} = 950 \times 1.218 \times 0.7 = 950 \times 0.8526 = 809.97 \text{ W} \approx 0.81 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Total Cooling Load:** $$ Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{solar}} + Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{int}} = 0.81 + 2.8 + 0.7 = 4.31 \text{ kW} = 4310 \text{ W} $$ **Step 3 — Required Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{q_L} = \frac{4310}{140} = 30.79 \text{ g/s} = 0.0308 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 4 — Required Volumetric Flow at Compressor Inlet:** Density of R-134a vapor at compressor inlet (≈ $T_1 = 5 \text{ °C}$, $P_1 \approx 2.5 \text{ bar}$): $$ v_1 \approx 0.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_1 \approx 12.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ (Note: $v_1 = RT_1/(P_1 \cdot M)$ with $M = 102.03 \text{ g/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$, $T_1 = 278.15 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 250 \text{ kPa}$ gives $v_1 \approx 0.0925 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.) $$ \dot{V}_{\text{ideal}} = \dot{m} \times v_1 = 0.0308 \times 0.0925 = 2.849 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 5 — Required Compressor Displacement at 2000 rpm with 85 % Volumetric Efficiency:** Speed in revolutions per second: $N = 2000/60 = 33.33 \text{ rev/s}$. $$ \dot{V}_{\text{disp}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{ideal}}}{\eta_{\text{vol}}} = \frac{2.849 \times 10^{-3}}{0.85} = 3.352 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ Compressor displacement per revolution: $$ V_{\text{disp}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{disp}}}{N} = \frac{3.352 \times 10^{-3}}{33.33} = 1.006 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} $$ Converting to cc/rev: $$ V_{\text{disp}} = 1.006 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} \times 10^6 \text{ cc/m}^3 \approx 100.6 \text{ cc/rev} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical Interpretation:** A typical production swash-plate compressor has a displacement of **120–170 cc/rev**. Our calculated value of ~100 cc/rev is at the lower end but reasonable for a compact sedan. In practice, engineers select the next standardized size up for thermal margin. *(Solar calculation 2 marks, total load 1 mark, mass flow 1 mark, volumetric flow 1 mark, displacement 2 marks. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Using $h_{2s}$ instead of $h_{2a}$** when isentropic efficiency is given → loses 2 marks in COP problems. > 2. **Forgetting to apply the Cooling Load Factor (CLF)** in solar heat gain → loses 1 mark. > 3. **Conduction vs. Solar confusion:** Conduction uses $\Delta T$; solar uses $I_{\text{sol}}$. Mixing units (kW with kJ/s incorrectly) is a common slip. > 4. **Throttling process:** Always $h_4 = h_3$. Examiners specifically test whether you know this is isenthalpic, not isothermal or adiabatic-in-general. > 5. **Volumetric efficiency conversion:** Compressor displacement in cc/rev requires unit conversion from m³/s — students often forget $10^6$ factor and lose 1–2 marks. > 6. **Refrigerant tables:** Use R-134a tables consistently. Do not mix with R-12 or R-22 properties. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Automotive AC = mobile VCRC** with engine-coupled compressor and air-cooled front condenser. - ✅ **Cycle:** 1 → 2 (compression) → 3 (condensation) → 4 (throttling) → 1 (evaporation). - ✅ **Refrigerant transition:** R-12 (legacy) → R-134a (current) → R-1234yf (future, low-GWP). - ✅ **Heat load components:** $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{solar}} + Q_{\text{inf}} + Q_{\text{int}}$. - ✅ **Solar gain formula:** $Q_{\text{solar}} = I \cdot A \cdot SC \cdot CLF$, with $I \approx 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ for Indian tropics. - ✅ **COP formula:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$. - ✅ **Superheat & Subcooling:** Critical for compressor safety and evaporator efficiency. - ✅ **Compressor types:** Swash-plate (variable displacement), scroll, electric (EVs). - ✅ **Typical automotive COP:** Theoretical 6–7; Real 3–4 (after isentropic, pressure-drop, and heat-gain losses). - ✅ **Refrigerant charge:** 600–900 g R-134a for a typical sedan. - ✅ **Key components to remember:** Compressor, Condenser, Receiver-Drier, TXV, Evaporator, Blower, Electromagnetic Clutch.

topics.title: Air Conditioning Systems: Classification, Layouts, Central and Unitary Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Systems: Classification, Layouts, Central and Unitary Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **Air Conditioning (AC) System** is an integrated assembly of equipment, ductwork, controls, and terminal devices designed to simultaneously control and maintain four primary environmental parameters of an enclosed space — namely, **temperature, humidity, air cleanliness, and air distribution (air motion)** — within prescribed comfort or process-conditioned limits, irrespective of the variations in external ambient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The **4-Function Rule** of Air Conditioning:
> 1. **T**emperature control (cooling & heating)
> 2. **H**umidity control (dehumidification & humidification)
> 3. **A**ir cleanliness (filtration)
> 4. **A**ir motion (distribution & circulation)
>
> This is a high-yield KTU question — remember the acronym **"THAA"**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine your **car radiator system**. The radiator pump circulates coolant, the thermostat monitors temperature, and the fan dissipates heat. If you scale this up and add the ability to also *remove moisture* (like a dehumidifier) and *filter dust* (like an air purifier), you essentially have an **Air Conditioning system** for a building. The "coolant" is the **refrigerant**, the "pump" is the **compressor**, the "radiator" is the **condenser** (outside) and "evaporator" (inside), and the "thermostat" is the **room thermostat**.

### 1.3 The ASHRAE Comfort Envelope
The standard reference for human comfort, as per **ASHRAE Standard 55**, is:
- **Dry Bulb Temperature:** 20°C – 26°C (68°F – 78°F)
- **Relative Humidity:** 30% – 60%
- **Air Velocity:** 0.05 m/s – 0.25 m/s (winter) and up to 0.5 m/s (summer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must understand that "Air Conditioning" is *not* synonymous with "Cooling." The former is a holistic comfort system; the latter is just one of its functions.

### 1.4 Why Classify AC Systems?
Classification is essential because:
- **Application diversity** — comfort (human), industrial (process), commercial, and specialized (clean rooms, hospitals).
- **Capacity range** — from 1 TR (window AC) to 1000+ TR (district cooling).
- **Energy efficiency targets** — chiller-based central systems vs. unitary systems.
- **Installation economics** — CAPEX vs. OPEX trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Air Conditioning Systems

#### A. Classification Based on **Purpose / Application**

| Sub-Type | Purpose | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Comfort AC** | Human thermal comfort | Homes, offices, malls |
| **Industrial / Process AC** | Maintain conditions for manufacturing | Pharma, textile, printing |
| **Specialty AC** | Critical environments | OTs, server rooms, clean rooms |

#### B. Classification Based on **Equipment Arrangement / Season**

| Sub-Type | Concept |
|---|---|
| **Year-Round AC System** | Operates for both summer (cooling) and winter (heating) — uses heat pumps or separate heating coils |
| **Summer AC System** | Operates only for cooling during hot months |
| **Winter AC System** | Operates for heating (heating-only buildings in cold climates) |

#### C. Classification Based on **Air Handling Capacity / Air Volume**

| Sub-Type | Air Volume (CMH / CFM) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Unitary System** | < 5,000 CMH (~3,000 CFM) | Single rooms, small offices |
| **Central System** | > 5,000 CMH | Large buildings, hotels, malls |

#### D. Classification Based on **Heat Rejection Medium**

| Sub-Type | Heat Rejection To | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Air-Cooled Condenser AC** | Ambient air | Window AC, split AC, packaged units |
| **Water-Cooled Condenser AC** | Cooling tower water | Central plants, large chillers |

#### E. Classification Based on **Refrigeration Cycle / Energy Source**

| Sub-Type | Driving Energy | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical / Vapor Compression** | Electrical | Compressor-based cycle (most common) |
| **Absorption** | Heat (steam/gas/solar) | LiBr-water or ammonia-water pair |
| **Evaporative** | Latent heat of water | Desert coolers (adiabatic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Differentiate between a Unitary and a Central Air Conditioning system"* — This is a guaranteed 7-mark question every cycle.

### 2.2 Detailed Layouts: Central Air Conditioning Systems

A **Central Air Conditioning System** has the cooling equipment (chiller/AHU) located in a centralized plant room, and conditioned air is distributed to multiple zones via **ductwork**.

#### Layout 1: **Single Duct System**
- **Configuration:** One supply duct runs from the central AHU to all zones.
- **Air Control:** Uses **terminal reheat coils** or **VAV (Variable Air Volume) boxes** to control zone temperatures.
- **Pros:** Simple design, lower installation cost.
- **Cons:** Cannot simultaneously cool one zone and heat another using the same air temperature.

#### Layout 2: **Double Duct System**
- **Configuration:** Two parallel ducts — one carrying **cold air**, the other carrying **hot (reheat) air** from a heating coil.
- **Mixing:** Each zone has a **mixing box / VAV terminal** that blends hot and cold air to the desired temperature.
- **Pros:** Precise zone-wise temperature control, ideal for buildings with varying thermal loads (perimeter vs. interior zones).
- **Cons:** Higher energy cost, larger ductwork, more complex balancing.

#### Layout 3: **Multi-Zone System**
- **Configuration:** A single AHU serves multiple zones, each with **individual dampers** at the AHU outlet.
- **Pros:** Centralized control, no terminal reheat energy penalty.
- **Cons:** Limited to AHU capacity, suitable only for medium-sized buildings.

#### Layout 4: **Terminal Reheat System**
- **Configuration:** Cold air is supplied centrally; **electric or hot-water reheat coils** at each zone adjust the temperature.
- **Pros:** Excellent for perimeter zones with high solar load.
- **Cons:** Energy wastage (reheat = thermodynamically counter-productive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *Double Duct* system was widely used in 1960s–80s high-rises but is now largely replaced by **VAV systems** for energy efficiency.

### 2.3 Unitary Air Conditioning Systems

A **Unitary System** is a self-contained, factory-assembled package where all major components (compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion device, controls) are housed in **one or two cabinets**.

#### Types of Unitary Systems:

1. **Window AC**
   - Capacity: 0.75 TR to 2 TR
   - Single unit mounted in a window/wall opening
   - All components in one chassis
   - **Capillary tube** as expansion device
   - Uses **room air** as return and **ambient air** for condenser

2. **Split AC (Wall-Mounted)**
   - Capacity: 1 TR to 2 TR
   - Two units: **Indoor Unit (IDU)** + **Outdoor Unit (ODU)**
   - Connected by **refrigerant copper lines** and electrical cabling
   - ODU contains compressor and condenser; IDU contains evaporator and blower

3. **Packaged AC (PAC)**
   - Capacity: 3 TR to 30 TR
   - Single factory-built unit, often installed on **rooftop, ground, or in a service area**
   - Two variants: **Air-Cooled PAC** & **Water-Cooled PAC**

4. **Ducted Split AC / Ceiling-Mounted Cassette**
   - For larger spaces, ducts distribute air from the indoor unit

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_s = m_s \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible Heat | kW or TR |
| 2 | $Q_L = m_s \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent Heat | kW or TR |
| 3 | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_L$ | Total Heat | kW or TR |
| 4 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | Cooling capacity | — |
| 5 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t}$ | Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{ERSH} = \text{ESHF} \times 1.10$ or $\text{ERSH} = \text{SHF} + 0.1$ (KTU standard) | Effective Sensible Heat | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Volume Flow} = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.23 \cdot \Delta T}$ (CFM) | Air quantity (CFM) | CFM |
| 8 | $\text{Volume Flow} = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.08 \cdot \Delta T}$ (CFM, in BTU/hr) | Alternative form | CFM |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.23 \cdot \Delta T} \text{ (m}^3/\text{s, SI)}$ | Air volume in SI | m³/s |
| 10 | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | Humidity ratio | kg/kg dry air |
| 11 | $h = 1.005 \cdot T + W \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$ | Enthalpy of moist air | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | — |
| 13 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{W_{comp}}$ | Heat Pump COP | — |
| 14 | $\text{EER} = \dfrac{\text{BTU/hr}}{W_{in}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | BTU/W·hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Tip:** Always remember the **1.23 constant** (for SI) and **1.08 constant** (for IP units) in the CFM formula. The constant **1.23 = ρ × cp × 60 / 1000** derived from the psychrometric properties of air.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Central systems** dominate **large commercial buildings** (hotels, hospitals, IT parks) due to economies of scale and centralized maintenance.
- **Unitary systems** dominate **residential and small commercial** sectors due to low first cost, plug-and-play installation, and zone independence.
- **Absorption systems** are preferred where **waste heat or steam is cheaply available** (e.g., co-generation plants, solar thermal input).
- **District Cooling** (a modern extension of central systems) is being adopted in cities like **Dubai, Singapore, and increasingly in Kerala's Smart City projects** for centralized chilled water distribution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Air Quantity (CFM) from Sensible Heat

The most important derivation in HVAC sizing — finding the **supply air flow rate** required to remove a given sensible heat load.

**Starting Point — Sensible Heat Equation:**

$$Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q_s$ = Sensible heat load (kW or kJ/s)
- $m$ = Mass flow rate of dry air (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between room and supply air (°C or K)

**Step 1: Convert mass flow to volumetric flow**

$$m = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of air ≈ **1.2 kg/m³** (at standard conditions)
- $\dot{V}$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/s)

**Step 2: Substitute in the sensible heat equation**

$$Q_s = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3: Solve for volumetric flow rate**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical constants**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{Q_s}{1.2 \times 1.005 \times \Delta T} = \frac{Q_s}{1.206 \cdot \Delta T} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Convert to CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute) for practical use**

1 m³/s = **2118.88 CFM**, and 1 kW = **3412.14 BTU/hr**

$$\text{CFM} = \frac{Q_s \text{(BTU/hr)}}{1.08 \times \Delta T \text{(°F)}}$$

In SI units, the commonly used CFM formula is:

$$\text{CFM} = \frac{Q_s \text{(kW)} \times 3412.14}{1.08 \times \Delta T \text{(°F)}}$$

Or directly using the engineering shortcut:

$$\text{CFM} = \frac{Q_s}{1.23 \times \Delta T} \quad \text{(where } Q_s \text{ is in kW, } \Delta T \text{ in °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **1.23** comes from: $\frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot 60 \text{ s/min}}{1000 \text{ (W→kW conversion)} \times 35.31 \text{ (m³→ft³)}}$
> This converts m³/s → CFM and kW → kJ/s scaling.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Central vs. Unitary Systems

| Parameter | Central AC System | Unitary AC System |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 50 TR to 5000+ TR | 0.75 TR to 30 TR |
| **Components** | Chiller + AHU + Ducts | Self-contained unit |
| **Installation Location** | Plant room (basement/rooftop) | Window, wall, or rooftop |
| **Ductwork** | Extensive | Minimal (only in ducted splits) |
| **Initial Cost (CAPEX)** | Very High | Low to Moderate |
| **Operating Cost (OPEX)** | Low (per TR) | Higher (per TR) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (4.5–6.0 COP chillers) | Moderate (2.8–3.5 EER) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (system-wide) | High (per-room control) |
| **Maintenance** | Centralized, requires skilled staff | Localized, simpler |
| **Space Required** | Dedicated plant room | Minimal |
| **Noise Level** | Low in conditioned space | Moderate (indoor unit) |
| **Lifespan** | 20–25 years | 10–15 years |
| **Suitability** | Large commercial, industrial | Residential, small offices |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A conference room has the following loads:
- Sensible Heat Load, $Q_s$ = **10 kW**
- Latent Heat Load, $Q_L$ = **2 kW**
- Room temperature = **26°C (DB)**
- Room RH = **50%**
- Supply air $\Delta T$ = **10°C** (i.e., supply at 16°C)

**Find:** (a) Total Heat (b) SHF (c) Supply air CFM (d) Mass flow rate

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Heat:**
$$Q_t = Q_s + Q_L = 10 + 2 = 12 \text{ kW} = 3.41 \text{ TR}$$

**(b) Sensible Heat Factor:**
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.833$$

**(c) Supply Air CFM:**
$$\text{CFM} = \frac{Q_s}{1.23 \times \Delta T} = \frac{10}{1.23 \times 10} = \frac{10}{12.3} = 0.813 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to CFM:
$$\text{CFM} = 0.813 \times 2118.88 = 1722.6 \text{ CFM}$$

**(d) Mass Flow Rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 0.813 = 0.9756 \text{ kg/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation Pattern in KTU ESE (14-mark question):**
> - Definition/Classification: 3–4 marks
> - Diagrams/Schematics: 3–4 marks
> - Analytical comparison or numerical: 4–5 marks
> - Conclusion/Justification: 2–3 marks
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Central Air Conditioning System Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Chilled Water Plant Room] --> B[Chiller Unit with Compressor]
    B --> C[Condenser Water Pump]
    C --> D[Cooling Tower]
    D --> E[Hot Air Rejection to Atmosphere]
    
    F[Chilled Water Pump] --> G[Air Handling Unit AHU]
    B --> F
    G --> H[Filter Section]
    H --> I[Cooling Coil with Chilled Water]
    I --> J[Humidifier or Reheat Coil]
    J --> K[Supply Fan Blower]
    K --> L[Main Supply Duct]
    
    L --> M[Zone 1 VAV Terminal]
    L --> N[Zone 2 VAV Terminal]
    L --> O[Zone 3 VAV Terminal]
    
    M --> P[Zone 1 Return Grille]
    N --> Q[Zone 2 Return Grille]
    O --> R[Zone 3 Return Grille]
    
    P --> S[Return Air Duct]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Return Air Mixing Box]
    T --> U[Exhaust Damper]
    U --> V[Fresh Air Intake]
    T --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Unitary (Window/Split) AC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room Air] --> B[Return Air Filter]
    B --> C[Evaporator Coil]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Blower Fan]
    D --> E[Supply Air to Room]
    
    F[Refrigerant Copper Line Liquid] --> G[Expansion Device Capillary Tube]
    G --> C
    C --> H[Compressor]
    H --> I[Condenser Coil]
    I --> J[Condenser Fan]
    J --> K[Hot Air Rejection to Outside]
    I --> F
    
    L[Thermostat Sensor] --> M[Control Circuit]
    M --> H
    M --> G
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Comparison of Central vs. Unitary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CENTRAL[Central AC System]
        C1[Central Chiller] --> C2[AHU Plant Room]
        C2 --> C3[Distribution Ductwork Network]
        C3 --> C4[Multiple Zones Served]
    end
    
    subgraph UNITARY[Unitary AC System]
        U1[Individual Compressor] --> U2[Self Contained Unit]
        U2 --> U3[Single Room or Zone]
        U1 -.independent units.-> U2
    end
    
    CENTRAL --- UNITARY
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Refrigeration Cycle in a Unitary AC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Low Pressure Vapor] -->|Compression High Pressure Superheated Vapor| B[Condenser Outdoor Unit]
    B -->|Heat Rejection to Ambient Air| C[Condensate Liquid High Pressure]
    C -->|Expansion Pressure Drop| D[Expansion Valve Capillary Tube]
    D -->|Low Pressure Liquid Vapor Mix| E[Evaporator Indoor Unit]
    E -->|Absorbs Heat from Room| F[Cooled Air to Room]
    F -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:** When asked to "draw a neat sketch," ensure you label all **four major components**, **state changes**, and **refrigerant flow direction**. Examiners allocate 2 marks just for proper labeling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Air Conditioning." List any four essential functions it performs.*

**Model Answer:**
Air Conditioning is the process of treating air to control its temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and distribution to meet the requirements of the conditioned space.

Four essential functions:
1. **Temperature Control** — Cooling in summer, heating in winter.
2. **Humidity Control** — Dehumidification (summer) and humidification (winter).
3. **Air Cleaning** — Filtration of dust, pollen, and contaminants.
4. **Air Circulation** — Proper distribution to maintain uniform conditions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Mention any three differences between a unitary and a central air conditioning system.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Unitary AC | Central AC |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacity: 0.75–30 TR | Capacity: 50–5000+ TR |
| 2 | Self-contained package | Components distributed across plant room |
| 3 | Installed in conditioned space | Installed in remote plant room |
| 4 | No (or minimal) ductwork | Extensive ductwork network |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a)* Classify air conditioning systems in detail based on **(i) Application, (ii) Equipment arrangement, and (iii) Heat rejection medium**. (7 marks)

*(b)* With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **central air conditioning system** highlighting the role of the AHU, chiller, and VAV terminals. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of AC Systems:**

**(i) Based on Application:**
- **Comfort AC** — for human occupants (offices, homes, malls).
- **Industrial / Process AC** — for product quality (pharma, food, electronics).
- **Specialty AC** — for sensitive environments (OTs, cleanrooms, server rooms).

**(ii) Based on Equipment Arrangement:**
- **Unitary System** — all components in one cabinet (window, split, packaged).
- **Central System** — chiller, AHU, and ducts distributed across a plant room and conditioned space.

**(iii) Based on Heat Rejection Medium:**
- **Air-Cooled AC** — condenser rejects heat to ambient air (most unitary systems).
- **Water-Cooled AC** — condenser rejects heat to cooling tower water (central chillers).

**(b) Working of Central AC System:**

The central AC system consists of the following major components:
1. **Chiller** — produces chilled water (5°C – 7°C) by vapor compression or absorption cycle.
2. **Cooling Tower** — rejects condenser heat to atmosphere through evaporative cooling.
3. **Chilled Water Pump** — circulates chilled water from chiller to AHU.
4. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** — mixes return air with fresh air, filters it, cools/humidifies it, and supplies it via ducts.
5. **VAV Terminals** — modulate the supply air volume to each zone based on thermostat demand.
6. **Return Air Ductwork** — carries room air back to the AHU for reconditioning.

**Working Principle:**
Warm return air + fresh air → mixed in AHU → passed through filter → cooled by chilled water coil → conditioned supply air distributed via ducts → VAV boxes regulate airflow per zone → room air returns via grilles.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **various types of unitary air conditioning systems** with neat sketches. (7 marks)

*(b)* A room has a sensible heat load of **8 kW** and a latent heat load of **2 kW**. The room conditions are **25°C DB and 50% RH**. The supply air is **10°C** below the room temperature. Determine the **(i) Total heat load in TR, (ii) Sensible Heat Factor, (iii) Supply air CFM, and (iv) Mass flow rate of supply air.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Unitary AC Systems:**

1. **Window AC** — Capacity 0.75–2 TR. Single chassis with compressor, condenser, evaporator, and capillary tube. Mounted in a window opening. Suitable for small rooms.

2. **Split AC** — Two units: IDU (indoor) and ODU (outdoor). Refrigerant lines connect them. Capacity 1–2 TR. Quieter operation; ODU can be placed in service area.

3. **Packaged AC (PAC)** — Single factory-built unit, 3–30 TR. Variants: air-cooled (rooftop) and water-cooled (with cooling tower).

4. **Ducted Split / Cassette AC** — Indoor unit concealed in ceiling with ductwork. Ideal for multiple rooms from a single outdoor unit.

5. **VRF / VRV System** — Variable Refrigerant Flow, multiple indoor units on a single outdoor unit, inverter-driven compressor. Energy efficient.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_s = 8$ kW, $Q_L = 2$ kW
- Room: 25°C DB, 50% RH
- $\Delta T = 10°C$

**(i) Total Heat in TR:**
$$Q_t = Q_s + Q_L = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_t = \frac{10}{3.517} = 2.843 \text{ TR}$$

**(ii) Sensible Heat Factor:**
$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80$$

**(iii) Supply Air CFM:**
$$\text{CFM} = \frac{Q_s}{1.23 \times \Delta T} = \frac{8}{1.23 \times 10} = \frac{8}{12.3} = 0.650 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\text{CFM} = 0.650 \times 2118.88 = 1377.27 \text{ CFM}$$

**(iv) Mass Flow Rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 0.650 = 0.78 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Questions:**
> 1. **"Air Conditioning" ≠ "Cooling"** — Many students write only about cooling. **Lose 2 marks** if you forget humidity, filtration, and circulation.
> 2. **Confusing SHF and ERSH** — SHF uses only sensible and latent loads; ERSH accounts for bypass factor. KTU **frequently asks** the difference.
> 3. **CFM Formula Units Mismatch** — Using 1.23 with $Q_s$ in BTU/hr gives wrong answers. Always confirm units first.
> 4. **Missing Labels in Diagrams** — A neat sketch **without labels** gets only 50% of diagram marks. KTU examiner allocation: 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for labeling.
> 5. **Confusing "Unitary" with "Central"** — A **packaged AC** is unitary (self-contained) even though it's large. The defining criterion is whether all components are in **one package**, not the capacity.
> 6. **Forgetting Bypass Factor** — When asked about coil performance, never assume 100% contact factor; real coils have BF of 0.05–0.2.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **THAA** = Temperature, Humidity, Air cleanliness, Air motion — the 4 functions of AC.
- ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — memorize this conversion.
- ✅ **SHF = $Q_s / Q_t$**; **ERSH = SHF + 0.1** (KTU standard assumption).
- ✅ **CFM formula (SI):** $\text{CFM} = \dfrac{Q_s \text{ (kW)}}{1.23 \times \Delta T \text{ (°C)}}$
- ✅ **CFM formula (IP):** $\text{CFM} = \dfrac{Q_s \text{ (BTU/hr)}}{1.08 \times \Delta T \text{ (°F)}}$
- ✅ **Five classifications of AC** — Application, Season, Capacity (Unitary/Central), Heat Rejection, Refrigeration Cycle.
- ✅ **Central AC** = Chiller + AHU + Ductwork + VAV — for large buildings (> 50 TR).
- ✅ **Unitary AC** = Self-contained (Window, Split, Packaged) — for small spaces (< 30 TR).
- ✅ **Double Duct system** = Cold + Hot air ducts with mixing box — best for zone-wise control but energy-intensive.
- ✅ **Single Duct with VAV** = Modern energy-efficient alternative to Double Duct.
- ✅ **Absorption systems** use heat (not electricity) as primary energy — preferred where waste heat is available.
- ✅ **EER** is used for cooling; **COP** is used for refrigeration systems; **kW/TR** is used for chillers.
- ✅ **R-410A, R-32** = modern refrigerants replacing R-22 (ODP = 0).
- ✅ **ASHRAE 90.1** sets energy efficiency standards for HVAC in commercial buildings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Compression Refrigeration System: Components (Compressors, Evaporators, Condensers, Expansion Devices, Fan Blowers)
Air Conditioning Protection and Engine Protection
Basic Overview of Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Overview of Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle that produces a cooling effect by utilizing a secondary working fluid (the **absorbent/refrigerant pair**) instead of mechanical shaft work. The system uses a thermal energy input at the **generator** to separate the refrigerant from the absorbent, after which the high-pressure refrigerant vapour is condensed, expanded, and evaporated to absorb heat at low temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** which consumes *mechanical/electrical work* at the compressor, the VARS consumes primarily *thermal energy* (heat) at the generator and a small amount of *pump work*. This makes it ideal when waste heat or solar energy is abundantly available.

The most common refrigerant-absorbent (working pair) combinations are:

| Working Pair | Refrigerant | Absorbent | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aqua-Ammonia | NH₃ (Ammonia) | H₂O (Water) | Industrial refrigeration below 0 °C |
| Lithium Bromide | H₂O (Water) | LiBr (Lithium Bromide) | Air-conditioning applications (above 0 °C) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Sponge & Bucket" Refrigerator**
>
> Imagine a kitchen sponge soaked with water sitting in a sealed bucket.
> - The **sponge (absorbent)** has a strong natural attraction for the **water (refrigerant)**.
> - If you **heat the sponge** over a stove (the *generator*), the water boils off as steam, leaving the sponge dry.
> - The steam is cooled on a cold plate (*condenser*) and collected as liquid water.
> - This liquid is then sprayed into an empty, warm bucket (*evaporator*), where it **evaporates**, absorbing heat from the surroundings — making the bucket cold!
> - The dry sponge is then transported back (*pump + absorber*) to absorb this newly evaporated water, and the cycle continues.
>
> The "heat from the stove" is analogous to the generator heat in a VARS — **no electricity-driven compressor is needed!**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Boiling Point of Ammonia (NH₃):** $-33.34 \; °\text{C}$ at 1 atm (ideal for low-temp refrigeration)
- **Boiling Point of Water (H₂O):** $100 \; °\text{C}$ at 1 atm
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of NH₃:** $\approx 1377 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of H₂O:** $\approx 2260 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** Dimensionless ratio of useful cooling to energy input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The VARS operates on a continuous closed-loop cycle. The thermal energy flow can be broken down into the following sequential logic steps:

1. **Low-pressure refrigerant vapour** enters the **Absorber** where it is absorbed by the weak solution (lean in refrigerant), forming a **strong solution** (rich in refrigerant).
2. The **solution pump** raises the pressure of this strong solution from absorber pressure to generator pressure. The pump work $W_p$ is very small compared to the heat input.
3. The high-pressure strong solution enters the **Generator (Boiler)**, where external heat $Q_G$ is supplied. This heat causes the refrigerant to vaporize and separate from the absorbent.
4. The refrigerant vapour may pass through a **Rectifier** to remove any absorbent traces before entering the **Condenser**.
5. In the **Condenser**, the high-pressure vapour rejects heat $Q_C$ to the surroundings and condenses to a high-pressure liquid.
6. The high-pressure liquid refrigerant passes through the **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)**, where its pressure and temperature drop drastically.
7. The low-pressure, low-temperature liquid refrigerant enters the **Evaporator**, where it absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the cooled space and evaporates, completing the refrigerant loop.
8. The weak solution (lean in refrigerant) from the generator returns to the absorber through a **Pressure Reducing Valve** to repeat the cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is VARS preferred over VCRS in some cases?**
> - It can run on **waste heat, solar energy, geothermal energy, or natural gas**.
> - **No moving parts** in the main refrigerant circuit (only a small liquid pump).
> - **Quiet operation** — ideal for libraries, hospitals, and residential buildings.
> - **COP is lower** than VCRS, but free/low-grade heat offsets this drawback.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Definition | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | Actual COP of VARS (cooling effect ÷ total energy input) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,ideal}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G}$ | Ideal COP ignoring negligible pump work | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$ | Maximum theoretical (Carnot-based) COP of VARS | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ | Carnot COP between evaporator and condenser temperatures | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_E = m_r \cdot (h_{fg})_{\text{ref}}$ | Cooling effect produced in evaporator | kW |
| 6 | $W_p = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_p}$ | Pump work (volume flow × pressure rise) | kW |
| 7 | $h_{f4} = h_{f3}$ | Throttling process (isenthalpic) in expansion valve | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $h_{f1} = h_{f4}$ | Throttling process in solution pressure-reducing valve | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\text{COP}_{\text{real}} = 0.4 \;\text{to}\; 0.6 \times \text{COP}_{\text{max}}$ | Typical actual COP range for practical VARS | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{real}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{max}}}$ | Thermal efficiency / Second-law efficiency of VARS | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Temperature Symbols (used universally in KTU problems):**
> - $T_E$ = Evaporator (cold space) temperature in **K**
> - $T_C$ = Condenser temperature in **K**
> - $T_G$ = Generator temperature in **K**
> - $T_A$ = Absorber temperature in **K**
>
> **Always convert °C to Kelvin** by adding $273.15 \; \text{K}$ before substitution.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of VARS in Engineering

- **Solar Air Conditioning:** Roof-mounted LiBr–H₂O chillers driven by solar thermal collectors (zero grid electricity for cooling).
- **Waste Heat Recovery:** Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants where exhaust gases drive ammonia absorption chillers.
- **Industrial Refrigeration:** Large cold-storage warehouses using ammonia-water systems.
- **Domestic Refrigerators:** Gas-operated absorption refrigerators (popular in RVs and remote areas).
- **District Cooling:** Centralized plants serving multiple buildings using steam-driven absorption chillers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum COP of VARS (Theoretical Limit)

We model the VARS as the **combined operation of a Refrigerator (Evaporator–Condenser) and a Heat Engine (Generator–Absorber)**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Equivalent Heat Engine

Consider the **Generator, Absorber, Pump, and Pressure-Reducing Valve** as forming a reversed heat engine that takes in heat $Q_G$ at temperature $T_G$, rejects heat $Q_A$ at temperature $T_A$, and delivers work equal to the pump work $W_p$.

For a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between $T_G$ and $T_A$:

$$
\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Q_A = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Energy Balance to the Heat Engine

The work output of this equivalent heat engine (which equals pump work $W_p$) is:

$$
W_p = Q_G - Q_A = Q_G - Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
W_p = Q_G \left( 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G} \right) = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Equivalent Refrigerator

The **Evaporator, Condenser, and the work input $W_p$** together constitute a reversed Carnot cycle (a refrigerator).

For a Carnot refrigerator operating between $T_E$ (cold) and $T_C$ (hot):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_p} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Two Cycles

Substituting the expression for $W_p$ from Step 2:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{W_p \cdot \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}}{Q_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{W_p}{Q_G}
$$

Substituting $W_p / Q_G = (T_G - T_A) / T_G$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,\,max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first term $\dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ is the Carnot refrigerator COP. The second term $\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$ is the **fraction of generator heat converted into useful work**. Since $W_p$ is much smaller than $Q_G$, the overall COP of a VARS is **always less than a comparable VCRS**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Vapour Compression (VCRS) | Vapour Absorption (VARS) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Thermal energy (generator) |
| Moving parts in main loop | Yes (compressor) | No (only solution pump) |
| Working fluid | Single refrigerant | Refrigerant + Absorbent pair |
| COP range | 2.5 to 6.0 | 0.4 to 1.2 |
| Noise level | High (mechanical) | Very low (quiet) |
| Maintenance | Moderate (moving parts) | Low |
| Use of waste heat | No | Yes |
| Size & weight | Compact for same capacity | Bulky |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Ideal fuel | Electricity | Natural gas / solar / waste heat |

## 3.3 Maximum COP for a Practical Aqua-Ammonia System

**Problem Statement:** A vapour absorption refrigeration system operates with an evaporator at $-10 \; °\text{C}$, condenser at $40 \; °\text{C}$, generator at $120 \; °\text{C}$, and absorber at $30 \; °\text{C}$. Calculate the maximum possible COP.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.

$$
T_E = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_C = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_G = 120 + 273.15 = 393.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_A = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the maximum COP formula.

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} \cdot \frac{393.15 - 303.15}{393.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} \cdot \frac{90.00}{393.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 5.263 \times 0.2289
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 1.205\;}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

- The theoretical maximum COP is **1.205**, meaning every kW of heat supplied at the generator can produce **1.205 kW of cooling**.
- In practical systems, the actual COP is typically **40% to 60%** of this maximum, so the expected real COP is between **0.48 and 0.72**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System

The diagram below shows the **complete refrigerant and solution flow** through the major components. The two loops are clearly distinguished: the **refrigerant loop** (red arrows) and the **solution loop** (blue arrows).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START Refrigerant Vapour Low Pressure Low Temperature] --> nodeB[ABSORBER Absorbs Vapour into Weak Solution Forms Strong Solution]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SOLUTION PUMP Raises Pressure from Absorber to Generator Pressure Small Pump Work Wp]
    nodeC --> nodeD[GENERATOR External Heat QG Supplied Refrigerant Boils Off from Strong Solution]
    nodeD --> nodeE[RECTIFIER Removes Absorbent Traps from Vapour]
    nodeE --> nodeF[CONDENSER High Pressure Vapour Rejects Heat QC Condenses to Liquid]
    nodeF --> nodeG[EXPANSION VALVE Throttling Process Pressure and Temperature Drop]
    nodeG --> nodeH[EVAPORATOR Low Pressure Liquid Absorbs Heat QE from Cold Space Evaporates]
    nodeH --> nodeA
    
    nodeD -. Weak Solution Returns .-> nodeI[PRESSURE REDUCING VALVE Throttling of Solution]
    nodeI -.-> nodeB
    
    nodeJ[INPUTS QG Heat at Generator Wp Pump Work] -. Feeds .-> nodeD
    nodeK[OUTPUTS QE Useful Cooling at Evaporator QC Heat Rejected at Condenser QA Heat Rejected at Absorber] -. Extracted .-> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For deeper understanding, the **functional architecture** below illustrates how each component interacts energetically with the surroundings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputHeat[Thermal Energy Input QG from Waste Heat or Solar] --> genBlock[GENERATOR Separates Refrigerant from Absorbent]
    genBlock -->|Refrigerant Vapour| rectBlock[RECTIFIER Purification Stage]
    rectBlock --> condBlock[CONDENSER Rejects Heat to Atmosphere]
    condBlock -->|Liquid Refrigerant| expBlock[EXPANSION VALVE Pressure Drop]
    expBlock --> evapBlock[EVAPORATOR Absorbs Heat from Cold Space]
    evapBlock -->|Low Pressure Vapour| absBlock[ABSORBER Recombines with Weak Solution]
    absBlock -->|Strong Solution| pumpBlock[SOLUTION PUMP Pressure Boost]
    pumpBlock --> genBlock
    
    genBlock -->|Weak Solution| prvBlock[PRESSURE REDUCING VALVE]
    prvBlock --> absBlock
    
    evapBlock -. Produces .-> coolingOutput[Useful Cooling Effect QE]
    condBlock -. Rejects .-> condHeat[Waste Heat QC]
    absBlock -. Rejects .-> absHeat[Absorber Heat QA]
```

## 4.3 P-h (Pressure-Enthalpy) Schematic Description

Since a P-h diagram for the **refrigerant loop** of VARS is identical to that of VCRS, the four key points are:

| Point | Location | Pressure | Phase | Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaporator exit | Low | Saturated vapour | Absorption |
| 3 | Condenser exit | High | Saturated liquid | Condensation |
| 4 | Evaporator inlet | Low | Liquid-vapour mix | Throttling |
| 7 | After expansion | Low | Liquid-vapour mix | Evaporation begins |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** — *Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" (COP) for a refrigeration system. Why is the COP of an ideal VARS always lower than that of a VCRS operating between the same temperature limits?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** COP of a refrigeration system is the ratio of the desired cooling effect $Q_E$ to the net energy input. For VARS, $\text{COP} = Q_E / (Q_G + W_p)$, where $Q_G$ is the generator heat and $W_p$ is the pump work.
- **Reason for lower COP (2 Marks):** In VARS, the only work input is the small pump work $W_p$, which is much less than the heat input $Q_G$. However, since $Q_G$ is the denominator and it is **larger than $W_p$ alone** (which would be used in VCRS), the COP of VARS is mathematically lower. Essentially, only a fraction $(T_G - T_A)/T_G$ of the generator heat is converted into useful work, reducing the effective cooling per unit energy consumed.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** — *List any four major components of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Generator (1 Mark):** Supplies heat $Q_G$ to vaporize the refrigerant from the strong solution.
2. **Condenser (0.75 Marks):** Rejects heat $Q_C$ and condenses the high-pressure refrigerant vapour to liquid.
3. **Evaporator (0.75 Marks):** Produces cooling effect $Q_E$ by evaporating the low-pressure refrigerant.
4. **Absorber (0.5 Marks):** Recombines the refrigerant vapour with the weak solution to form a strong solution, completing the solution loop.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** before substituting into the COP formula. This alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula** (mixing up the VCRS Carnot COP with the VARS max COP formula).
> 3. **Not clearly stating assumptions** (e.g., reversible/ideal cycle, negligible pump work, throttling is isenthalpic).
> 4. **Skipping units** in intermediate steps — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Omitting a labelled diagram** — for VARS questions, always include the block diagram showing the two loops; this fetches 2 marks easily.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System** using ammonia-water as the working pair. Clearly identify the refrigerant loop and solution loop, and state the function of each component.

(b) **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **maximum COP of an ideal VARS** in terms of evaporator, condenser, generator, and absorber temperatures.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)*

The system consists of two interconnected loops:

**Refrigerant Loop (2 Marks):**
- Low-pressure ammonia vapour leaves the **Evaporator** (1) and enters the **Absorber** (2), where it dissolves in weak aqua-ammonia solution to form a strong solution.
- After being pumped to high pressure, the strong solution enters the **Generator** (3), where heat $Q_G$ vaporizes the ammonia.
- Ammonia vapour passes through the **Rectifier** (4) to remove water traces, then enters the **Condenser** (5), where it rejects heat $Q_C$ and condenses.
- The liquid ammonia is throttled through the **Expansion Valve** (6) and enters the **Evaporator** to absorb heat $Q_E$ from the cooled space.

**Solution Loop (1 Mark):** The weak solution from the generator returns to the absorber via a **pressure-reducing valve**.

**Functions (2 Marks):**
- **Generator:** Separates refrigerant using heat input.
- **Absorber:** Recombines refrigerant with absorbent, releasing absorption heat $Q_A$.
- **Rectifier:** Purifies refrigerant vapour.
- **Solution Pump:** Boosts solution pressure to generator level.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Equivalent Heat Engine (2 Marks):**
Consider the Generator-Absorber-Pump assembly as a heat engine operating between $T_G$ (source) and $T_A$ (sink).

$$
\frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} \implies Q_A = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

**Step 2 — Pump Work (2 Marks):**

$$
W_p = Q_G - Q_A = Q_G - Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_G} = Q_G \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Refrigerator (1 Mark):**
The Evaporator-Condenser with work $W_p$ forms a Carnot refrigerator:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_p} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

**Step 4 — Combining (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{W_p}{Q_G} \cdot \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

Substituting $W_p / Q_G = (T_G - T_A) / T_G$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \left(\dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\right)\;}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Compare the **Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Systems** on the basis of energy input, working fluid, COP, moving parts, and applications.

(b) **[7 Marks]** An aqua-ammonia VARS operates with evaporator at $-15 \; °\text{C}$, condenser at $35 \; °\text{C}$, generator at $110 \; °\text{C}$, and absorber at $25 \; °\text{C}$. Calculate (i) the maximum theoretical COP, and (ii) the expected actual COP range assuming practical efficiency between 40% and 60%.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Basis | VCRS | VARS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Thermal energy (generator) + small pump work |
| Working fluid | Single refrigerant | Refrigerant-absorbent pair (NH₃–H₂O or LiBr–H₂O) |
| COP | 2.5 to 6.0 | 0.4 to 1.2 |
| Moving parts | Yes (compressor) | No (only solution pump) |
| Applications | Domestic refrigeration, AC, cold storage | Waste-heat recovery, solar cooling, large AC plants |

**Key Observations (2 Marks):**
- VCRS is more efficient but requires high-grade electrical energy.
- VARS can use low-grade/waste thermal energy, making it economically attractive where heat is cheap and abundant.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data (1 Mark):**

$$
T_E = -15 + 273.15 = 258.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_C = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_G = 110 + 273.15 = 383.15 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_A = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \; \text{K}
$$

**Part (i) — Maximum Theoretical COP (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{258.15}{308.15 - 258.15} \cdot \frac{383.15 - 298.15}{383.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{258.15}{50.00} \cdot \frac{85.00}{383.15}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 5.163 \times 0.2219
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = 1.146\;}
$$

**Part (ii) — Actual COP Range (3 Marks):**

Lower bound (40% efficiency):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual,\,min}} = 0.40 \times 1.146 = 0.458
$$

Upper bound (60% efficiency):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual,\,max}} = 0.60 \times 1.146 = 0.688
$$

$$
\boxed{\;0.458 \leq \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \leq 0.688\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System**

- ✅ **VARS uses heat, not mechanical work**, as its primary energy input at the **generator**.
- ✅ The cycle has **two loops**: the **refrigerant loop** and the **solution loop**.
- ✅ The **maximum COP formula** is: $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$
- ✅ Always convert **°C → K** by adding $273.15$ before any calculation.
- ✅ The four temperatures are arranged as: $T_E < T_A < T_C < T_G$.
- ✅ **Aqua-Ammonia (NH₃–H₂O)** is used for refrigeration **below 0 °C**; **Lithium Bromide (LiBr–H₂O)** is used for **A/C above 0 °C**.
- ✅ The **solution pump work $W_p$** is very small compared to $Q_G$ — often neglected in ideal-cycle analysis.
- ✅ Throttling in both the **expansion valve** and **pressure-reducing valve** is **isenthalpic** ($h_{\text{in}} = h_{\text{out}}$).
- ✅ COP of VARS is **always lower** than VCRS for the same temperature limits, but VARS is preferred when **waste/solar heat** is available.
- ✅ Typical practical efficiency: actual COP = **40% to 60%** of theoretical COP_max.
- ✅ **No moving parts** in the main refrigerant circuit → quiet, low-maintenance operation.
- ✅ Common KTU exam trap: students forget to multiply the Carnot COP term by the heat-engine efficiency term $\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outside and Inside Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outside and Inside Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Outside and Inside Design Conditions?

In any HVAC project, the engineer must first decide **two sets of reference weather states** before a single duct is drawn or a chiller is sized. These are the **Outside Design Conditions (ODC)** and the **Inside Design Conditions (IDC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outside Design Conditions (ODC):** The most severe combination of outdoor temperature, humidity, and air conditions that the HVAC system is expected to handle while still maintaining comfort inside the conditioned space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inside Design Conditions (IDC):** The target indoor environment — a specific combination of dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, air movement, and air cleanliness — that the system must maintain for the occupants' thermal comfort, health, and process requirements.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of designing an **HVAC system** like planning a **kitchen recipe for a large wedding banquet**:

- **Outside Design Conditions** are like asking: *"What is the worst possible weather on the wedding day?"* — you must be ready for the most extreme heat, the heaviest humidity, or the coldest morning, because you cannot control nature.
- **Inside Design Conditions** are like deciding: *"What temperature and ambience do the guests need to feel comfortable inside the hall?"* — a slightly cool room (around **24 °C**) with gentle air movement is what makes them enjoy the event.

If the chef prepares the kitchen only for "average weather," the food will spoil on a hot day and the guests will be uncomfortable. Likewise, an HVAC system designed only for average weather will fail in extreme summers. Hence, ODC and IDC form the **bookends of all HVAC load calculations**.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Bodies
