## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Sol-Air Temperature" and explain its role in the cooling load calculation of an automobile body panel.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Sol-Air Temperature** is a fictitious outdoor surface temperature that combines the actual outdoor air temperature with the effect of incident solar radiation absorbed by the surface. It is defined mathematically as:

$$T_{sol-air} = T_{outdoor} + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o} - \frac{\epsilon \cdot \Delta R}{h_o}$$

For an automobile, it serves two critical purposes:
1. **Simplifies the conduction equation** by replacing solar flux + convection into a single equivalent temperature, allowing use of the standard $Q = U A \Delta T$ formula.
2. **Represents realistic peak conditions** — for a silver-painted car in tropical sun, $T_{sol-air}$ can reach **70 °C to 90 °C** even when ambient is only 38 °C, dramatically increasing the cooling load.

The term is essential for opaque panels (roof, doors, hood, trunk); it does not apply to glass, where solar radiation passes through rather than being absorbed at the surface. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six heat gain components that contribute to the cooling load of an automobile passenger cabin.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six major heat gain components are:
1. **Heat conduction through opaque body panels** (roof, doors, floor)
2. **Heat conduction and solar gain through glazing** (windshield, windows)
3. **Heat from occupants** (sensible + latent metabolic heat)
4. **Heat from ventilation air** (sensible + latent)
5. **Heat from infiltration** (leakage through seals)
6. **Heat from internal equipment** (engine bay radiation, electronics)

A complete cooling load calculation sums all six components to determine the required refrigeration capacity. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

### 5.2 Part B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** (full internal choice as per KTU 2024 scheme).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for calculating the **cooling load of an automobile**, listing all heat gain components with their respective formulas. State clearly the design assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a compact car with cabin volume 6.5 m³, glazing area 3.2 m² (SHGC = 0.65), opaque body area 11 m² (U = 2.8 W/(m²·K), $\alpha_s$ = 0.6), outside design conditions 40 °C / 60% RH with solar radiation 900 W/m², inside conditions 25 °C / 50% RH, 3 occupants, and ventilation of 25 L/s, compute the **total cooling load**. Use $h_o$ = 12 W/(m²·K) and $h_{fg}$ = 2460 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Formulas (7 Marks):**

The cooling load of an automobile is computed by summing six independent heat gain components. The standard procedure is:

**Step 1 — Identify the envelope areas:** Catalog total opaque area $A_w$, total glazing area $A_g$, broken into subcategories (windshield, sides, rear, sunroof), each with its own SHGC.

**Step 2 — Determine design conditions:** Specify $T_o$, RH_o, $T_i$, RH_i, and solar intensity $I_t$ on each surface orientation.

**Step 3 — Compute sol-air temperature** for opaque body panels:
$$T_{sol-air} = T_o + \frac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat gain components:**
- Opaque conduction: $Q_w = U_w A_w (T_{sol-air} - T_i)$
- Glass conduction: $Q_{gc} = U_g A_g (T_o - T_i)$
- Glass solar gain: $Q_{gs} = A_g \cdot SHGC \cdot I_t$ (summed per glazing type)
- Occupants: $Q_{occ} = n \cdot 100 \; W$
- Ventilation: $Q_v = \dot{m}_v [c_p (T_o - T_i) + (W_o - W_i) h_{fg}]$
- Infiltration: $Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot (ACH \cdot V/3600) \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- Equipment: $Q_{eq}$ (given, e.g., firewall radiation)

**Step 5 — Total cooling load:**
$$Q_{total} = Q_w + Q_{gc} + Q_{gs} + Q_{occ} + Q_v + Q_{inf} + Q_{eq}$$

**Design assumptions** (typical): $h_o$ = 6 to 30 W/(m²·K), $\alpha_s$ depends on paint colour, $T_i$ = 25 °C, ACH = 2, occupant metabolic rate = 100 W per person. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sol-air temperature:**
$$T_{sol-air} = 40 + \frac{0.6 \times 900}{12} = 40 + 45 = 85 \; °C$$

**Step 2 — Opaque conduction:**
$$Q_w = 2.8 \times 11 \times (85 - 25) = 2.8 \times 11 \times 60 = 1848 \; W$$

**Step 3 — Glass conduction:**
$$Q_{gc} = 5.8 \times 3.2 \times (40 - 25) = 5.8 \times 3.2 \times 15 = 278.4 \; W$$

**Step 4 — Glass solar gain:**
$$Q_{gs} = 3.2 \times 0.65 \times 900 = 1872 \; W$$

**Step 5 — Occupants:**
$$Q_{occ} = 3 \times 100 = 300 \; W$$

**Step 6 — Ventilation:**
$$\dot{m}_v = 1.2 \times 0.025 = 0.030 \; kg/s$$

At 40 °C, 60% RH: $W_o \approx 0.0273 \; kg/kg$
At 25 °C, 50% RH: $W_i \approx 0.00988 \; kg/kg$

$$Q_{v,s} = 0.030 \times 1.005 \times (40 - 25) = 0.452 \; kW = 452 \; W$$

$$Q_{v,l} = 0.030 \times (0.0273 - 0.00988) \times 2460 = 0.030 \times 0.01742 \times 2460 = 1286 \; W$$

$$Q_v = 452 + 1286 = 1738 \; W$$

**Step 7 — Infiltration:**
$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{2 \times 6.5}{3600} = 0.00361 \; m^3/s$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.00361 \times 1.005 \times 15 = 65 \; W \; \text{(sensible)}$$

Adding latent (≈ 80 W): $Q_{inf} \approx 145 \; W$

**Step 8 — Total cooling load:**
$$Q_{total} = 1848 + 278.4 + 1872 + 300 + 1738 + 145$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 6.18 \; kW}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for calculating the **heating load of an automobile** for winter conditions. Compare and contrast it with the cooling load procedure, highlighting the differences in dominant load components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedan operates in winter with outside temperature −7 °C, inside setpoint 22 °C, cabin volume 9 m³, glass area 4 m² (U = 5.8 W/(m²·K)), opaque area 14 m² (U = 2.5 W/(m²·K)), 4 occupants, and ventilation 30 L/s. Compute the **total heating load** including a safety margin of 10%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Comparison (7 Marks):**

The heating load procedure is structurally similar to cooling load but with **opposite signs** and certain load components removed or reduced.

**Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Determine outside/inside temperatures:** $T_o$ is low (often negative in cold climates), $T_i$ = 20–22 °C.

**Step 2 — Compute envelope heat losses:**
- Opaque walls: $Q_w = U_w A_w (T_i - T_o)$
- Glass conduction: $Q_g = U_g A_g (T_i - T_o)$ — no solar term at night

**Step 3 — Compute ventilation loss:**
$$Q_v = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$
(No latent term — cold outside air is usually dry.)

**Step 4 — Compute infiltration loss:**
$$Q_{inf} = \rho \cdot (ACH \cdot V/3600) \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_o)$$

**Step 5 — Subtract internal gains:**
- Occupants: 100 W per person (subtracted)
- Solar gain (if daytime): subtracted
- Engine waste heat (if running): partially subtracted

**Step 6 — Total heating load:**
$$Q_{heat} = Q_w + Q_g + Q_v + Q_{inf} - Q_{int,gains}$$

**Differences from Cooling Load Procedure:**

| Aspect | Cooling | Heating |
|---|---|---|
| Solar gain | **Major contributor** (often 30–40%) | Subtracts if present |
| Latent load | **Significant** (dehumidification) | Usually absent |
| $T_{sol-air}$ | Required | Not used (night-time) |
| Occupant load | Added | Subtracted |
| Equipment load | Engine bay radiation added | Engine heat subtracted |
| Magnitude | **3–4× higher** | Lower |

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Opaque heat loss:**
$$Q_w = 2.5 \times 14 \times (22 - (-7)) = 2.5 \times 14 \times 29 = 1015 \; W$$

**Step 2 — Glass heat loss:**
$$Q_g = 5.8 \times 4 \times 29 = 672.8 \; W$$

**Step 3 — Ventilation heat loss:**
$$\dot{m}_v = 1.2 \times 0.030 = 0.036 \; kg/s$$

$$Q_v = 0.036 \times 1.005 \times 29 = 1.049 \; kW = 1049 \; W$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration heat loss (assume 2 ACH):**
$$\dot{V}_{inf} = \frac{2 \times 9}{3600} = 0.005 \; m^3/s$$

$$Q_{inf} = 1.2 \times 0.005 \times 1.005 \times 29 = 175 \; W$$

**Step 5 — Subtract occupant gains:**
$$Q_{int} = 4 \times 100 = 400 \; W$$

**Step 6 — Gross heating load:**
$$Q_{heat,gross} = 1015 + 672.8 + 1049 + 175 - 400 = 2511.8 \; W$$

**Step 7 — Apply 10% safety margin:**
$$Q_{heat,final} = 2511.8 \times 1.10$$

$$\boxed{Q_{heat,final} \approx 2763 \; W \approx 2.76 \; kW}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting humidity ratio conversion** in ventilation latent load — students often use $(RH_o - RH_i) \cdot h_{fg}$ instead of $(W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}$. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU paper valuations.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using W/m²·°C and K interchangeably is correct numerically, but writing U in BTU/hr·ft²·°F causes arithmetic errors.
> 3. **Ignoring sol-air temperature** for dark-coloured vehicles — examiners specifically check if you apply $\alpha_s \cdot I_t / h_o$ for body panels.
> 4. **Double-counting occupants** — once in sensible and again in latent is correct; but adding them with the wrong sign for heating mode loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping infiltration** in "approximate" problems — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if this term is missing.
> 6. **Not splitting glass SHGC** for windshield vs side windows — modern laminated windshields have lower SHGC than tempered side glass.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cooling & Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles**

- ✅ **Total Cooling Load** = $Q_{wall} + Q_{glass} + Q_{occ} + Q_{vent} + Q_{inf} + Q_{eq}$
- ✅ **Total Heating Load** = $Q_{losses} - Q_{internal\;gains}$
- ✅ **Sol-air temperature** is required **only for opaque panels**, not glass.
- ✅ **SHGC** quantifies the fraction of solar radiation that passes through glass and becomes heat inside the cabin.
- ✅ **Automotive glass is a major load** because it has high SHGC and large area — up to 60% of envelope.
- ✅ **Ventilation has two parts**: sensible ($c_p \Delta T$) and latent ($h_{fg} \Delta W$) — both must be included for cooling.
- ✅ **Heating load excludes latent term** — cold outside air is typically dry.
- ✅ **Infiltration** modeled via ACH: $\dot{V}_{inf} = ACH \cdot V_{cabin} / 3600$
- ✅ **Physical constants to memorize:** $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³, $h_{fg}$ = 2460 kJ/kg
- ✅ **Typical design conditions:** $T_i$ = 25 °C (cool), 22 °C (heat), 50% RH (cool)
- ✅ **Cooling load ≈ 3 to 4× heating load** for the same vehicle due to solar gain and latent dehumidification.
- ✅ **Occupant metabolic heat**: 60 W sensible + 40 W latent = 100 W per person (seated, 25 °C).
- ✅ **Firewall radiation** from engine bay adds **100 to 500 W** — often the largest "equipment" term.
- ✅ **The cooling load drives compressor sizing**; the heating load drives **heater core sizing** and **blower airflow**.
- ✅ **A safety margin of 5–10%** is recommended on the final design value for both heating and cooling.
- ✅ **U-values to remember**: opaque body ≈ 2.5 W/(m²·K), laminated glass ≈ 5.8 W/(m²·K), single-pane glass ≈ 5.7 W/(m²·K).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance
Refrigerants: Classification, Properties, and Selection Criteria
Commonly Used Refrigerants in Automobile Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Commonly Used Refrigerants in Automobile Applications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Automobile Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a working fluid used in the vapor-compression refrigeration cycle of an automotive air-conditioning (MAC) system to absorb heat from the vehicle cabin (evaporator) at low temperature/pressure and reject it to the ambient (condenser) at high temperature/pressure. In the context of KTU 2024 automobile HVAC studies, it is a specially formulated fluid with defined thermodynamic, environmental, toxicological, and material-compatibility characteristics that suit the compact, leak-prone, and safety-critical environment of a moving vehicle. For an automobile application, the refrigerant must additionally satisfy: 1. Operate efficiently across a wide range of compressor speeds (idle ~700 rpm to highway ~4000 rpm). 2. Be compatible with the elastomers, hose materials, and lubricants (PAG/POE oils) used in MAC systems. 3. Have a low **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and increasingly, a low **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**. 4. Exhibit chemical stability over years of service inside metal/aluminum heat exchangers. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Blood of the AC System — Plain English Intuition** > Think of an automobile AC system as the *circulatory system* of a living organism. The **compressor** is the *heart*, the **condenser** is the *lungs exhaling heat*, the **expansion device** is a *valve/narrowing*, and the **evaporator** is the *skin absorbing coolness*. The **refrigerant** is the **blood** that flows through all of these, carrying heat from the cabin and dumping it outside. Just as blood must be the right type (A, B, AB, O) for the body, a refrigerant must be the right "type" so it boils at the right temperature, doesn't poison the environment, and doesn't corrode the metal pipes. Using the wrong refrigerant is like putting diesel in a petrol engine — it technically may run, but the system is poisoned. ## 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics (Bold Constants) - **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** — Reference: R-11 = **1.0** - **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** — Reference: CO₂ = **1.0** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR4 basis) - **Atmospheric Lifetime (years)** — Time a molecule persists before breakdown - **ASHRAE Safety Classification** — Letter (A=lower toxicity, B=higher toxicity) + Number (1=no flame propagation, 2=lower flammability, 3=higher flammability) - **Normal Boiling Point (NBP)** — Saturation temperature at **101.325 kPa** ## 1.4 Historical Evolution of Automobile Refrigerants > [!NOTE] > The trajectory of MAC refrigerants mirrors global environmental policy: > > **1930s – 1990s:** **R-12 (Dichlorodifluoromethane, CCl₂F₂)** — A CFC, dominant for ~50 years. > **1990s – 2010s:** **R-134a (1,1,1,2-Tetrafluoroethane, CH₂FCF₃)** — An HFC, became the global standard after the Montreal Protocol. > **2010s – Present:** **R-1234yf (2,3,3,3-Tetrafluoropropene, CF₃CF=CH₂)** — An HFO, introduced under EU MAC Directive **2006/40/EC** for new vehicle types from **2011** and all new registrations from **2017**. > **Emerging:** **R-744 (CO₂)** — Transcritical, used in EVs and high-efficiency systems (e.g., BMW, Daimler). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Properties Required of an Automobile Refrigerant A refrigerant used in a moving vehicle must satisfy a multi-objective optimization across the following domains: ### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties - **Moderate Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg):** Higher h_fg means smaller refrigerant mass flow for the same cooling capacity → smaller compressor displacement. - **Low Specific Volume at Compressor Inlet:** Reduces compressor swept volume. - **Suitable Saturation Pressures:** Suction pressure must be > atmospheric (to prevent air ingress through leaks); discharge pressure must be manageable (typically < **2.5 MPa** for automotive systems). - **Low Compressor Discharge Temperature:** To prevent lubricant breakdown (PAG oil degrades above ~150 °C). ### 2.1.2 Environmental Properties - **ODP = 0** (mandatory since Montreal Protocol, 1987). - **Low GWP:** EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC mandates GWP < **150** for new type approvals from 2011 onwards. This is what triggered the R-1234yf transition. ### 2.1.3 Chemical & Material Properties - **Chemical Stability:** Inert to copper, aluminum, steel, and elastomers (HNBR, EPDM). - **Lubricant Miscibility:** Must dissolve in PAG (Polyalkylene Glycol) or POE (Polyolester) oil used with the compressor. - **Non-Corrosive:** Should not form acids in presence of moisture. ### 2.1.4 Safety Properties - **ASHRAE A1 preferred:** Low toxicity, no flame propagation. R-1234yf is **A2L** (mildly flammable), accepted because the charge size in vehicles is small (< 1.5 kg). ## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of Major Automobile Refrigerants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Refrigerant Properties** > (All values are typical/normal-condition; consult NIST REFPROP for precision.) | Property | R-12 | R-134a | R-1234yf | R-152a | R-744 (CO₂) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Chemical Family | CFC | HFC | HFO | HFC | Natural | | Molecular Formula | CCl₂F₂ | CH₂FCF₃ | CF₃CF=CH₂ | CH₃CHF₂ | CO₂ | | Molecular Mass (g/mol) | 120.91 | 102.03 | 114.04 | 66.05 | 44.01 | | NBP (°C) | −29.8 | −26.1 | −29.5 | −24.0 | −78.5 (sublimes) | | Critical Temp. (°C) | 112.0 | 101.1 | 94.7 | 113.3 | 31.0 | | Critical Pressure (MPa) | 4.11 | 4.06 | 3.38 | 4.50 | 7.38 | | ODP | 0.82 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,430 | 4 | 124 | 1 | | Atmospheric Life (yrs) | 100 | 14 | 10.5 | 1.4 | Variable | | ASHRAE Class | A1 | A1 | A2L | A2 | A1 | | Lubricant | Mineral | PAG/POE | POE/PAG | POE | POE (special) | | LFL (kg/m³) | None | None | 0.058 | 0.103 | None | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Refrigerant Performance Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following equations form the analytical core for any numerical problem on refrigerants. ### 2.3.1 Volumetric Cooling Capacity (q_v) The refrigeration effect per unit volume of refrigerant sucked by the compressor: $$q_v = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{v_1}$$ where **h₁** = enthalpy at compressor inlet (kJ/kg), **h₄** = enthalpy at evaporator outlet (= h₁ ideally for dry compression), **v₁** = specific volume at compressor inlet (m³/kg). Units: **kJ/m³** ### 2.3.2 Coefficient of Performance (COP) $$COP_{ref} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ where **Q_e** = evaporator heat absorption rate, **W_c** = compressor work. ### 2.3.3 Compressor Work (per unit mass) $$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$ ### 2.3.4 Pressure Ratio (rp) $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$ Automotive systems typically operate at **rp ≈ 8 to 12**. ### 2.3.5 Discharge Temperature Approximation (Isentropic) $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$$ where **k** = specific heat ratio (Cp/Cv). For R-134a ≈ 1.13, for R-1234yf ≈ 1.10, for R-12 ≈ 1.14. ### 2.3.6 GWP Calculation (Reference-Based) $$GWP_x = \frac{\text{Radiative forcing per kg of } x \text{ over 100 yrs}}{\text{Radiative forcing per kg of } CO_2 \text{ over 100 yrs}}$$ ### 2.3.7 Environmental Equivalence If a vehicle has **m** kg of refrigerant with GWP = G: $$CO_2\text{-equivalent leakage} = m \times G \;\; \text{(kg CO}_2\text{eq)}$$ This is the basis of the **EU F-Gas Regulation** and **Kigali Amendment** compliance. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Service Workshop Identification:** Knowing the GWP allows technicians to compute carbon-equivalent leakage for **F-Gas Regulation** reporting. - **Retrofit Decisions:** R-12 to R-134a retrofit required lubricant change (mineral → PAG) and condenser upgrades. - **EV/Hybrid Systems:** Lower GWP refrigerants and natural refrigerants (R-744) reduce total life-cycle carbon footprint — critical for **LEED** and **ISO 14001** certified automotive plants. - **Fire Safety in Tunnels:** A2L refrigerants (R-1234yf, R-152a) require engineering controls (PRD — Pressure Relief Devices venting refrigerant safely) to prevent accumulation in enclosed parking structures. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why R-12 Was Phased Out — GWP-Based Comparative Analysis > [!NOTE] > This is a standard KTU-style numerical problem. We'll work through it methodically. ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A 1990 vintage automobile originally uses **2.5 kg of R-12**. In a year, due to a leak, **150 g** of refrigerant escapes into the atmosphere. Compute: (a) The CO₂-equivalent emission from this single vehicle. (b) How much R-134a would have the same environmental impact? (c) How much R-1234yf would have the same environmental impact? Given: GWP of R-12 = 10,900; R-134a = 1,430; R-1234yf = 4. ### 3.1.2 Solution **Step 1 — Identify given data.** Let mass leaked, **m = 150 g = 0.150 kg**. **Step 2 — Apply the GWP equivalence formula.** $$CO_2\text{-eq} = m_{leaked} \times GWP$$ **Step 3 — Part (a): R-12 leakage.** $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R12} = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R12} = 1{,}635 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq}$$ **Step 4 — Part (b): R-134a equivalent mass.** For equivalent impact, we set: $$m_{134a} \times 1430 = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ Solving for **m₁₃₄ₐ**: $$m_{134a} = \frac{0.150 \times 10{,}900}{1430} = \frac{1{,}635}{1{,}430}$$ $$m_{134a} = 1.143 \;\; \text{kg}$$ **Step 5 — Part (c): R-1234yf equivalent mass.** For equivalent impact: $$m_{1234yf} \times 4 = 0.150 \times 10{,}900$$ $$m_{1234yf} = \frac{1{,}635}{4}$$ $$m_{1234yf} = 408.75 \;\; \text{kg}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** The same 150 g leak has the same climate impact as: - **1.143 kg of R-134a** leaking, or - **408.75 kg of R-1234yf** leaking. This shows the **enormous reduction in environmental burden** achieved by transitioning to R-1234yf — a single 150 g leak of R-12 is equivalent to leaking nearly half a ton of R-1234yf. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Discharge Temperature Comparison > [!NOTE] > This derivation uses the isentropic relation, frequently tested in KTU ESE. ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement A vehicle AC compressor compresses R-134a from **P₁ = 0.25 MPa** (suction) to **P₂ = 2.0 MPa** (discharge). The suction temperature is **T₁ = 5 °C = 278.15 K**. Compute the isentropic discharge temperature. Given: For R-134a, **k = 1.13** (approximate ideal-gas value at ~300 K). ### 3.2.2 Solution **Step 1 — Pressure ratio computation.** $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2.0}{0.25} = 8.0$$ **Step 2 — Apply the isentropic temperature relation.** $$T_2 = T_1 \left(r_p\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the exponent.** $$\frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{1.13 - 1}{1.13} = \frac{0.13}{1.13} = 0.1150$$ **Step 4 — Compute the pressure-ratio power term.** $$r_p^{0.1150} = 8.0^{0.1150}$$ Using logarithms: $$\ln(8.0) = 2.0794$$ $$0.1150 \times 2.0794 = 0.2391$$ $$e^{0.2391} = 1.270$$ **Step 5 — Compute T₂.** $$T_2 = 278.15 \times 1.270 = 353.25 \;\; \text{K}$$ $$T_2 = 353.25 - 273.15 = 80.1 \;\; °C$$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** The isentropic discharge temperature of **80.1 °C** is well within the lubricant stability limit. For comparison, computing the same for R-1234yf (k ≈ 1.10) yields a slightly higher exponent (0.0909) but similar magnitude discharge temperature. **R-12 (k ≈ 1.14)** yields marginally higher discharge temperature than R-134a for the same pressure ratio. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Refrigerant > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Decision Logic** | Application Scenario | Recommended Refrigerant | Justification | |---|---|---| | Vintage pre-1995 car, original system | R-12 (if available) | Compatibility, but illegal to vent | | 1995–2014 production car | R-134a | OEM design, well-supported | | New car (post-2017 EU / global) | R-1234yf | GWP < 150 compliance | | Electric Vehicle (high-heat-load) | R-744 or R-1234yf | High COP, low GWP, high critical temp not needed | | Tropical climate vehicle (India) | R-134a / R-1234yf | Both suitable; condensation pressure acceptable | | Hybrid retrofit (R-12 → R-134a) | R-134a + PAG oil | Requires flushing, new dryer, label change | --- ## 3.4 Verification: Volumetric Cooling Capacity Comparison ### 3.4.1 Quick Numerical Example A MAC system uses R-134a with evaporator at **0.244 MPa**, h₁ ≈ **402 kJ/kg**, h₄ ≈ **252 kJ/kg**, v₁ ≈ **0.084 m³/kg**. Compute q_v. **Step 1 — Net refrigeration effect.** $$q_e = h_1 - h_4 = 402 - 252 = 150 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric cooling capacity.** $$q_v = \frac{q_e}{v_1} = \frac{150}{0.084}$$ $$q_v = 1{,}785.7 \;\; \text{kJ/m}^3$$ This value is used to size the compressor displacement for a given cooling duty. A higher q_v means a smaller, lighter compressor — desirable for fuel efficiency. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Refrigerant Selection Logic for Automobile AC ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select MAC Refrigerant] --> B{Is vehicle pre-1995?} B -- Yes --> C[Check legacy R-12 system
and F-Gas compliance] C --> D[Option 1: Recycle R-12
Option 2: Retrofit to R-134a] B -- No --> E{Is vehicle post-2017
EU or GWP regulated market?} E -- No --> F[Use R-134a
OEM Standard] E -- Yes --> G{Is system designed
for high pressure transcritical?} G -- Yes --> H[Use R-744
CO2 Transcritical] G -- No --> I[Use R-1234yf
HFO Low GWP] D --> J[Service and Recycle] F --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[End: Refrigerant Charged
and System Operational] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Refrigerant Property Trade-off Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Refrigerant Property
Trade-off] --> B[Thermodynamic
Performance] A --> C[Environmental
Impact] A --> D[Material
Compatibility] A --> E[Safety
Classification] B --> B1[Latent Heat h_fg] B --> B2[Saturation Pressures] B --> B3[Discharge Temp] C --> C1[ODP must be 0] C --> C2[GWP lt 150 target] C --> C3[Atmospheric Life] D --> D1[Elastomer Seal] D --> D2[Lubricant PAG POE] D --> D3[Metal Corrosion] E --> E1[Toxicity A or B] E --> E2[Flammability 1 2L 2 3] E --> E3[Charge Size Limit] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Functional Flow of Refrigerant in Automobile AC System ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Compressor Suction
Low Pressure Vapor] --> S2[Compressor
Compression] S2 --> S3[Discharge
High Pressure Vapor] S3 --> S4[Condenser
Heat Rejection to Ambient] S4 --> S5[High Pressure Liquid] S5 --> S6[Receiver Dryer
Moisture Removal] S6 --> S7[Expansion Valve
Pressure Drop] S7 --> S8[Low Pressure Mixture] S8 --> S9[Evaporator
Heat Absorption from Cabin] S9 --> S1 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Refrigerant Generations ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Refrigerant Evolution
in MAC Systems] --> B[Generation 1
CFC Era 1930 to 1990] A --> C[Generation 2
HFC Era 1990 to 2015] A --> D[Generation 3
HFO Era 2015 to Present] A --> E[Generation 4
Natural Refrigerants Future] B --> B1[R-12 CCl2F2
ODP 0.82 GWP 10900] C --> C1[R-134a CH2FCF3
ODP 0 GWP 1430] D --> D1[R-1234yf CF3CFCH2
ODP 0 GWP 4 A2L] E --> E1[R-744 CO2
ODP 0 GWP 1 Transcritical] E --> E2[R-152a CH3CHF2
ODP 0 GWP 124 A2] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]` **Q: List any two desirable properties of a refrigerant used in automobile air-conditioning systems.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — to comply with the Montreal Protocol and protect the stratospheric ozone layer. 2. **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP)** — preferably less than 150, as mandated by the EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC, to limit climate impact in case of accidental leakage. *(Any two from: high latent heat, chemical stability, non-flammability or low flammability, lubricant miscibility with PAG oil, low toxicity, suitable discharge temperature, non-corrosive to metals.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set A]` **Q: Why is R-12 (CFC) no longer used as a refrigerant in modern automobile AC systems?** **Model Answer:** R-12 is a chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) with an **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) of 0.82** and a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 10,900**. Its chlorine atoms catalytically destroy stratospheric ozone, and its high GWP causes severe greenhouse forcing. Under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and subsequent amendments, CFCs were phased out globally. Modern vehicles use R-134a, R-1234yf, or R-744 instead. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B for this module offers an internal choice. Both options are provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a comparison table, explain the properties of commonly used refrigerants in automobile applications: R-12, R-134a, R-1234yf, and R-744. Discuss their ODP, GWP, ASHRAE classification, and lubricant compatibility. **(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle AC system uses R-134a. The compressor compresses the refrigerant from **0.20 MPa, 0 °C** to **1.5 MPa**. Assuming isentropic compression with **k = 1.13** for R-134a, calculate the discharge temperature and comment on the lubricant stability. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison Table:** *(See SECTION 2.2 — Table reproduced below for full marks.)* | Property | R-12 | R-134a | R-1234yf | R-744 | |---|---|---|---|---| | ODP | 0.82 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,430 | 4 | 1 | | ASHRAE Class | A1 | A1 | A2L | A1 | | Lubricant | Mineral oil | PAG / POE | POE / PAG | POE (special grade) | **Discussion:** - **R-12** dominated the industry from the 1930s but was banned under the Montreal Protocol due to its ODP. - **R-134a** became the universal replacement in the 1990s because of zero ODP and acceptable thermodynamic performance, but its GWP of 1,430 is high. - **R-1234yf** is a fourth-generation HFO with GWP = 4, designed specifically to meet the EU MAC Directive limit of GWP < 150. Slight flammability (A2L) is managed by limiting charge size and using PRD valves. - **R-744 (CO₂)** is a natural refrigerant with GWP = 1, used in transcritical cycles for EVs (e.g., BMW i3) due to its excellent heat transfer and high critical temperature suitability. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - P₁ = 0.20 MPa = 200 kPa - T₁ = 0 °C = 273.15 K - P₂ = 1.5 MPa = 1500 kPa - k = 1.13 **Step 1: Pressure ratio** $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5$$ **Step 2: Compute exponent** $$\frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{1.13 - 1}{1.13} = 0.1150$$ **Step 3: Pressure ratio power** $$r_p^{0.1150} = 7.5^{0.1150}$$ Using logarithms: $$\ln(7.5) = 2.0149$$ $$0.1150 \times 2.0149 = 0.2317$$ $$e^{0.2317} = 1.2608$$ **Step 4: Discharge temperature** $$T_2 = 273.15 \times 1.2608 = 344.37 \;\; K$$ $$T_2 = 344.37 - 273.15 = 71.22 \;\; °C$$ **Step 5: Comment on lubricant stability** The isentropic discharge temperature of approximately **71 °C** is well below the PAG oil degradation threshold of ~150 °C. Therefore, the lubricant remains stable and the compressor is safe under these conditions. In real systems, the actual discharge temperature is higher (typically 80–110 °C) due to non-ideal effects such as superheat, friction, and non-isentropic losses, but it still remains within safe operating limits for automotive duty cycles. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set C]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Montreal Protocol and the Kigali Amendment in the context of automobile refrigerants. Discuss how they have shaped the transition from R-12 to R-1234yf. **(b) [7 Marks]** A MAC system originally charged with **2.0 kg of R-12** is retrofitted to use **R-134a**. If **20 g of refrigerant leaks annually** from each system, compare the annual CO₂-equivalent emissions before and after retrofit. Given: GWP(R-12) = 10,900, GWP(R-134a) = 1,430. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment:** **Montreal Protocol (1987):** An international treaty signed under the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) to protect the stratospheric ozone layer. It mandated the phase-out of **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** like R-12 and **Halons**. For automobile AC, this directly caused the replacement of R-12 (ODP = 0.82) with R-134a (ODP = 0). **Subsequent Amendments:** - **London Amendment (1990):** Added CFCs and carbon tetrachloride to the control list. - **Copenhagen Amendment (1992):** Accelerated phase-out, added HCFCs. **Kigali Amendment (2016):** An amendment to the Montreal Protocol specifically targeting **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** like R-134a, because HFCs have zero ODP but high GWP. Under Kigali, developing countries including India follow a phasedown schedule: baseline consumption frozen in 2024, 10% reduction by 2029, 80% reduction by 2045. **Impact on Automobiles:** The combined effect of the Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment is the global transition: - **R-12 → R-134a** (1990s) — driven by ODP concerns. - **R-134a → R-1234yf** (2015 onwards) — driven by GWP concerns. **Part (b) — Numerical Comparison:** **Given:** - Mass leaked annually, m = 20 g = 0.020 kg - GWP(R-12) = 10,900 - GWP(R-134a) = 1,430 **Step 1: CO₂-equivalent with R-12 (before retrofit)** $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R12} = m \times GWP_{R12} = 0.020 \times 10{,}900 = 218 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq}$$ **Step 2: CO₂-equivalent with R-134a (after retrofit)** $$CO_2\text{-eq}_{R134a} = m \times GWP_{R134a} = 0.020 \times 1{,}430 = 28.6 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq}$$ **Step 3: Reduction in emissions** $$\Delta = 218 - 28.6 = 189.4 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq}$$ **Step 4: Percentage reduction** $$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{189.4}{218} \times 100 = 86.88\%$$ **Conclusion:** Retrofitting from R-12 to R-134a reduces annual CO₂-equivalent emissions from refrigerant leakage by approximately **87%** per vehicle. This is a significant environmental gain, although modern R-1234yf (GWP = 4) would achieve over **99.9%** reduction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **ODP vs GWP Confusion:** Many students wrongly state that R-134a has high ODP. Remember: R-134a has **zero ODP** but **high GWP**. ODP applies to CFCs/HCFCs only. > 2. **Unit Errors in GWP Problems:** Mass leaked must be in **kg**, not grams, when multiplied by GWP. Forgetting to convert **g → kg** is the most common arithmetic error. > 3. **Temperature in Kelvin:** All gas-law calculations (including the isentropic temperature formula) require **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Using °C will give wrong answers. > 4. **Refrigerant Name vs. Number:** R-12 and CFC-12 are the same. R-134a and HFC-134a are the same. R-1234yf and HFO-1234yf are the same. Examiners expect both to be accepted. > 5. **Lubricant Forgetting:** When comparing refrigerants, students often miss the **lubricant compatibility** row. PAG oil goes with R-134a/R-1234yf; mineral oil went with R-12. This is a 1-mark differentiator in 14-mark answers. > 6. **ASHRAE A2L Misreading:** R-1234yf is **A2L (mildly flammable)**, not A2 or A1. The "L" denotes low burning velocity. Writing "A2" alone loses a mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Automobile Refrigerants** - ☐ **Three generations:** CFC (R-12) → HFC (R-134a) → HFO (R-1234yf). Plus natural R-744 as the emerging 4th-gen choice. - ☐ **ODP must be 0** for any modern automobile refrigerant. Reference ODP is R-11 = 1.0. - ☐ **GWP < 150** is the EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC threshold; R-1234yf (GWP = 4) is the compliant choice. - ☐ **ASHRAE Safety Class:** R-12, R-134a, R-744 are **A1** (non-flammable, low toxicity). R-1234yf is **A2L** (mildly flammable). R-152a is **A2**. - ☐ **Lubricant compatibility:** R-12 uses mineral oil; R-134a and R-1234yf use **PAG** or **POE** oil. - ☐ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** banned CFCs; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** phases down HFCs. - ☐ **CO₂-equivalent emission formula:** **E = m × GWP**, with **m in kg** and result in **kg CO₂eq**. - ☐ **Isentropic discharge temperature:** **T₂ = T₁ (P₂/P₁)^((k−1)/k)**; always use **Kelvin** for T₁. - ☐ **Automotive pressure ratio** is typically **8 to 12**; discharge pressure usually **< 2.5 MPa**. - ☐ **R-744 (CO₂)** operates in **transcritical cycle** for vehicle applications — no condensation occurs; the gas cooler replaces the condenser. - ☐ **R-1234yf charge limit** in vehicles is typically **< 1.5 kg** to keep flammability risk low in passenger cabins. - ☐ **Flammability metric** for A2L refrigerants is the **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** in kg/m³. R-1234yf LFL ≈ 0.058 kg/m³. - ☐ **Pressure Relief Device (PRD)** is mandatory in R-1234yf systems to safely vent refrigerant in case of fire, preventing tank rupture. - ☐ **Compatibility warnings:** R-12 and R-134a systems are **not** directly cross-compatible; retrofit requires flushing, dryer replacement, oil change, and label updates. - ☐ **Critical temperature** of R-744 is **31.0 °C** — close to ambient — which is why it is used in **transcritical** cycles, not subcritical.

topics.title: Distribution Duct System: Sizing, Supply and Return Ducts, Types of Grills, Diffusers, Ventilation, Air Noise Level, and Layout for Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distribution Duct System: Sizing, Supply and Return Ducts, Types of Grills, Diffusers, Ventilation, Air Noise Level, and Layout for Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Distribution Duct System – Formal KTU Definition

The **Distribution Duct System** in an automobile HVAC unit is the network of conduits (rigid and flexible tubes, channels, and plenum chambers) used to convey conditioned air (cooled, heated, dehumidified, or fresh air) from the HVAC unit outlet to the various passenger compartment zones (driver, co-passenger, rear passengers, windshield, foot well) and to channel the recirculated or stale air back to the evaporator/return inlet. The duct system includes the **plenum, supply ducts, return ducts, registers, grilles, diffusers, and noise-attenuating elements** integrated into the vehicle's instrument panel, headliner, floor console, and pillars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In an automobile HVAC system, the duct is not a passive pipe — it is an *engineered aerodynamic device* that must (a) deliver the right mass flow rate ($\dot{m}$) at the right pressure drop ($\Delta P$), (b) distribute air uniformly with the correct throw and drop pattern, (c) maintain low noise (NC ≤ 55 for passenger cars), and (d) be packaged within tight vehicle constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine the human **circulatory system**. The heart is your HVAC unit, the arteries are the **supply ducts** (carrying fresh, conditioned, oxygen-rich air to every organ), and the veins are the **return ducts** (carrying deoxygenated air back to the heart/lungs to be re-conditioned). The **grilles and diffusers** act like capillaries — small openings that spray air gently and evenly into a body region (driver's face, feet, rear cabin). Just as a blocked artery causes pressure buildup and a heart attack, a poorly sized duct causes high back-pressure, noisy operation, and poor cooling. **Duct sizing = choosing the right "vessel diameter" for the required "blood flow" without straining the heart.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world constraint:** In cars, duct cross-sections are not circular like in buildings — they are typically **flat oval, rectangular, or annular** to fit inside the IP (instrument panel) beam, A-pillar, and B-pillar spaces.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

- **Air velocity in ducts:** $V = 5$ to **$8 \text{ m/s}$** (automotive supply ducts)
- **Diffuser face velocity:** $V_{face} = 2.5$ to **$4 \text{ m/s}$** (to avoid draft)
- **Return grille face velocity:** $V_{ret} \leq 3 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at $20°\text{C}$, $101.325 \text{ kPa}$)
- **Noise Criterion (NC):** NC 40 – NC 50 for automobile cabins
- **Air change rate:** $20$ to **$30 \text{ ACH}$** (passenger car ventilation, fresh air mode)
- **Duct pressure drop target:** $\Delta P \leq 25$ to **$50 \text{ Pa}$** total (to keep blower power low)

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Duct Sizing – Two Principal Methods

### 2.1.1 Equal Friction Method (Most Common in Automobiles)

In this method, the duct main is sized so that the **frictional pressure drop per unit length** is held constant throughout the system (typically $1 \text{ Pa/m}$ for low-velocity automotive ducts). Branch ducts are then sized by maintaining the same friction rate, which automatically reduces their size as airflow decreases.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Compute total supply airflow $Q_{total}$ from cooling load: $Q_{total} = \dot{m}/\rho$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
2. Decide on friction rate $R = \Delta P/L$ (Pa/m) — for autos, $R = 0.8$ to $1.2 \text{ Pa/m}$
3. Using the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** for circular equivalent duct, compute diameter $D_{eq}$ for the chosen $R$ and $Q$
4. Convert circular equivalent to rectangular/oval to fit packaging constraints
5. Iterate for branches: each branch receives a fraction of $Q_{total}$ but maintains the same $R$

### 2.1.2 Static Regain Method (Used in Larger Commercial Systems)

The static regain method sizes each duct section so that the **static pressure is constant** at every branch take-off. The kinetic energy converted back to static pressure at each branch equals the friction loss in the next section. This method is generally not used in automobiles due to compactness, but is mentioned for completeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why equal friction dominates automotive design:** The duct runs in a car are short (typically $0.5$ to $1.5 \text{ m}$ from evaporator to outlets), so a low, constant friction rate gives uniform airflow with predictable blower performance.

## 2.2 Supply Ducts vs Return Ducts

| Feature | Supply Duct | Return Duct |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Deliver conditioned air to outlets | Collect stale/recirculated air back to evaporator inlet |
| **Pressure** | Slightly higher than cabin (positive) | Slightly lower than cabin (negative) |
| **Typical location (car)** | Behind dashboard, A-pillar, B-pillar, console | Below IP, under front seats, plenum at evaporator inlet |
| **Size** | Smaller cross-section (higher velocity) | Larger cross-section (lower velocity for low noise) |
| **Insulation** | Insulated to prevent condensation | Generally not insulated |

## 2.3 Grilles and Diffusers – Definitions

- **Grille:** A louvered or perforated face plate that **terminates a duct opening** and allows air to enter/leave a space with some direction control. Used primarily at the **return air inlet** or as simple supply outlets (e.g., side window demister outlet).
- **Diffuser:** A specifically shaped outlet device designed to **decelerate the airstream and spread it uniformly** over a wide area with controlled throw, drop, and spread pattern. Used for face-level and foot-level outlets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorise:** A *diffuser* is a device that reduces the kinetic energy of a moving fluid and redistributes it over an area, while a *grille* is primarily a covering or termination with limited aerodynamic shaping.

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric flow rate = area × velocity | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 3 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P}$ | Hydraulic diameter (for non-circular ducts) | m |
| 4 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_h} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pa |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{L}$ | Friction rate (equal-friction method) | $\text{Pa/m}$ |
| 6 | $D_{eq} = \left( \dfrac{12.67 \cdot Q^{1.9}}{R^{0.55}} \right)^{1/5.2}$ | Circular equivalent diameter from equal-friction (ASHRAE) | m |
| 7 | $A_{rect} = a \cdot b$, $D_{eq}^{rect} = \dfrac{1.30 \cdot (ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ | Equivalent round for rectangular duct | m |
| 8 | $P_{sound} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{rms}^2}{P_{ref}^2}\right)$ | Sound power level, $P_{ref} = 20 \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB |
| 9 | $L_{w,A} = L_{w} + K_A$ | A-weighted sound power level | dB(A) |
| 10 | $NC = f(L_{w,A}, f)$ | Noise Criterion (curve-fitting from octave band SPLs) | NC index |
| 11 | $V_{throw} = K \cdot V_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_x}$ | Throw velocity decay along diffuser jet | m/s |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{noise} \propto V^{5 \text{ to } 6}$ | Empirical rule: duct noise ~ $V^5$ to $V^6$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use `|` inside the table above.** The vertical pipe is reserved as a column separator. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in math expressions only inside the `$ $` delimiters (which LaTeX will render, not Markdown). In the table above, all absolute-value style expressions have been removed to preserve the markdown parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle NVH engineers** use duct sizing to keep blower-induced noise below 40 dB(A) at the driver's ear at 2500 RPM blower speed.
- **Cabin air quality engineers** use grille/diffuser placement to ensure CO₂ stays below 1000 ppm under recirculation.
- **Thermal comfort engineers** use throw distance and drop pattern data to deliver air *tangentially* over occupants, avoiding direct cold drafts on the chest and face.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Circular Equivalent Duct Diameter from Equal-Friction Method

**Starting point — Darcy-Weisbach equation:**

$$
\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
$$

Substituting $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$:

$$
\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \right)^2
$$

Simplify:

$$
\Delta P_f = \frac{8 \rho f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}
$$

Define friction rate $R = \Delta P_f / L$:

$$
R = \frac{8 \rho f Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}
$$

Solve for $D$:

$$
D^5 = \frac{8 \rho f Q^2}{\pi^2 R}
$$

$$
D = \left( \frac{8 \rho f Q^2}{\pi^2 R} \right)^{1/5}
$$

**Now introduce ASHRAE's empirical correlation for friction factor** for turbulent flow in smooth ducts ($f \approx 0.026 \cdot D^{-0.25}$ for the practical airflow range). After substituting and simplifying, the well-known ASHRAE chart-fit equation emerges:

$$
D_{eq} = \left( \frac{12.67 \cdot Q^{1.9}}{R^{0.55}} \right)^{1/5.2}
$$

**Units check:** $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $R$ in $\text{Pa/m}$, $D_{eq}$ in metres. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Round Diameter for a Rectangular Duct

For a rectangular duct of sides $a$ and $b$ (in metres), the ASHRAE equivalent round diameter that produces **the same friction loss per unit length at the same airflow** is:

$$
D_{eq}^{rect} = \frac{1.30 \cdot (a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Define aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b$
2. Total perimeter $P_{rect} = 2(a + b)$
3. Cross-sectional area $A_{rect} = ab$
4. Hydraulic diameter of rectangle: $D_h^{rect} = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$
5. By curve-fitting the Darcy–Weisbach equation for rectangular vs circular ducts over a wide $\alpha$ range, ASHRAE derived the empirical constant $1.30$ and exponents $0.625$ and $0.25$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (typical KTU 3-mark style):** A car HVAC evaporator must deliver $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ of cooled air through a main supply duct. Design an equivalent round duct using the equal-friction method with $R = 1.0 \text{ Pa/m}$.

**Step 1: Apply the ASHRAE formula.**

$$
D_{eq} = \left( \frac{12.67 \cdot (0.05)^{1.9}}{(1.0)^{0.55}} \right)^{1/5.2}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $Q^{1.9}$.**

$$
(0.05)^{1.9} = e^{1.9 \cdot \ln(0.05)} = e^{1.9 \cdot (-2.9957)} = e^{-5.6919} = 0.003377
$$

**Step 3: Numerator.**

$$
12.67 \times 0.003377 = 0.04278
$$

**Step 4: Denominator base.**

$$
(1.0)^{0.55} = 1.0
$$

**Step 5: Ratio and 5.2-th root.**

$$
D_{eq} = (0.04278)^{1/5.2}
$$

Take $\ln$:

$$
\ln(0.04278) = -3.152
$$

Divide:

$$
\frac{-3.152}{5.2} = -0.6062
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
D_{eq} = e^{-0.6062} = 0.5457 \text{ m} \approx 0.546 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical interpretation:** A 0.546 m diameter duct is **not feasible** in a car. This is why automotive engineers **convert to flat oval or rectangular** equivalents. For example, using $a/b = 4$ (typical for a flat duct), the equivalent rectangular size for the same friction rate would be approximately $a = 0.30 \text{ m}$, $b = 0.075 \text{ m}$ — which fits within the IP beam.

**Step 6: Convert to rectangular using the ASHRAE equivalent round formula:**

Set $\alpha = 4$, so $a = 4b$. Then:

$$
D_{eq}^{rect} = \frac{1.30 \cdot (4b^2)^{0.625}}{(4b + b)^{0.25}} = \frac{1.30 \cdot 4^{0.625} \cdot b^{1.25}}{5^{0.25} \cdot b^{0.25}} = \frac{1.30 \cdot 2.378 \cdot b}{1.495} = 2.067 \cdot b
$$

For $D_{eq}^{rect} = 0.546 \text{ m}$:

$$
b = \frac{0.546}{2.067} = 0.2641 \text{ m}
$$

$$
a = 4b = 1.056 \text{ m}
$$

This is also too large — so in practice the **friction rate is increased** (e.g., $R = 2.5 \text{ Pa/m}$) or the velocity is allowed up to $V = 8 \text{ m/s}$ and the duct is **split into parallel branches**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Throw Distance of a Diffuser

The throw $T$ (distance from outlet to terminal velocity $V_T = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$) for a radial diffuser is:

$$
T = K \cdot \frac{V_0 \cdot A_0^{1/2}}{V_T}
$$

where $K \approx 1.7$ to $2.2$ for a typical automotive louvered diffuser, $V_0$ is the discharge velocity, $A_0$ is the discharge area. The **drop** $D$ (vertical fall over throw) is:

$$
D = 0.5 \cdot T \quad \text{(Coanda-attached flow on ceiling)} 
$$

$$
D = 0.7 \cdot T \quad \text{(free jet in cabin)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Throw rule of thumb for cars:** Throw should equal **1.5 × distance from outlet to occupant's upper body** to ensure good mixing without direct draft.

## 3.5 Noise Level Estimation (Statistical Approximation)

The A-weighted sound power level generated by airflow in a duct is empirically:

$$
L_{w,A} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dot{V}^n \cdot \Delta P^m \right) + C
$$

For ducts with $V < 10 \text{ m/s}$, exponents $n \approx 2.5$, $m \approx 1.0$, and $C \approx 30 \text{ dB}$. For a typical automotive supply duct, $\dot{V} = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\Delta P = 50 \text{ Pa}$:

$$
L_{w,A} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left( (0.05)^{2.5} \cdot 50 \right) + 30
$$

Compute:

$$
(0.05)^{2.5} = 0.000559
$$

$$
0.000559 \times 50 = 0.02796
$$

$$
\log_{10}(0.02796) = -1.553
$$

$$
L_{w,A} \approx -15.53 + 30 = 14.47 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is sound **power**, not sound **pressure**. The pressure level at the driver's ear is $L_p = L_w - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ dB for a point source, where $r$ is distance. For $r = 0.5 \text{ m}$:
>
> $L_p \approx 14.47 - 20\log_{10}(0.5) - 11 = 14.47 + 6.02 - 11 = 9.49 \text{ dB(A)}$
>
> This is below the typical cabin background, so acceptable. If the duct were grossly undersized, $\Delta P$ and $L_w$ would balloon by 20–30 dB, becoming objectionable.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive HVAC Duct Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph evapUnit[Evaporator and Blower Unit]
        evap1[Evaporator Core]
        blower1[Centrifugal Blower]
        evap1 --> blower1
    end

    subgraph modeBox[Mode Door and Plenum Box]
        mode1[Mode Actuator]
        plenum1[Main Plenum]
        blower1 --> mode1
        mode1 --> plenum1
    end

    subgraph supplyNet[Supply Duct Network]
        mainD[Main Supply Duct]
        apipe[A Pillar Duct]
        bpipe[B Pillar Duct]
        consol[Console Duct]
        foot[Floor Duct]
        plenum1 --> mainD
        mainD --> apipe
        mainD --> bpipe
        mainD --> consol
        mainD --> foot
    end

    subgraph outletSet[Outlet Devices]
        faceDiff[Face Level Diffusers]
        footOut[Foot Level Grilles]
        demist[Windshield Demister Outlets]
        rearOut[Rear Cabin Vents]
        apipe --> faceDiff
        apipe --> demist
        consol --> faceDiff
        consol --> rearOut
        foot --> footOut
    end

    subgraph returnNet[Return Duct and Inlet]
        retGrille[Cabin Return Grille]
        retPlen[Return Plenum to Evap]
        filter1[Cabin Air Filter]
        retGrille --> filter1
        filter1 --> retPlen
        retPlen --> evap1
    end

    subgraph ventPath[Fresh Air Ventilation Path]
        ambIn[Ambient Air Inlet Cowl]
        recircDoor[Recirculation Door]
        ambIn --> recircDoor
        recircDoor --> plenum1
    end

    subgraph noiseCtrl[Noise and Acoustic Control]
        liner1[Duct Liner]
        silencer1[Hood Silencer]
        blower1 -.->|radiated noise| liner1
        liner1 --> silencer1
    end
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic – Grille vs Diffuser Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[Need to terminate a duct outlet] --> q1{What is the primary function?}
    q1 -->|Return air intake| useGrille[Use Return Grille - perforated or louvered]
    q1 -->|Supply air distribution| q2{Is uniform spread and low draft required?}
    q2 -->|Yes, over a wide area| useDiff[Use Diffuser - radial, slot, or louvered]
    q2 -->|No, simple directional throw acceptable| useReg[Use Register - grille with damper]
    useDiff --> q3{Available packaging space?}
    q3 -->|Compact, narrow slot| slotDiff[Slot Diffuser - common in A-pillar]
    q3 -->|Plenum behind IP| radialDiff[Radial Diffuser - common in center console]
    useReg --> q4{Manual adjustment needed?}
    q4 -->|Yes| adjReg[Adjustable Register]
    q4 -->|No| fixedGrille[Fixed Grille]
```

## 4.3 Duct Sizing Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Required airflow Qtotal and trunk length L] --> selM{Select sizing method}
    selM -->|Equal friction| eqFric[Choose friction rate R = 0.8 to 1.2 Pa per m]
    selM -->|Static regain| sgMeth[Compute static pressure at each branch - rare in autos]
    eqFric --> compD[Compute circular equivalent diameter Deq]
    compD --> packCheck{Cross section fits vehicle packaging?}
    packCheck -->|No| convert[Convert to rectangular or oval using ASHRAE eq]
    packCheck -->|Yes| accept[Circular duct acceptable - rare in cars]
    convert --> velCheck{Velocity in range 5 to 8 m per s?}
    velCheck -->|Yes| final[Finalise duct size]
    velCheck -->|No| splitNet[Split into parallel branches and re size]
    splitNet --> final
    final --> noiseChk{Noise check: Lp at driver ear less than NC45?}
    noiseChk -->|Yes| approve[Approved]
    noiseChk -->|No| addLin[Add acoustic liner or use larger duct]
    addLin --> noiseChk
```

    end

    end

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between a grille and a diffuser used in an automobile HVAC distribution system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Grille | Diffuser |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A louvered/perforated termination that simply admits or expels air | A purpose-built aerodynamic outlet that decelerates and spreads the air |
| **Aerodynamic shaping** | Minimal | Extensive (vanes, cones, slots) |
| **Application** | Return air inlet, demister outlet | Face-level and foot-level supply outlets |
| **Pressure drop** | Low | Engineered to recover static pressure |
| **Throw control** | Poor | Precise throw, drop, spread |
| **Draft control** | Poor | Good (low discharge velocity) |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the application table.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: State the standard velocity range adopted for the design of supply ducts and diffusers in a passenger car HVAC system. Justify the choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Supply duct velocity:** $5$ to **$8 \text{ m/s}$**
- **Diffuser face velocity:** $2.5$ to **$4 \text{ m/s}$**

**Justification (2 marks):**

- Higher velocity in ducts is acceptable because ducts are concealed and the air subsequently enters the diffuser where it is decelerated. Duct velocity $> 8 \text{ m/s}$ causes rapid noise escalation (since $L_w \propto V^5$ to $V^6$).
- Diffuser face velocity is kept low to avoid occupant draft and noise. Below $2.5 \text{ m/s}$, the throw is too short to reach the occupant; above $4 \text{ m/s}$, the air feels like a cold draft on the face.

---

## Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **Equal Friction Method** of duct sizing as applied to an automobile HVAC system. State the formula used and the typical friction rate value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A main supply duct in a car delivers $Q = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at a permissible friction rate of $R = 1.0 \text{ Pa/m}$. Determine (i) the circular equivalent diameter, and (ii) the equivalent rectangular dimensions for an aspect ratio $\alpha = 3$, and check if the resulting velocity lies in the recommended range. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 (2 marks): Definition.** The Equal Friction Method sizes the duct main and branches such that the frictional pressure drop per unit length ($R = \Delta P / L$) is held constant throughout the network. This produces a duct that gradually tapers as airflow reduces toward the outlets, keeping total system $\Delta P$ predictable.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Formula.** Using the ASHRAE empirical fit:

$$
D_{eq} = \left( \frac{12.67 \cdot Q^{1.9}}{R^{0.55}} \right)^{1/5.2}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Typical friction rate.** For automotive low-velocity systems, $R = 0.8$ to $1.2 \text{ Pa/m}$ is adopted. Building HVAC uses $1.0 \text{ Pa/m}$ as a common default.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Justification.** The low $R$ keeps velocity moderate (5–8 m/s) and noise acceptable, at the cost of slightly larger duct cross-section — a worthwhile trade-off in cabin comfort.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Part (i) – Circular equivalent diameter (4 marks):**

Apply the ASHRAE formula with $Q = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and $R = 1.0 \text{ Pa/m}$:

$$
D_{eq} = \left( \frac{12.67 \cdot (0.04)^{1.9}}{(1.0)^{0.55}} \right)^{1/5.2}
$$

Compute $(0.04)^{1.9}$:

$$
\ln(0.04) = -3.2189 \Rightarrow 1.9 \times (-3.2189) = -6.116 \Rightarrow e^{-6.116} = 0.002215
$$

Numerator: $12.67 \times 0.002215 = 0.02806$

Denominator: $1.0^{0.55} = 1.0$

Take 5.2-th root:

$$
\ln(0.02806) = -3.573 \Rightarrow \frac{-3.573}{5.2} = -0.687 \Rightarrow e^{-0.687} = 0.503 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{eq} = 0.503 \text{ m}}
$$

**Part (ii) – Rectangular equivalent (3 marks):**

With aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b = 3$, the ASHRAE equivalent round for rectangle:

$$
D_{eq}^{rect} = \frac{1.30 \cdot (ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}
$$

Substitute $a = 3b$:

$$
0.503 = \frac{1.30 \cdot (3b^2)^{0.625}}{(3b + b)^{0.25}} = \frac{1.30 \cdot 3^{0.625} \cdot b^{1.25}}{4^{0.25} \cdot b^{0.25}} = \frac{1.30 \cdot 1.968 \cdot b}{1.414}
$$

$$
0.503 = \frac{2.558 \cdot b}{1.414} = 1.810 \, b
$$

$$
b = \frac{0.503}{1.810} = 0.278 \text{ m}, \quad a = 3b = 0.834 \text{ m}
$$

**Velocity check:** $V = Q / A = 0.04 / (0.278 \times 0.834) = 0.04 / 0.2318 = 0.1726 \text{ m/s}$

This is **far too low** — the duct is grossly oversized for the given $Q$. This confirms that for $Q = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ the **circular equivalent of 0.5 m** is also not practically buildable; the system must use **higher R** (say 2.5–4 Pa/m) and/or split into multiple parallel branches.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $D_{eq}$ and stop, without checking if the result is **practically packageable** inside a car. Marks are lost for not performing the **feasibility check** (velocity and dimensions). Always end with: *"The result indicates a single duct of this size is impractical for an automobile; parallel branching is required."*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Discuss the **types of diffusers** commonly used in automobile HVAC systems with sketches. Compare their throw, drop, and noise characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **layout of an automobile HVAC duct system** with reference to (i) the plenum chamber, (ii) supply ducts (face, foot, demist, rear), (iii) return duct, and (iv) the noise control measures employed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Types of diffusers (5 marks):**

1. **Radial (Cone) Diffuser:** Circular outlet with concentric cones or vanes producing a $360°$ radial spread. Used in the **center console** for face-level ventilation of driver and co-passenger.
   - **Throw:** Medium (0.4–0.7 m)
   - **Drop:** Medium
   - **Noise:** Lowest (best static pressure recovery)

2. **Slot Diffuser:** Long, narrow rectangular slot (typically 200–400 mm long, 5–15 mm wide). Used in **A-pillar** and **IP top** for windshield demisting and face-level supply.
   - **Throw:** Long, narrow (Coanda-attached to windshield)
   - **Drop:** Very low (air clings to glass)
   - **Noise:** Low

3. **Louvered Diffuser / Register:** Multiple parallel louvres that can be manually rotated. Used for **face-level side outlets** on dashboard ends.
   - **Throw:** Adjustable (short to medium)
   - **Drop:** Medium
   - **Noise:** Moderate

4. **Perforated Face Diffuser:** Used for **foot-level** outlets where low velocity and downward throw are desired.
   - **Throw:** Short, downward
   - **Drop:** High
   - **Noise:** Very low

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Diffuser Type | Throw | Drop | Noise | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Radial cone | Medium | Medium | Lowest | Center console |
| Slot | Long, narrow | Very low | Low | A-pillar, IP top |
| Louvered register | Adjustable | Medium | Moderate | Dashboard ends |
| Perforated | Short | High | Very low | Foot-level |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Layout of an automobile HVAC duct system:**

**(i) Plenum chamber (1.5 marks):** A **sealed box (plenum)** is mounted directly behind the dashboard, housing the evaporator, heater core, blower, and mode doors. The plenum acts as a pressurised air distribution manifold — air leaving the blower enters the plenum and is routed via the mode door to either the A/C evaporator, the heater core, or both (for blended air). The plenum has multiple duct take-offs (face, foot, demist, rear).

**(ii) Supply ducts (2.5 marks):**
- **Face-level ducts:** Run from the plenum through the centre of the IP to the centre console and the dashboard end outlets (driver and co-passenger face diffusers).
- **Foot-level ducts:** Direct conditioned air downward through ducts in the IP lower section to the driver and co-passenger footwell grilles.
- **Demist ducts:** Run up the **A-pillars** to slot diffusers that blow air tangentially across the inner surface of the windshield to prevent fogging.
- **Rear ducts:** Run under the centre console or along the transmission tunnel to the rear passenger face/foot outlets.

**(iii) Return duct (1.5 marks):** A single large return grille is typically located **under the dashboard** (often behind the glove box) or on the **side kick panel**. Air is drawn through a **cabin air filter** (particulate and charcoal) and returned to the blower inlet. The return duct is generally larger in cross-section to keep face velocity low (≤ 3 m/s) and noise low.

**(iv) Noise control measures (1.5 marks):**
- **Duct liners:** Foam or fibreglass lining of 6–12 mm on duct inner walls absorbs aerodynamic noise.
- **Hood silencers:** Helmholtz-resonator-type silencers fitted to blower inlet or outlet to attenuate blower whine.
- **Low velocity design:** Duct sizing keeps $V \leq 8$ m/s to keep $L_w \propto V^5$ from blowing up.
- **Blower RPM control:** Blower speed is electronically ramped to avoid sudden noise transients.
- **Vibration isolation:** Flexible couplings between blower motor and housing to avoid structure-borne noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Students often describe *building* HVAC ducts (circular GI ducts with VAV boxes). In **automobiles**, ducts are **mostly rectangular/oval plastic (PP, ABS, or PP-EPDM)** and integrated into the **instrument panel, pillars, and console** — there is no suspended ceiling. Marks lost if this distinction is not made.
>
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Forgetting the **cabin air filter** in the return path. A 1-mark question on return duct often expects a 0.5-mark mention of the filter.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 3, Distribution Duct System**

- **Duct Sizing:** Equal friction method is the **default for automobiles**; static regain is for large commercial systems and is rarely used in cars.
- **ASHRAE Equivalent Round Formula:** $D_{eq} = \left( 12.67 \cdot Q^{1.9} / R^{0.55} \right)^{1/5.2}$ — *memorise this for Part B numericals.*
- **ASHRAE Rectangular Equivalent:** $D_{eq}^{rect} = 1.30 \cdot (ab)^{0.625} / (a+b)^{0.25}$ — *memorise this for Part B conversions.*
- **Velocity Targets:** Duct $V = 5$–$8 \text{ m/s}$; Diffuser face $V = 2.5$–$4 \text{ m/s}$; Return grille $V \leq 3 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Friction rate** for low-velocity automotive ducts: $R = 0.8$–$1.2 \text{ Pa/m}$.
- **Grille vs Diffuser:** Grille = simple termination; Diffuser = aerodynamically shaped outlet for spread and draft control.
- **Diffuser Types (4):** Radial cone, slot, louvered register, perforated face.
- **Supply Ducts in a Car:** Face-level, foot-level, demist (A-pillar), rear (console/tunnel).
- **Return Duct Location:** Below IP, near glove box or kick panel, with cabin air filter.
- **Air Change Rate:** 20–30 ACH in fresh-air mode.
- **Noise Criterion:** NC 40–50 acceptable; aim for $L_p \leq 40$ dB(A) at driver ear at full blower.
- **Noise scaling law:** $L_w \propto V^{5\text{–}6}$ — halve the velocity to cut noise by ~15 dB.
- **Throw design rule:** $T \approx 1.5 \times$ distance from outlet to occupant's upper body.
- **Duct material in cars:** Plastic (PP, ABS) — not metal — to reduce weight, cost, and condensation.
- **Insulation:** Supply ducts that carry cold air are **externally insulated** to prevent dew-point condensation on the dashboard.
- **Recirculation door:** Plenum-mounted; when closed, only cabin air is recirculated; when open, fresh ambient air enters through the cowl grille.
- **Mode doors:** Three or four doors in the plenum direct air to demist, face, foot, or bi-level outlets.
- **Acoustic treatment:** Duct liners (foam/glass wool), hood silencers (Helmholtz resonators), and flexible blower mounts.

**Common numerical pattern (Part B, 7 marks):**

> *"Compute the circular equivalent diameter of a duct carrying $Q \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at a friction rate of $R$ Pa/m, then convert to a rectangular section with aspect ratio $\alpha$, and check velocity."*
>
> *Step template:* (1) Apply ASHRAE $D_{eq}$ formula → (2) Substitute $a = \alpha b$ into the rectangular equivalent formula → (3) Solve for $b$ and $a$ → (4) Compute $V = Q/(ab)$ → (5) Comment on feasibility and recommend changes if $V$ is out of range.

**One-line definitions (for Part A, 3-mark questions):**
- **Grille:** Louvered or perforated termination device.
- **Diffuser:** Aerodynamic outlet that decelerates and spreads the air.
- **Equal Friction Method:** Duct sizing that maintains constant $\Delta P / L$ throughout the system.
- **Friction Rate ($R$):** Pressure drop per unit length of duct, in Pa/m.
- **Noise Criterion (NC):** Single-number index of octave-band sound pressure level, used to specify acceptable HVAC noise.
- **Throw:** Horizontal distance from a diffuser outlet to the point where the jet velocity falls to 0.25 m/s.
- **Drop:** Vertical fall of an air jet over its throw.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Routing and Temperature Control: Objectives, Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit, Automatic Temperature Control, Duct System, Controlling Flow, and Vacuum Reserve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Routing and Temperature Control: Objectives, Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit, Automatic Temperature Control, Duct System, Controlling Flow, and Vacuum Reserve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Air Routing and Temperature Control** in a vehicular HVAC system refers to the integrated arrangement of ducts, doors, actuators, sensors, and control logic that directs conditioned air from the HVAC module to specific outlets (defrost, panel, floor) while simultaneously maintaining the desired cabin temperature through automatic modulation of blower speed, blend door position, and refrigerant flow.

The **Recirculating Unit** is the central air-handling housing that contains the evaporator, heater core, blower, filter, and blend door, and is responsible for the controlled re-circulation of cabin air across the cooling and heating surfaces.

**Automatic Temperature Control (ATC)** is a closed-loop feedback system that continuously samples in-car, ambient, and sun-load temperatures and automatically positions the blend door, mode doors, and blower to maintain a user-selected set-point temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module is the highest-weightage portion of PEMUT742 (≈ 25–30 % of ESE marks). Mastery of door-actuation logic, blend-air equations, and vacuum reserve sizing is essential for full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the HVAC system as a **postman with multiple doors**:

- The **postman (blower)** carries letters (air) into a building.
- The **building (recirculating unit)** has several rooms — a cold room (evaporator) and a hot room (heater core).
- The **blend door** is like a *mixing valve* on a shower — it decides how much cold water and how much hot water to mix before sending it to you.
- The **mode doors** are like the *valves on each floor* — they decide whether the air goes to the *windshield* (defrost), *dashboard vents* (panel), or *your feet* (floor).
- The **thermostat (ATC controller)** is the *building manager* who keeps checking the temperature and adjusting the mixing valve automatically so you never feel too hot or too cold.
- The **vacuum reserve** is a *stored water tank* — it stores engine vacuum so that even when the engine is barely running, there is enough "pneumatic pressure" to operate the mode doors smoothly.

### 1.3 Engineering Constants & Standards

- Standard cabin set-point range: **18 °C – 32 °C** (most OEMs)
- Engine manifold vacuum: **15 – 20 in. Hg** (idle); drops to **3 – 5 in. Hg** at WOT
- Vacuum actuator working pressure: **10 – 15 in. Hg**
- Blower air delivery: **150 – 450 CFM** (passenger car)
- Duct velocity limit: **≤ 8 m/s** (panel vents), **≤ 12 m/s** (main trunk)
- Typical evaporator air-side ΔT: **8 °C – 12 °C**
- Heater core air-side ΔT: **40 °C – 70 °C** (max heat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Convention:** All temperatures in °C, pressures in kPa or in. Hg, airflow in CFM (cubic feet per minute) or m³/h. 1 CFM = **0.000 471 947 443 m³/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Air Routing and Temperature Control

The system must achieve **five concurrent engineering objectives**:

1. **Thermal Comfort** — Maintain cabin air within the ASHRAE comfort envelope (operative temperature 20 °C – 26 °C in summer, 22 °C – 24 °C in winter).
2. **Defogging / Defrosting** — Deliver warm, dry air to the windshield to keep the glass above the dew point and clear of frost/condensation.
3. **Air Distribution Efficiency** — Direct air to the *correct* outlet (face, feet, windshield) with minimum leakage and noise.
4. **Energy Efficiency** — Minimise compressor and blower work by using blend-air modulation (using waste heat) before engaging refrigeration.
5. **Quick Response** — Achieve set-point temperature within 30 – 60 seconds of adjustment.

### 2.2 Components of the Recirculating Unit (Step-by-Step Logic)

| # | Component | Function | Why it Matters |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Blower Motor & Fan** | Forces cabin air through the HVAC module | Provides the pressure head (ΔP) to overcome core pressure drops |
| 2 | **Air Filter (Cabin Filter)** | Removes dust, pollen, PM2.5 | Protects evaporator fins; improves IAQ |
| 3 | **Evaporator Core** | Refrigerant evaporates → absorbs latent + sensible heat | Primary cooling device |
| 4 | **Heater Core** | Engine coolant (90 °C) transfers heat to air | Primary heating device (uses waste heat) |
| 5 | **Blend (Mix) Door** | Variable-position damper between cold and hot air streams | Sets delivery temperature |
| 6 | **Mode Doors** | Defrost / Panel / Floor / Bi-level routing | Directs air to specific outlets |
| 7 | **Recirculation Door** | Fresh vs. re-circulated cabin air | Affects cooling load and CO₂ buildup |
| 8 | **Expansion Valve / Orifice Tube** | Meter refrigerant into evaporator | Couples refrigerant side to air side |
| 9 | **Thermostatic Switch / Evaporator Sensor** | Cuts compressor at ≈ 0 °C evap. temp. | Prevents coil freeze-up |
| 10 | **ATC Sensors & Actuators** | Feedback & control elements | Enables automatic operation |

### 2.3 Automatic Temperature Control (ATC) — Operational Logic

The ATC is a **multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) closed-loop control system**. Its control algorithm performs the following:

**Inputs (Sensors):**
- In-car temperature sensor (NTC thermistor, aspirated)
- Ambient (outside) air temperature sensor
- Sun-load sensor (photodiode on dashboard)
- Evaporator outlet air temperature
- Coolant temperature
- Humidity sensor (premium vehicles)

**Outputs (Actuators):**
- Blend door position (DC servo / stepper motor)
- Blower speed (PWM / FET / resistor pack)
- Mode door position(s)
- Recirculation door position
- A/C compressor clutch / variable displacement

**Control Law (Simplified):**

The error signal is computed as:

$$e(t) = T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{car,filtered}}$$

where $T_{\text{car,filtered}}$ is a low-pass-filtered cabin temperature to suppress door-opening transients.

The control output is typically a **PI (Proportional-Integral) law**:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

with anti-windup clamping. Sun load adds an offset:

$$u_{\text{effective}} = u(t) + K_{\text{sun}} \cdot I_{\text{sun}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern ATC uses a **two-degree-of-freedom structure** — a *feed-forward* term compensates for sun load and ambient temperature instantly, while the *feedback* PI term removes steady-state error.

### 2.4 Duct System

The duct system is the **plumbing of the HVAC module**. Design objectives:

- Minimum **pressure drop** (ΔP ≤ 250 Pa in trunk duct)
- Uniform **velocity profile** at outlets
- **Acoustic comfort** (NC-45 cabin target → duct velocity ≤ 8 m/s)
- **Thermal insulation** of cooled-air ducts to prevent re-heat from engine bay

**Duct Material Properties (Reference Values):**

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Thermal Cond. (W/m·K) | Application |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|------------|
| EPP Foam | 30 – 80 | 0.035 | Plenum / main ducts |
| PP + Talc | 950 | 0.22 | Structural ducts |
| ABS | 1050 | 0.20 | Mode-door housings |
| Aluminium | 2700 | 205 | Premium high-temp ducts |

### 2.5 Controlling Flow — Door Actuation Methods

| Actuation Type | Energy Source | Force Output | Used For |
|----------------|---------------|--------------|----------|
| **Vacuum Actuator** | Engine manifold vacuum | 20 – 80 N | Mode doors, recirc door |
| **Electric Servo (DC)** | 12 V supply | 5 – 15 N·m | Blend door (precision) |
| **Stepper Motor** | 12 V pulsed | 0.05 – 0.2 N·m | Premium blend door |
| **PWM-controlled Servo** | 12 V PWM | Variable | Variable blower speed |

### 2.6 Vacuum Reserve

**Why needed:** At idle the engine generates adequate vacuum, but at wide-open throttle (WOT) manifold vacuum collapses to near-atmospheric, leaving no force to actuate doors. At part-throttle or decelerating (DFCO), vacuum is high.

**Vacuum Reserve Components:**

1. **Intake Manifold Tap** — Source point (above throttle plate)
2. **Check Valve (Non-Return)** — Holds vacuum in reservoir when engine vacuum drops
3. **Reservoir Tank** — Stores vacuum (typically **0.5 L – 2 L** capacity)
4. **Vacuum Bleed Valve** — Releases reserve slowly to prevent "snap" actuation
5. **Vacuum Harnesses** — Nylon tubing routing vacuum to actuators

**Functional States:**

| Engine Condition | Manifold Vacuum | Check Valve | Reservoir State |
|------------------|----------------|-------------|------------------|
| Idle | 18 in. Hg | Open (charging) | Stays charged |
| Cruise | 15 in. Hg | Open | Maintains charge |
| WOT | 4 in. Hg | Closed (isolates) | Supplies reserve |
| DFCO | 22 in. Hg | Open | Recharges fully |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = V \cdot A$ | Volumetric airflow | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot V \cdot A$ | Mass airflow | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer | W |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{lat}} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$ | Latent heat (moisture removal) | W |
| 5 | $\Delta P = f \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach duct pressure drop | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{manifold}} = \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Vacuum pressure differential | Pa |
| 7 | $V_{\text{reserve}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{leak}} \cdot t_{\text{reserve}}}{\ln(P_i / P_f)}$ | Vacuum reservoir sizing | m³ |
| 8 | $T_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h$ | Blend-air mixing temperature | °C |
| 9 | $R_{\text{blower}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}}}{V^2}$ | Fan affinity law (speed) | — |
| 10 | $\text{BHP} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | Blower brake horsepower | W |
| 11 | $e(t) = T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{car}}$ | ATC error signal | °C |
| 12 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt$ | PI control law | % door position |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Exam:** Always assume $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Blend-Air (Mixing) Equation

**Problem Setup:** Cold air (from evaporator) at $T_c$ with mass flow $\dot{m}_c$ mixes with hot air (from heater core) at $T_h$ with mass flow $\dot{m}_h$. Determine the mixed-air temperature $T_m$.

**Step 1 — Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to the adiabatic mixing chamber (no heat loss to surroundings, no work):

$$\dot{m}_c h_c + \dot{m}_h h_h = (\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h) h_m$$

where $h$ is specific enthalpy.

**Step 2 — Use ideal-gas relation** for air: $h = c_p T$. Substitute:

$$\dot{m}_c c_p T_c + \dot{m}_h c_p T_h = (\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h) c_p T_m$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $c_p$** (identical for both streams since both are air):

$$\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h = (\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h) T_m$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_m$:**

$$\boxed{\;T_m = \frac{\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce blend-door ratio $r$** defined as the fraction of total airflow that passes through the heater core:
