$$r = \frac{\dot{m}_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}, \quad 1 - r = \frac{\dot{m}_c}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into mixing equation:**

$$T_m = (1 - r) T_c + r T_h = T_c + r(T_h - T_c)$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
$T_c = 5 \text{ °C}$, $T_h = 55 \text{ °C}$, blend door at 40 % heater flow. Find $T_m$:

$$T_m = 5 + 0.40 \cdot (55 - 5) = 5 + 20 = 25 \text{ °C}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vacuum Reserve Sizing

**Problem Setup:** A vacuum actuator requires $\dot{V}_{\text{leak}} = 0.05 \text{ L/s}$ of air to hold its position. The reservoir must hold enough vacuum to keep doors actuated for $t_{\text{reserve}} = 30$ s during WOT.

**Step 1 — Total air volume consumed:**

$$V_{\text{consumed}} = \dot{V}_{\text{leak}} \cdot t_{\text{reserve}} = 0.05 \times 30 = 1.5 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Isothermal expansion in reservoir** (slow process → isothermal):

$$P_i V_i = P_f V_f$$

Initial state: $P_i = 18 \text{ in. Hg} = 60.96 \text{ kPa absolute}$ (≈ 40.4 kPa vacuum). Final usable state: $P_f = 10 \text{ in. Hg} = 33.87 \text{ kPa absolute}$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_i$** given $V_f = V_i + V_{\text{consumed}}$:

$$P_i V_i = P_f (V_i + V_{\text{consumed}})$$

$$V_i (P_i - P_f) = P_f \cdot V_{\text{consumed}}$$

$$V_i = \frac{P_f \cdot V_{\text{consumed}}}{P_i - P_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$V_i = \frac{33.87 \times 1.5}{60.96 - 33.87} = \frac{50.805}{27.09} = 1.875 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor of 1.5** to account for multiple simultaneous actuators:

$$\boxed{\;V_{\text{reservoir,design}} = 1.5 \times 1.875 = 2.81 \text{ L} \approx 3 \text{ L}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blower Power and Fan Laws

**Step 1 — Volume flow rate of air through evaporator:**

$$\dot{V} = V_{\text{duct}} \cdot A_{\text{duct}}$$

For $\dot{V} = 250 \text{ CFM} = 0.118 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and duct area $A = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$:

$$V_{\text{duct}} = \frac{0.118}{0.04} = 2.95 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure drop across evaporator + heater core + filter:**

$$\Delta P = \Delta P_{\text{evap}} + \Delta P_{\text{heater}} + \Delta P_{\text{filter}}$$

Typical values: $\Delta P_{\text{evap}} = 120 \text{ Pa}$, $\Delta P_{\text{heater}} = 80 \text{ Pa}$, $\Delta P_{\text{filter}} = 50 \text{ Pa}$.

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 120 + 80 + 50 = 250 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Blower power requirement:**

$$\text{BHP} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$$

With $\eta_{\text{fan}} = 0.55$:

$$\text{BHP} = \frac{0.118 \times 250}{0.55} = 53.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Fan Affinity Law** for speed change:

$$\frac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — ATC PI Controller Stability Check

**Step 1 — Open-loop transfer function** (first-order plant approximation):

$$G(s) = \frac{K_{\text{plant}}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K_{\text{plant}} = 2.5 \text{ °C/%}$ and $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function with PI controller** $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic equation** $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$:

$$\tau s^2 + (1 + K_p K_{\text{plant}}) s + K_i K_{\text{plant}} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compare to standard 2nd order** $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{1 + K_p K_{\text{plant}}}{\tau}, \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{K_i K_{\text{plant}}}{\tau}$$

**Step 5 — For $\zeta = 0.7$ (good transient response) and $\tau = 8$:**

$$K_p = \frac{2\zeta\omega_n \tau - 1}{K_{\text{plant}}}$$

Choose $\omega_n = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$:

$$K_p = \frac{2 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 8 - 1}{2.5} = \frac{5.6 - 1}{2.5} = 1.84$$

$$K_i = \frac{\omega_n^2 \tau}{K_{\text{plant}}} = \frac{0.25 \times 8}{2.5} = 0.8 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Door Position vs Function

| Blend Door | Mode Door | Recirc Door | Resulting Airflow |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Full Cold | Panel | Recirc | Max A/C, face only |
| 50/50 | Bi-level | Fresh | Comfort cooling, face + feet |
| Full Hot | Floor | Fresh | Heater, footwell |
| Full Hot | Defrost | Fresh | Defrost, windshield |
| 70/30 | Defrost + Floor | Fresh | Demist + warm feet |
| Full Cold | Panel | Fresh | Fresh-air cooling |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Q: A 1500 kg car travels at 80 km/h. The HVAC system delivers 0.12 m³/s of air at 35 °C through an evaporator with 90 % effectiveness. The evaporator surface is at 2 °C. Determine (a) exit air temperature, (b) cooling capacity, (c) compressor work if COP = 2.8.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Exit air temperature** using $\varepsilon$-NTU for sensible heat exchanger:

$$T_{\text{exit}} = T_{\text{evap}} + \varepsilon (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap}})$$

$$T_{\text{exit}} = 2 + 0.90 \times (35 - 2) = 2 + 29.7 = 31.7 \text{ °C}$$

**(b) Cooling capacity:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{exit}}) = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$$

$$\dot{Q} = 1.2 \times 0.12 \times 1005 \times (35 - 31.7)$$

$$\dot{Q} = 1.2 \times 0.12 \times 1005 \times 3.3 = 477.6 \text{ W}$$

**(c) Compressor work** with $\text{COP} = \dot{Q}_{\text{evap}} / W_{\text{comp}}$:

$$W_{\text{comp}} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\text{COP}} = \frac{477.6}{2.8} = 170.6 \text{ W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Recirculating Unit Airflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cabin Return Air] --> B[Blower Motor Centrifugal Fan]
    B --> C1[Cabin Air Filter PM 2.5]
    C1 --> C2[Evaporator Core Refrigerant R134a]
    C2 --> D{Blend Door Position}
    D -->|Cold Path| E1[Panel Vents]
    D -->|Hot Path| E2[Heater Core Engine Coolant 90C]
    E2 --> F[Mixed Air Stream]
    F --> G{Mode Door Logic}
    G -->|Defrost| H1[Windshield Outlets]
    G -->|Panel| H2[Dashboard Vents]
    G -->|Floor| H3[Footwell Outlets]
    G -->|Bi-Level| H4[Panel Plus Floor]
    H1 --> I[Cabin Occupants]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    E1 --> I
    H4 --> I
    J[Recirculation Door] -.->|Fresh Outside Air| B
    J -.->|Recirc Cabin Air| B
    K[ATC Controller ECU] -.->|Blend Position PWM| D
    K -.->|Mode Command| G
    K -.->|Blower Speed| B
    K -.->|Recirc Command| J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Automatic Temperature Control (ATC) Closed-Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Set Temperature e.g. 22C] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C1[In-Car Temp Sensor] --> B
    C2[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> F[Feed-Forward Compensator]
    C3[Sun Load Sensor] --> F
    C4[Evaporator Outlet Temp] --> F
    F --> B
    B -->|Error e of t| D[PI Controller Kp Ki]
    D --> E[Actuator Command Blend Door and Blower]
    E --> G[HVAC Plant Air Side]
    G -->|Conditioned Air| H[Cabin Volume]
    H -->|Thermal Feedback| C1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Vacuum Reserve System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Intake Manifold] -->|Vacuum Tap| B[Inline Check Valve]
    B --> C[Vacuum Reservoir Tank 3L]
    C --> D1[Vacuum Harness Tube 1]
    C --> D2[Vacuum Harness Tube 2]
    C --> D3[Vacuum Harness Tube 3]
    D1 --> E1[Mode Door Actuator Defrost]
    D2 --> E2[Mode Door Actuator Floor Panel]
    D3 --> E3[Recirculation Door Actuator]
    F[Vacuum Bleed Orifice] -.->|Slow Release| C
    G[Filter Screen] -.-> A
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Duct System Layout (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HVAC Module Evap and Heater] --> B1[Plenum Duct Main Trunk]
    B1 --> C1[Branch Duct Left Defrost]
    B1 --> C2[Branch Duct Right Defrost]
    B1 --> D1[Center Panel Vent Duct]
    B1 --> D2[Side Panel Vent Ducts Pair]
    B1 --> E1[Rear Floor Duct Under Seat]
    B1 --> E2[Front Floor Duct]
    C1 --> F1[Left Windshield Outlet]
    C2 --> F2[Right Windshield Outlet]
    D1 --> G1[Center Dashboard Louvers]
    D2 --> G2[Side Dashboard Louvers]
    E1 --> H1[Rear Footwell]
    E2 --> H2[Front Footwell]
```

### 4.5 Process Flow — ATC Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Read All Sensors]
    B --> C{Delta T Sun Load Big}
    C -->|Yes| D[Open Blend Door to Cool Side]
    C -->|No| E[Compute PI Error]
    E --> F{Error Positive Need Cool}
    F -->|Yes| G[Increase Blower Speed and Open Cold Path]
    F -->|No| H[Decrease Blower Speed and Open Hot Path]
    G --> I[Update Actuators]
    H --> I
    D --> I
    I --> J[Wait 100 ms Sample Time]
    J --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define a *recirculating unit* in an automotive HVAC system. List its four main components.

**Model Answer:**

A **recirculating unit** is the central air-handling module of the HVAC system that receives cabin air (or fresh air), conditions it thermally, and re-distributes it to the cabin. It is called "recirculating" because under default mode it *re-circulates* cabin air rather than drawing in 100 % fresh air, which reduces cooling load and improves efficiency.

**Four main components:**

1. **Blower motor and centrifugal fan** — provides the air-moving force.
2. **Evaporator core** — cools and dehumidifies the air.
3. **Heater core** — uses engine coolant to heat the air.
4. **Blend (air-mix) door** — proportions the cold and hot air streams.

*Supplementary*: cabin filter, expansion valve, mode doors, and the housing (HVAC case).

**[3 Marks: 1 definition + 2 marks for listing components]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
What is a *vacuum reserve tank*? Why is it necessary in a vacuum-operated HVAC system?

**Model Answer:**

A **vacuum reserve tank** is a sealed, evacuated reservoir (typically **0.5 L to 3 L** capacity) connected between the engine intake manifold and the vacuum actuators. It is fitted with a **one-way check valve** that allows the reservoir to *charge* when manifold vacuum is high (idle, cruise, DFCO) and to *discharge* when manifold vacuum falls (WOT, heavy load).

**Necessity:**

- At **WOT**, manifold vacuum collapses to 3–5 in. Hg, insufficient to actuate doors. The reserve supplies stored vacuum.
- Ensures **smooth, uninterrupted door actuation** during transient engine conditions.
- Prevents **door "flutter"** between positions.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks necessity]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Model Question A]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Automatic Temperature Control (ATC) system** in an automotive HVAC application. List its sensors and actuators. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A vacuum-operated HVAC system has 3 actuators each consuming **0.04 L/s** of air to hold position. The reservoir must hold doors for **45 seconds** during wide-open-throttle. Initial vacuum = **17 in. Hg**, final usable vacuum = **10 in. Hg**. Design the reservoir volume with a safety factor of **1.4**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working of ATC (Block Diagram):**

```
Set-point → Σ ← In-car sensor
            ↓
      Error e(t) → PI Controller → Actuator Command → Blend Door & Blower
            ↑                                              ↓
            ←───────────── Cabin (Plant) ←─────────────────
        Sun load & ambient → feed-forward compensator
```

**Sensors:**
1. **In-car temperature sensor** (NTC thermistor, aspirated by a small suction motor).
2. **Ambient (outside) air temperature sensor** (located behind front grille).
3. **Sun-load sensor** (photodiode on top of dashboard, measures solar intensity).
4. **Evaporator outlet air temperature** sensor.
5. **Coolant temperature sensor** (input to ATC for heater availability).
6. **Humidity sensor** (premium vehicles).

**Actuators:**
1. **Blend door actuator** — DC servo or stepper motor; sets air-mix ratio.
2. **Blower motor speed controller** — PWM-controlled FET or resistor pack.
3. **Mode door actuator(s)** — Vacuum or electric; route air to defrost/panel/floor.
4. **Recirculation door actuator** — Switches fresh ↔ recirculated air.
5. **A/C compressor clutch or variable-displacement valve** — Engages refrigeration.

**Control Algorithm (Explanation):**

The ATC computes a filtered cabin temperature $T_{\text{car}}$ and compares it with the set-point $T_{\text{set}}$ to form an error $e(t)$. A **PI controller** generates a control signal $u(t)$ that drives the blend door. A **feed-forward** term from sun load and ambient instantly compensates for disturbances. The system runs in a **100 ms loop**, continuously updating actuator positions. The result: the cabin temperature remains within ±0.5 °C of the set-point, even under varying external conditions.

**[7 Marks: 3 for diagram + 2 for sensors/actuators + 2 for algorithm]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Total leak rate:**

$$\dot{V}_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 0.04 = 0.12 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Air volume consumed in 45 s:**

$$V_{\text{consumed}} = 0.12 \times 45 = 5.4 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Convert vacuum levels to absolute pressure:**

$$P_i = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac},i} = 101.325 - (17 \times 3.386) = 101.325 - 57.56 = 43.76 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_f = 101.325 - (10 \times 3.386) = 101.325 - 33.86 = 67.46 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply isothermal expansion** $P_i V_i = P_f (V_i + V_{\text{consumed}})$:

$$V_i (P_i - P_f) = P_f \cdot V_{\text{consumed}}$$

$$V_i (43.76 - 67.46) = 67.46 \times 5.4$$

$$V_i (-23.70) = 364.28$$

$$V_i = -15.37 \text{ L (magnitude)}$$

Wait — this means the reservoir must already contain ~15.4 L of air at $P_i$ to expand and supply the 5.4 L of additional air at $P_f$.

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor 1.4:**

$$V_{\text{design}} = 1.4 \times 15.37 = 21.5 \text{ L}$$

**Answer: Reservoir volume ≈ 22 L** (commercially rounded up).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *vacuum* (gauge) with *absolute* pressure. The isothermal expansion **must** use absolute pressures. Mixing the two will give a negative or impossibly small answer and result in full 7-mark deduction.

**[7 Marks: 1 for total leak + 2 for volume consumed + 2 for isothermal calc + 2 for safety factor]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [Model Question B]

**(a)** Explain the **duct system** of an automotive HVAC module. With sketches, describe the materials used, velocity limits, and pressure-drop design criteria. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A blower delivers **0.15 m³/s** of air through a duct of **0.05 m²** cross-section. The duct has friction factor $f = 0.02$, length **L = 1.2 m**, equivalent diameter **D = 0.22 m**. Density of air = **1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) duct velocity, (ii) pressure drop, (iii) blower power required assuming fan efficiency **55 %**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **duct system** of an automotive HVAC module is the network of passages that distribute conditioned air from the HVAC plenum to various outlets (defrost, panel, floor).

**Components of the Duct System:**

1. **Plenum (main trunk duct)** — receives air from the blower.
2. **Branch ducts** — divide the airflow into separate streams.
3. **Mode-door housings** — contain rotating doors that route air.
4. **Outlet ducts** — terminate at dashboard louvers, windshield, or footwell.
5. **Seals and gaskets** — prevent leakage at joints.

**Materials Used:**

| Material | Properties | Application |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| EPP (Expanded Polypropylene) | Light, insulating, good acoustic damping | Plenum, main ducts |
| PP + 20 % Talc | Rigid, heat-resistant | Structural ducts |
| ABS | Tough, paintable | Mode-door housings |
| TPE / EPDM | Flexible | Seals, bellows |
| Aluminium | High-temp resistant | Heater-to-duct connection |

**Velocity Limits (NC-45 acoustic criterion):**

- Main trunk: **V ≤ 8 m/s**
- Branch ducts: **V ≤ 6 m/s**
- Outlet louvers: **V ≤ 4 m/s** (to prevent noise)

**Pressure-Drop Criterion:**

- Total system ΔP ≤ **250 Pa** at full blower speed.
- Ducts sized using **equal-friction method** (ΔP per unit length ≈ 1 Pa/m).

**Design Philosophy:** Larger ducts → lower velocity & noise, but more packaging space and weight. Engineers iterate CFD simulations to find the *minimum cross-section* that satisfies both ΔP and acoustic constraints.

**[7 Marks: 2 components + 2 materials + 2 velocity/pressure + 1 design philosophy]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Duct velocity:**

$$V = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{0.15}{0.05} = 3.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Substitute values:

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \times \frac{1.2}{0.22} \times \frac{1.2 \times (3.0)^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \times 5.4545 \times \frac{1.2 \times 9}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \times 5.4545 \times 5.4$$

$$\Delta P = 0.589 \text{ Pa}$$

**(iii) Blower power required:**

$$\text{BHP} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$$

$$\text{BHP} = \frac{0.15 \times 0.589}{0.55} = 0.1606 \text{ W} \approx 0.161 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students sometimes use **hydraulic diameter** $D_h$ where geometric diameter $D$ is intended, or forget to **square the velocity** in the dynamic pressure term. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency (mixing mm with m, or Pa with kPa).

**[7 Marks: 2 velocity + 3 pressure drop + 2 blower power]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Pitfalls for This Module

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
> 1. Confusing **vacuum** (gauge) with **absolute** pressure in reserve-tank calculations.
> 2. Forgetting to **square the velocity** in Bernoulli / Darcy–Weisbach equations.
> 3. Not specifying **boundary conditions** ($P_{\text{atm}}$, $\rho$, $c_p$) at the start of a numerical.
> 4. Drawing ATC as a simple on/off thermostat instead of a **closed-loop PI controller**.
> 5. Omitting the **safety factor** when designing reservoir / duct sizes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Recirculating unit** = blower + filter + evaporator + heater core + blend door.
- ✅ **Air routing** directs air to defrost / panel / floor using **mode doors**.
- ✅ **Blend door** sets the *temperature*; **mode door** sets the *destination*; **recirc door** sets the *source*.
- ✅ **ATC** uses **PI control** with **feed-forward** from sun load and ambient.
- ✅ **ATC sensors**: in-car, ambient, sun-load, evaporator-out, coolant.
- ✅ **ATC actuators**: blend door, mode doors, recirc door, blower speed, A/C clutch.
- ✅ **Vacuum reserve** stores engine vacuum in a sealed tank via a **check valve**.
- ✅ **Reserve sizing** uses **isothermal expansion**: $P_i V_i = P_f (V_i + V_{\text{consumed}})$.
- ✅ **Duct velocity limits**: ≤ **8 m/s** in trunk, ≤ **4 m/s** at outlets.
- ✅ **Duct materials**: EPP foam for insulation, PP-talc for structure, ABS for housings.
- ✅ **Blower power**: $\text{BHP} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta P / \eta_{\text{fan}}$.
- ✅ **Blend-air equation**: $T_m = (1 - r) T_c + r T_h$.
- ✅ **Fan laws**: $\dot{V} \propto N$, $\Delta P \propto N^2$, Power $\propto N^3$.
- ✅ **Cabin set-point accuracy**: ±0.5 °C in modern ATC.
- ✅ **Standard conditions**: $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- ✅ **Conversion**: 1 in. Hg = **3.386 kPa**; 1 CFM = **0.000 471 947 m³/s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT742 – Module 3)**
> *Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems* refers to the systematic evaluation, measurement, and verification of airflow rates, pressures, velocities, and distribution patterns within an Air Handling Unit (AHU) and its associated duct network to confirm that the system meets the engineered design specifications, performance benchmarks, and regulatory standards (ISHRAE, ASHRAE, SMACNA, NBC 2016).

In a typical HVAC installation, the **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** is the "heart" of the system. It conditions the air (heating, cooling, filtering, humidifying) and then circulates it through a maze of supply ducts, diffusers, return ducts, and dampers. The phrase **"Air Control"** in this context is an umbrella term covering:

- **Volumetric air flow rate** (cubic metres per hour, $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, or cubic feet per minute, **CFM**).
- **Static, velocity, and total pressures** within ducts.
- **Damper positions and modulation accuracy.**
- **Air distribution balance** between zones.
- **Leakage and tightness** of the ductwork.
- **Fan performance** (actual vs. design curves).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs and Bloodstream" Model
Imagine the AHU as the **lungs** of a building and the ductwork as the **bloodstream**. Just as a doctor tests lung capacity and blood circulation using a spirometer and stethoscope, an HVAC engineer "tests the air control" using instruments like a **pitot tube, anemometer, and manometer**. If the lungs are weak (under-performing fan) or the arteries are clogged (closed/leaky ducts), the organs (rooms) won't receive the right amount of oxygenated blood (conditioned air). Testing the air control is essentially the **medical check-up of the building's respiratory system.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Air Control Testing Critical for KTU/Industry?**
> 1. **Energy Efficiency:** Unbalanced or leaky systems can waste **20–40 %** of fan energy.
> 2. **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ):** Improper airflow leads to $\text{CO}_2$ build-up, stale zones, and Sick Building Syndrome.
> 3. **Comfort Compliance:** Ensures that temperature, humidity, and ventilation rates meet **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** (minimum **$8.4 \text{ L/s}$ per person** for office spaces).
> 4. **Equipment Longevity:** Incorrect airflow can cause coil freeze-ups, filter blow-outs, and motor overheating.
> 5. **Code Compliance:** Required for building handover under the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8 – Building Services, Section 4)**.

### Key Performance Parameters Tested
| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air Flow Rate | $\dot{V}$ or $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, **CFM** | Quantifies total air delivered |
| Velocity Pressure | $P_v$ | $\text{Pa}$ (or inches of water, **in.wg**) | Used to compute velocity |
| Static Pressure | $P_s$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Resistance of ductwork |
| Total Pressure | $P_t$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Energy per unit volume of air |
| Air Changes per Hour | **ACH** | $\text{h}^{-1}$ | Ventilation adequacy |
| External Static Pressure | **ESP** | $\text{Pa}$ or **mm.wg** | Fan selection criterion |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Air Control Testing

The testing procedure aims to **verify, adjust, and balance (TAB)** the AHU and duct system against the design intent. The principal engineering objectives are:

1. **Quantitative Verification** — Confirm that the actual supply, return, and outdoor air flow rates match the design values within a tolerance of **$\pm 10\%$** (per ASHRAE / SMACNA guidelines).
2. **Qualitative Verification** — Ensure that air distribution is uniform, drafts are minimised, and the system is stable under varying load conditions.
3. **Functional Verification** — Validate that dampers, VAV (Variable Air Volume) boxes, and control valves modulate correctly in response to thermostat/thermostat signals.
4. **Leakage Quantification** — Identify and quantify duct leakage, which in unconditioned spaces must be **$\le 5\%$ of nominal flow** (SMACNA Seal Class A/B).
5. **Acoustic & Vibration Check** — Ensure fan and duct-borne noise is within permissible dB(A) limits.

## 2.2 Categories of Tests Performed

- **Pre-Commissioning Tests (Airside):** Duct leakage test (Duct Pressure Class), fan rotation, filter fitment, insulation inspection.
- **Performance Tests (In-Situ):** Air flow measurement at terminals, ESP measurement at AHU, fan curve verification.
- **Control Response Tests:** PID loop response, damper stroke verification, VAV box tracking.
- **Final Balancing:** Proportional balancing of branch ducts using traverse method.

## 2.3 Instruments Used in Air Control Testing

| Instrument | Function | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Pitot Tube** with Inclined Manometer | Measures velocity pressure in ducts | $\pm 1\%$ full scale |
| **Hot-Wire Anemometer** | Point velocity measurement at grilles/diffusers | $\pm 3\%$ of reading |
| **Vane Anemometer** (Rotating) | Average velocity at outlets | $\pm 5\%$ |
| **Capture Hood (Flow Hood)** | Total CFM at diffusers/grilles | $\pm 3\%$ |
| **U-tube / Inclined Manometer** | Static and total pressure | $\pm 0.5 \text{ Pa}$ |
| **Tachometer** | Fan RPM verification | $\pm 1 \text{ RPM}$ |
| **Clamp Meter / Watt-meter** | Fan motor power | $\pm 1\%$ |
| **Thermometer / Psychrometer** | Air temperature / RH | $\pm 0.5\,^\circ\text{C}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the relationship between velocity pressure, velocity, and flow rate. Memorise the constants **$4005$** (for $\text{ft/min}$ to in.wg) and **$1.29 \text{ kg/m}^3$** (air density at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$, sea level).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every equation you will need for a numerical question on AHU air control testing. **All constants are at standard air density $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and sea-level atmospheric pressure.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Volumetric flow rate = Area $\times$ Velocity | $Q: \text{m}^3/\text{s},\; A: \text{m}^2,\; V: \text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $P_v = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure (fundamental Bernoulli) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $P_t = P_s + P_v$ | Total = Static + Velocity | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 4 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_v}{\rho}} = \dfrac{P_v^{0.5}}{0.0509}$ | Velocity from velocity pressure (for $\rho = 1.20$) | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $V = 4005 \sqrt{P_v}$ | Imperial form (with $P_v$ in **in.wg**, $V$ in **fpm**) | $\text{fpm}$ |
| 6 | $Q = 60 \times A \times V$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$ conversion | — |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{CFM}} = A_{\text{ft}^2} \times V_{\text{fpm}}$ | Cubic Feet per Minute | CFM |
| 8 | $P_{\text{ESP}} = P_{s,\text{supply duct}} + P_{s,\text{return duct}}$ | External Static Pressure of AHU fan | $\text{Pa}$ or **mm.wg** |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Fan Law 1 (Flow varies directly with speed) | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Fan Law 2 (Pressure varies with square of speed) | — |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2}\right)^2$ | Fan Law (Affinity Law) — $P \propto Q^2$ along system curve | — |
| 12 | $\text{Leakage \%} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{leaked}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}} \times 100$ | Duct leakage rate | \% |
| 13 | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{supply}}}{V_{\text{room}}} \times 60$ | Air changes per hour (with $\dot{V}$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$) | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| 14 | $\eta_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot P_{\text{total}}}{1000 \cdot P_{\text{electrical}}}$ | Fan total efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $P_s = \Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Duct friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | $\text{Pa}$ |

> **Note on LaTeX Safety:** I have used $\vert\cdot\vert$ or simply written absolute values as "magnitude" in text to avoid breaking the markdown pipe `|` symbol in tables.

## 2.5 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why $P_v = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$?** This is the **dynamic pressure** term from Bernoulli's energy equation. When air is in motion, part of its total energy is kinetic.
- **Why traverse the duct?** A duct cross-section has a **non-uniform velocity profile** (high at centre, low near walls due to boundary layer). A log-Tchebycheff or equal-area traverse averages this out for an accurate reading.
- **Why balance from the branch closest to the fan?** This is the **"proportional balancing"** philosophy — start with the index run (longest/restrictive) and balance progressively to keep the system curve stable.
- **Why measure ESP at AHU?** The fan is selected by its design ESP. Actual measured ESP tells the engineer whether the duct resistance is higher (under-design fan selection, dirty filter) or lower (over-spec fan, open bypass) than intended.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are deliberately **exhaustive** for the KTU 2024 scheme. Every algebraic step is shown, with a verbal explanation of the engineering logic, so that a student can reproduce them in the answer script.

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity from Velocity Pressure

**Starting Point — Bernoulli's Equation (along a streamline, incompressible, steady flow):**

$$
P_t = P_s + \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}
$$

By definition, $P_t - P_s = P_v$. Therefore:

$$
P_v = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}
$$

Solving for $V$:

$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_v}{\rho}}
$$

**Substituting standard air density $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$:**

$$
V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_v}{1.20}} = \sqrt{1.667 \, P_v} = 1.291 \, P_v^{0.5}
$$

**Therefore, the working equation is:**

$$
V = 1.291 \, \sqrt{P_v} \quad [\text{with } V \text{ in m/s, } P_v \text{ in Pa}]
$$

**Imperial equivalent (ASHRAE form):** Using $\rho = 0.075 \text{ lb/ft}^3$ and unit conversions ($P_v$ in **in.wg**, $V$ in **fpm**), the constant evaluates to **$4005$**:

$$
V = 4005 \sqrt{P_v}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate from Duct Traverse

The pitot-traverse method divides the duct cross-section into **$N$ equal concentric rings** (log-Tchebycheff rule) and measures $P_v$ at each traverse point. The **average velocity** is computed as:

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C \sqrt{P_{v,i}}
$$

where $C$ is the pitot-tube calibration factor (typically $C \approx 0.84$ to $1.00$ for a standard pitot). For a perfectly aligned, calibrated pitot, $C = 1$.

**Total volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = A_{\text{duct}} \times V_{\text{avg}}
$$

**Numerical Example (typical KTU problem type):**

> A rectangular duct of cross-section $600 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm}$ is traversed using 6 equal-area points. The measured velocity pressures (in $\text{Pa}$) are: $12, 18, 22, 25, 19, 14$. The pitot constant is $C = 0.98$. Determine the flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$.

**Step 1 — Compute area:**
$$
A = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert each $P_v$ to local velocity:**
$$
V_i = C \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{v,i}}{\rho}} = 0.98 \sqrt{1.667 \, P_{v,i}} = 0.98 \times 1.291 \sqrt{P_{v,i}}
$$

$$
V_i = 1.265 \sqrt{P_{v,i}}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= 1.265 \sqrt{12} = 1.265 \times 3.464 = 4.382 \text{ m/s} \\
V_2 &= 1.265 \sqrt{18} = 1.265 \times 4.243 = 5.367 \text{ m/s} \\
V_3 &= 1.265 \sqrt{22} = 1.265 \times 4.690 = 5.933 \text{ m/s} \\
V_4 &= 1.265 \sqrt{25} = 1.265 \times 5.000 = 6.325 \text{ m/s} \\
V_5 &= 1.265 \sqrt{19} = 1.265 \times 4.359 = 5.514 \text{ m/s} \\
V_6 &= 1.265 \sqrt{14} = 1.265 \times 3.742 = 4.734 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute mean velocity:**
$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{4.382 + 5.367 + 5.933 + 6.325 + 5.514 + 4.734}{6} = \dfrac{32.255}{6} = 5.376 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute flow rate:**
$$
Q = 0.24 \times 5.376 = 1.290 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$:**
$$
Q = 1.290 \times 3600 = 4645 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This 6-point traverse typically uses the **log-Tchebycheff spacing** (point distances from the duct wall: $0.043D, 0.293D, 0.5D, 0.707D, 0.866D, 0.957D$ where $D$ is the equivalent diameter), which gives a **statistically accurate mean** even for highly turbulent or skewed profiles.

## 3.3 Derivation: Leakage Rate Calculation

**Duct leakage class** is determined by pressuring the duct to a test pressure (e.g., $250 \text{ Pa}$ for low-pressure residential systems) and measuring the air volume lost over a fixed time.

**Leakage flow rate:**
$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V_{\text{added}}}{t_{\text{test}}}
$$

where $V_{\text{added}}$ is the volume of air that must be injected to maintain the test pressure over time $t_{\text{test}}$.

**Leakage class (per SMACNA):**
$$
L = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{0.65}}{A_{\text{duct surface}}}
$$

> Units: $L$ in $\text{cfm/100 ft}^2$ of duct surface area at test pressure $P$ in **in.wg**.

**Percentage leakage criterion:**
$$
\% \text{Leakage} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_{\text{design}}} \times 100
$$

**Acceptance criterion:** For sealed Class A ducts, leakage $\le 1\%$; for unsealed Class C, $\le 5\%$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Fan Affinity Laws (Used During Speed Trim Adjustments)

If a fan's speed is changed (e.g., using a **VFD** to meet design airflow), the new flow, pressure, and power scale as:

$$
\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \quad ; \quad \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \quad ; \quad \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A fan delivers $Q_1 = 8000 \text{ CFM}$ at $P_1 = 600 \text{ Pa}$ and $N_1 = 1450 \text{ RPM}$. To reduce flow to $Q_2 = 6000 \text{ CFM}$, what is the new speed and new pressure?

**Step 1 — Compute speed ratio:**
$$
\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{6000}{8000} = 0.75
$$

**Step 2 — New speed:**
$$
N_2 = 0.75 \times 1450 = 1087.5 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — New pressure:**
$$
P_2 = 0.75^2 \times 600 = 0.5625 \times 600 = 337.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Power reduction:**
$$
W_2 = 0.75^3 \times W_1 = 0.4219 \times W_1 \Rightarrow \text{Energy saving} \approx 58\%
$$

> **Engineering Note:** This 58 % energy saving is **why VFD-based airflow control** is a key component of modern AHU testing and energy management — directly aligned with **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, India 2017)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air Control Testing Workflow

The following **Mermaid flow diagram** illustrates the end-to-end testing procedure from instrument selection to final commissioning report.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Design Documents] --> A1[Pre-Commissioning Review]
    A1 --> A2{Ductwork Class Verified?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Rectify Joints and Sealing]
    A3 --> A2
    A2 -- Yes --> B1[Leakage Test at Test Pressure]
    B1 --> B2{Leakage within Class A Limit 1 percent?}
    B2 -- No --> B3[Re-Seal and Re-Test]
    B3 --> B1
    B2 -- Yes --> C1[AHU Fan Rotation Check]
    C1 --> C2[Filter and Coil Inspection]
    C2 --> C3[Insert Pitot Tube at Traverse Plane]
    C3 --> C4[Measure Static and Velocity Pressure]
    C4 --> C5[Compute Average Velocity and Flow Q]
    C5 --> D1{Design Q Match within 10 percent?}
    D1 -- No --> D2[Adjust VFD or Damper]
    D2 --> C3
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Proportional Balancing of Branches]
    E1 --> E2[Measure ESP at AHU Fan Inlet and Outlet]
    E2 --> E3{ESP within Design Range?}
    E3 -- No --> F1[Trim Impeller or Adjust Pulley]
    F1 --> C3
    E3 -- Yes --> G1[Terminal Device Flow Verification]
    G1 --> G2[Capture Hood at Each Diffuser]
    G2 --> H1[Control Loop Response Test]
    H1 --> H2[PID and Damper Stroke Check]
    H2 --> I1[Compile TAB Report]
    I1 --> I2[Client Handover and Sign Off]

    style A0 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Instrument Interconnection at AHU Test Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AHU[Air Handling Unit] -- Supply Duct --> TP1[Test Plane 1 Pitot Manometer]
    AHU -- Return Duct --> TP2[Test Plane 2 Pitot Manometer]
    AHU -- Outside Air --> TP3[Test Plane 3 Pitot Manometer]
    TP1 --> CALC[Flow Calculation Q equals A times V avg]
    TP2 --> CALC
    TP3 --> CALC
    CALC --> ESP[ESP equals PS supply plus PS return]
    ESP --> FAN[Fan Performance Verification]
    FAN --> VFD[VFD Speed Adjustment]
    VFD --> AHU
    DIFF[Supply Diffusers] --> HOOD[Capture Hood Anemometer]
    HOOD --> BAL[Branch Balancing Module]
    BAL --> VFD

    style AHU fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CALC fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VFD fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Duct Traverse Point Layout (Log-Tchebycheff)

The schematic below shows the **6-point traverse** across a circular duct of diameter $D$. Each point is at a specific non-dimensional radial distance from the wall, following the **log-Tchebycheff** rule that minimises sampling error for turbulent flow.

```
Circular Duct Cross-Section (Diameter = D)

        +----------------------+
        |                      |
        |    (P1)        (P6)  |   P1 = 0.043 D from wall
        |                      |   P2 = 0.293 D
        |  (P2)     (P5)       |   P3 = 0.500 D (center)
        |                      |   P4 = 0.707 D
        |     (P3, P4)         |   P5 = 0.866 D
        |                      |   P6 = 0.957 D
        |    (P4)        (P3)  |
        |                      |
        |  (P5)     (P2)       |
        |                      |
        |    (P6)        (P1)  |
        |                      |
        +----------------------+

  - Two perpendicular diameters (90 deg apart) are used.
  - 6 points on each diameter = 12 points total (per ASHRAE 111).
  - Or 6 points per single diameter (simplified KTU exam version).
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Air Control Test Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Isolate AHU and Verify Safety Lockout] --> step2[Step 2: Inspect Ductwork for Visible Defects]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Perform Duct Leakage Test]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Energise Fan and Verify Rotation]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Traverse Each Main Duct with Pitot Tube]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Record P_s and P_v at AHU Inlet and Outlet]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Calculate ESP and Compare with Design]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Measure Flow at Each Terminal with Capture Hood]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Adjust Balancing Dampers Iteratively]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Verify Damper Control Response to Thermostat]
    step10 --> step11[Step 11: Final Tabulation and Sign Off]

    style step1 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333
    style step11 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define 'External Static Pressure' (ESP) of an AHU fan and explain its significance in air control testing.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **External Static Pressure (ESP)** of an AHU fan is the total static pressure the fan must overcome to deliver the design air volume through the connected ductwork. It is computed as:
>
> $$\text{ESP} = P_{s,\text{supply duct}} + P_{s,\text{return duct}}$$
>
> (excluding the AHU internal components such as coils, filters, dampers, which are accounted for internally by the manufacturer).
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** ESP is the **primary selection criterion** for the fan. During testing, the measured ESP is compared against the design ESP. A higher-than-design ESP indicates **duct under-sizing, dirty filters, or closed dampers**, while a lower ESP indicates **over-sized ductwork or open bypass**. **A deviation beyond $\pm 10\%$** necessitates fan re-selection or system modification.
>
> **Unit (1 mark):** SI unit is **$\text{Pa}$**; in Indian practice, **mm of water gauge (mm.wg)** is also common ($1 \text{ mm.wg} \approx 9.81 \text{ Pa}$).

### **Q2. List any three instruments used for measuring air flow in HVAC ductwork and state the principle of one of them.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Three instruments are:
> 1. **Pitot-static tube with inclined manometer** (1 mark)
> 2. **Vane anemometer** (1 mark)
> 3. **Capture hood (flow hood)** (1 mark)
>
> **Principle of Pitot Tube:** It measures the **velocity pressure** $P_v$ of the air stream, which is the difference between the total pressure $P_t$ (measured at the impact opening facing the flow) and the static pressure $P_s$ (measured at the side openings perpendicular to the flow). The velocity is then computed as $V = \sqrt{2 P_v / \rho}$, and the flow rate is $Q = A \times V$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — With Sub-Parts

**Q.A.** A centrifugal AHU fan is rated to deliver **$12\,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$** at an **ESP of $750 \text{ Pa}$** when running at **$1450 \text{ RPM}$**. During a field air control test, the measured values are:
- Duct dimensions: **$800 \text{ mm} \times 500 \text{ mm}$**
- Traverse readings (Pa) at 6 points: **$18, 24, 28, 30, 26, 20$**
- Static pressure at supply side: **$+380 \text{ Pa}$**
- Static pressure at return side: **$-340 \text{ Pa}$**
- Pitot calibration constant $C = 0.98$, air density $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Answer the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the actual volumetric flow rate delivered, compare it with the design value, and compute the percentage deviation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the actual ESP and check whether the fan is operating within the design range. If the flow is to be reduced to the design value using a VFD, calculate the new RPM and new ESP using fan affinity laws.

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Actual Flow Rate Calculation

**Step 1 — Duct cross-sectional area:**
$$
A = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at each traverse point:**
$$
V_i = C \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{v,i}}{\rho}} = 0.98 \times 1.291 \times \sqrt{P_{v,i}} = 1.265 \sqrt{P_{v,i}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= 1.265 \sqrt{18} = 1.265 \times 4.243 = 5.367 \text{ m/s} \\
V_2 &= 1.265 \sqrt{24} = 1.265 \times 4.899 = 6.197 \text{ m/s} \\
V_3 &= 1.265 \sqrt{28} = 1.265 \times 5.292 = 6.694 \text{ m/s} \\
V_4 &= 1.265 \sqrt{30} = 1.265 \times 5.477 = 6.929 \text{ m/s} \\
V_5 &= 1.265 \sqrt{26} = 1.265 \times 5.099 = 6.450 \text{ m/s} \\
V_6 &= 1.265 \sqrt{20} = 1.265 \times 4.472 = 5.657 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean velocity:**
$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{5.367 + 6.197 + 6.694 + 6.929 + 6.450 + 5.657}{6} = \dfrac{37.294}{6} = 6.216 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual volumetric flow:**
$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = A \times V_{\text{avg}} = 0.40 \times 6.216 = 2.486 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$:**
$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = 2.486 \times 3600 = 8951 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 6 — Deviation from design:**
$$
\% \text{Deviation} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{design}}}{Q_{\text{design}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{8951 - 12000}{12000} \times 100 = -25.4\%
$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** The fan is delivering only $74.6\%$ of the design flow — a serious shortfall that demands corrective action via VFD, damper adjustment, or pulley change.

### Part (b) — ESP and Fan Affinity Calculations

**Step 1 — Measured ESP:**
$$
\text{ESP}_{\text{actual}} = P_{s,\text{supply}} - P_{s,\text{return}} = 380 - (-340) = 380 + 340 = 720 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with design:**
$$
\text{Design ESP} = 750 \text{ Pa}, \quad \text{Actual} = 720 \text{ Pa} \Rightarrow \text{Deviation} = \dfrac{720 - 750}{750} \times 100 = -4.0\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** ESP is within $\pm 10\%$ — **acceptable**. The problem is **low flow at near-design pressure**, indicating the fan is operating **away from its design point** (likely the system resistance is higher than expected, perhaps due to closed balancing dampers).

**Step 3 — VFD Speed Trim:**

To deliver design flow $Q_{\text{design}} = 12\,000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ using Fan Law 1:
$$
\dfrac{Q_{\text{new}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{new}}}{N_{\text{actual}}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 1450 \times \dfrac{12\,000}{8951} = 1450 \times 1.3405 = 1943.7 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — New ESP at the new operating point** (Note: when changing speed, the system curve remains the same, so we must compute the *new pressure required to push design flow through the same system resistance*). The system resistance $R$ is constant, and $P = R Q^2$:

$$
\dfrac{P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{actual}}} = \left(\dfrac{Q_{\text{new}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{12\,000}{8951}\right)^2 = 1.797
$$

$$
P_{\text{new}} = 1.797 \times 720 = 1294 \text{ Pa}
$$

> **Final Comment (1 mark):** The new ESP ($1294 \text{ Pa}$) significantly exceeds the design ESP ($750 \text{ Pa}$) because the duct system has higher resistance than the original design. This implies the **fan is undersized for the system resistance**, and a VFD speed-up to $1944 \text{ RPM}$ will likely overload the motor. The proper engineering recommendation is to **reduce system resistance** (open balancing dampers, clean filters, enlarge ducts) rather than increase fan speed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** Describe the procedure for **Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing (TAB)** the air side of an Air Handling System. Include a note on duct leakage testing and the role of the **ASHRAE Standard 111** in field measurement.

#### **Model Answer Outline (14 marks):**

**(a) TAB Procedure [7 marks]**

1. **Pre-TAB Documentation Review** (1 mark): Verify drawings, equipment schedules, control diagrams.
2. **Pre-Inspection** (1 mark): Visual check of ductwork, insulation, supports, damper access, and clean filters.
3. **Leakage Test** (1 mark): Pressurise representative duct section to design test pressure and quantify leakage.
4. **Fan Performance Verification** (1 mark): Measure $P_{s,\text{supply}}, P_{s,\text{return}}$, RPM, motor current, and plot on the fan curve.
5. **Main Duct Traverse** (1 mark): Pitot traverse of all main supply, return, and outside-air ducts.
6. **Branch Balancing** (1 mark): Iterative adjustment of balancing dampers starting from the most restrictive (index) branch.
7. **Terminal Air Measurement and Final Report** (1 mark): Capture hood readings at every grille/diffuser; compile a TAB report with $\pm 10\%$ tolerance check.

**(b) Duct Leakage Testing and ASHRAE 111 [7 marks]**

- **Duct Leakage Test Procedure** (3 marks): Seal all openings, attach a calibrated test fan (e.g., Duct Blaster), pressurise to test pressure ($250 \text{ Pa}$ for low-pressure, $1000 \text{ Pa}$ for medium-pressure), record injected air volume over time. Compute leakage class per SMACNA ($L = Q_L \cdot P^{0.65}/A_{\text{duct}}$).
- **Acceptance Criteria** (2 marks): SMACNA Seal Class A (sealed): $\le 1\%$; Class B: $\le 3\%$; Class C: $\le 5\%$. For healthcare and cleanroom: $\le 1\%$.
- **ASHRAE Standard 111** (2 marks): Defines **"Practices for Measurement, Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing of Building HVAC Systems."** Specifies the **equal-area log-Tchebycheff traverse** method, the use of calibrated instruments, the $\pm 10\%$ tolerance band, and the format of the final TAB report. It is the **standard reference** in the Indian consulting industry for AHU acceptance testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating **velocity pressure** with **static pressure** — they are *not* the same. $P_v$ is *dynamic*, $P_s$ is *potential*, and $P_t = P_s + P_v$. Many students incorrectly use $P_s$ in the formula $V = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **pitot calibration constant $C$** — it is *not* 1.00 for a non-ideal probe; the answer is penalised if the $C$ factor is omitted.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting units consistently — mixing **mm.wg** and **Pa** in the same equation. Use $1 \text{ mm.wg} = 9.81 \text{ Pa}$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In **fan affinity law** problems, students often wrongly assume the *system curve is constant* when speed changes. Correct interpretation: **system resistance is constant**, so $P \propto Q^2$ at the new speed.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Quoting a $\pm 10\%$ tolerance without specifying that this is per **ASHRAE / SMACNA**, not an arbitrary KTU rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems**

- [x] **Air Control Testing = TAB** (Testing, Adjusting, Balancing) of AHU and ductwork.
- [x] **Primary Goal:** Verify that **$Q$, $P_s$, $P_v$, $P_t$, and ESP** match design within **$\pm 10\%$**.
- [x] **Fundamental equation:** $Q = A \cdot V$, where $V = \sqrt{2 P_v / \rho}$.
- [x] **Pitot traverse** uses **log-Tchebycheff** or **equal-area** points (typically 6 or 12) for accurate mean velocity.
- [x] **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$, sea level.
- [x] **Conversion constants:** $V = 4005 \sqrt{P_v}$ (fpm, in.wg); $1 \text{ in.wg} = 249 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ CFM} = 1.699 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$.
- [x] **External Static Pressure (ESP):** $\text{ESP} = P_{s,\text{supply}} + \vert P_{s,\text{return}} \vert$.
- [x] **Fan Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$; $P \propto N^2$; $W \propto N^3$. System resistance $\Rightarrow P \propto Q^2$.
- [x] **Duct Leakage Class** (SMACNA): $L = Q_L \cdot P^{0.65}/A_{\text{duct}}$ in $\text{cfm/100 ft}^2$.
- [x] **ASHRAE Standard 111** governs field measurement; **SMACNA** governs leakage classification; **ECBC 2017** (India) governs energy compliance.
- [x] **Instruments to remember:** Pitot tube + inclined manometer, capture hood, vane/hot-wire anemometer, tachometer, U-tube manometer.
- [x] **Balancing philosophy:** Start from the **index run** (longest/restrictive), work progressively toward the AHU.
- [x] **Acceptance Tolerance:** **$\pm 10\%$** of design for flow; **$\pm 5\%$** for pressure; **$\le 5\%$** leakage for Class B/C ducts.
- [x] **Energy Hook:** A well-balanced system can save **20–40 %** of fan energy — directly relevant to **ECBC-compliant green buildings**.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Candidates:** Always cite the **standard reference** (ASHRAE 111, SMACNA, ISHRAE, ECBC) in long answers, draw a **clearly labelled schematic** in TAB questions, and present numerical solutions in a **tabular format** with units. This signals professional competence to the examiner and routinely gains **1–2 grace marks.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls (Thermostats, Humidistat, Control Dampers, Pressure Cut Outs, Relays)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls (Thermostats, Humidistat, Control Dampers, Pressure Cut Outs, Relays) # Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Air Conditioning Control** refers to the integrated network of sensing, decision-making, and actuating devices that regulate the thermodynamic and psychrometric state of air within a conditioned space to maintain prescribed parameters of **temperature**, **humidity**, **cleanliness**, and **air motion**. The five foundational control elements mandated under KTU Module 3 are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus-Mandated Common Controls (Module 3)** > 1. **Thermostats** – Temperature-sensing control devices > 2. **Humidistats** – Humidity-sensing control devices > 3. **Control Dampers** – Airflow modulation devices > 4. **Pressure Cut-Outs** – Safety pressure-sensing switches > 5. **Relays** – Electromagnetic switching/amplifying devices ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The HVAC "Nervous System" Imagine your body as a building, and your **brain** as the central controller. The five common controls function exactly like the body's nervous system: | Human Body Analog | HVAC Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Skin (temperature) | **Thermostat** | Senses deviation from setpoint | | Skin (moisture) | **Humidistat** | Senses humidity deviation | | Muscles (blood flow) | **Control Dampers** | Modulates the working fluid (air) | | Pain reflex | **Pressure Cut-Out** | Safety shutdown on extremes | | Spinal cord neurons | **Relays** | Low-power signal → high-power action | > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Control Loop Structure:** Every HVAC control operates on the closed-loop principle: > **SENSOR** → **CONTROLLER** → **ACTUATOR** → **PROCESS** → (back to SENSOR) ### 1.3 Why These Controls Are Indispensable A modern HVAC system without these controls is a **runaway thermodynamic system** — energy-inefficient, unsafe, and unable to deliver occupant comfort. The American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard **55** and Indian Standard **IS 1391** both require precise control of indoor thermal conditions for **human comfort and process integrity**. Standard Performance Metrics (bolded for emphasis): - **Operative Temperature: 20°C – 26°C** (for comfort) - **Relative Humidity: 30% – 60%** (for comfort) - **CO₂ Level: < 1000 ppm** (for IAQ) - **Duct Static Pressure: 25 mm – 50 mm w.g.** (for ducted systems) # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermostats — The Temperature Sentinel A thermostat is a **temperature-actuated switch** that opens/closes an electrical circuit in response to a change in temperature, comparing it to a predetermined setpoint. ### 2.1.1 Types of Thermostats (KTU Board-Relevant) **A) Bimetallic Strip Thermostat (Most common in residential split ACs)** Two metals with **different coefficients of linear expansion** ($\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$) are bonded. Temperature change causes differential expansion, producing mechanical deflection. **Deflection Equation of a Bimetallic Strip:** The free-end deflection $\delta$ of a bimetallic strip of length $L$, thickness $t$, for a temperature change $\Delta T$ is: $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ Where $K$ is a geometry constant (typically **0.5 to 0.75** depending on end conditions). **B) Gas-Filled Thermostat (Industrial grade)** Uses the **Ideal Gas Law**: $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ When temperature rises at constant volume, pressure rises proportionally → moves a diaphragm → opens/closes contacts. **C) Electronic Thermostat (Modern DDC systems)** Uses **thermistors** (resistance varies with temperature) or **RTDs** (Resistance Temperature Detectors). The governing equation is the **Steinhart-Hart equation** for an NTC thermistor: $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot [\ln(R)]^3$$ Where $T$ is in Kelvin, $R$ is resistance in ohms, and $A, B, C$ are calibration constants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** For part-load modulation, modern thermostats use **Proportional (P)**, **Proportional-Integral (PI)**, or **PID control algorithms** rather than simple on/off hysteresis. --- ## 2.2 Humidistats — The Moisture Sentinel A humidistat is a **humidity-actuated control device** that maintains relative humidity by switching humidification/dehumidification equipment. ### 2.2.1 Working Principle Most humidistats use **human hair, nylon, or animal membrane** as the sensing element. These materials **elongate when humidity increases** because they absorb moisture. The elongation is mechanically amplified to operate a switch. **Empirical Elongation Equation:** $$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta \phi$$ Where $\beta$ is the moisture expansion coefficient, $\Delta \phi$ is the change in RH, $L_0$ is the original length. ### 2.2.2 Dew-Point vs. Relative-Humidity Sensing > [!NOTE] > **RH = (Partial Pressure of Water Vapour / Saturation Pressure at Same Temperature) × 100%** Electronic humidistats use **capacitive polymer sensors** or **chilled-mirror dew-point sensors**. The chilled-mirror operates on the principle that a mirror cooled to the **dew-point temperature** $T_{dp}$ will form condensation, detected optically. $$\phi = \frac{p_v(T_{dp})}{p_{vs}(T_{room})} \times 100\%$$ --- ## 2.3 Control Dampers — The Airflow Regulators **Control dampers** are louvered devices installed in ductwork to **modulate the volume flow rate** of air by varying the flow cross-sectional area. ### 2.3.1 Types (KTU Focus) | Type | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **Opposed Blade** | Adjacent blades rotate in opposite directions | Good mixing downstream | | **Parallel Blade** | All blades rotate in same direction | Linear flow characteristic | | **Butterfly Damper** | Single disc rotating about a shaft | On/off service | | **Inlet Vane Damper** | Pre-rotation of air entering fan | Centrifugal fan control | | **Variable Volume (VAV) Box** | Pressure-independent damper | Variable Air Volume systems | ### 2.3.2 Governing Flow Equation The volumetric flow rate through a damper is governed by the orifice-like equation: $$Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_{air}}}$$ Where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient (typically **0.6 – 0.65** for sharp-edged dampers), $A_{eff}$ is the effective open area, $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the damper, $\rho_{air}$ is air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C). --- ## 2.4 Pressure Cut-Outs — The Safety Sentinel A **pressure cut-out** is a safety switch that **opens the control circuit** when system pressure exceeds (high-pressure cut-out) or falls below (low-pressure cut-out) a preset value. ### 2.4.1 Operating Principle Uses a **bellows or diaphragm** sensing element connected to the refrigerant line. When pressure force exceeds the spring pre-load: $$F_{pressure} = P \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$ $$F_{pressure} \geq F_{spring} \rightarrow \text{Switch Trips}$$ ### 2.4.2 Critical KTU Application Points > [!WARNING] > **Mandatory Placement (Refrigerant Circuit):** > - **High-Pressure Cut-Out (HPCO):** Compressor discharge line (cuts off at typically **24 – 28 bar** for R-410A) > - **Low-Pressure Cut-Out (LPCO):** Compressor suction line (cuts off at typically **2 – 3 bar** for R-410A) > - **Differential Setting:** Cut-out and cut-in must differ by a fixed value to prevent short-cycling (typically **3 – 5 bar** differential) ### 2.4.3 Duct Static Pressure Sensor For VAV systems, a **differential pressure transducer** measures $\Delta P$ across a pitot tube or across a filter bank to maintain duct pressure at the design value of approximately **25 mm w.g.** (≈ **245 Pa**). --- ## 2.5 Relays — The Electrical Muscle A **relay** is an **electromagnetic switch** that uses a low-power control signal to switch a higher-power load. In HVAC, relays are essential because thermostats and humidistats carry currents of only **milliamps**, while compressors and contactors require **amperes**. ### 2.5.1 Operating Equation The electromagnetic force pulling the armature is: $$F_{mag} = \frac{(N \cdot I)^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$ Where $N$ is turns, $I$ is coil current, $\mu_0$ is permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**, $A$ is pole-face area, $g$ is air-gap length. The relay operates when: $$F_{mag} \geq F_{spring}$$ ### 2.5.2 KTU-Relevant Relay Types | Type | Construction | Application | |---|---|---| | **Electromagnetic Relay (EMR)** | Coil + armature + contacts | General switching | | **Solid State Relay (SSR)** | Triac/SCR + optocoupler | Silent, fast switching for heaters | | **Contactor** | Heavy-duty EMR | Motors, compressors | | **Time-Delay Relay** | Pneumatic/electronic timer | Sequential start/stop of chillers | | **Step Relay** | Latching, multi-position | Multi-stage heating/cooling | --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | Bimetallic Strip Deflection | $\delta = K \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2 / t$ | m | Few mm | | Ideal Gas Pressure-Temp | $P_2/P_1 = T_2/T_1$ (at const V) | Pa/K | — | | Relative Humidity | $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}) \times 100\%$ | % | 30–60 % | | Damper Flow | $Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | m³/s | — | | Pressure Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | — | | Relay Magnetic Force | $F = (NI)^2 \mu_0 A / (2g^2)$ | N | — | | Wet-Bulb Approx. | $T_{dp} \approx T_{wb} - (100 - \phi)/5$ | °C | — | | Heat Transfer Through Wall | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | — | | Sensible Heat | $Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | — | | Latent Heat | $Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | — | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility These five controls together form the **automation backbone** of: - **Smart Buildings** (IoT-integrated BMS using BACnet/Modbus protocols) - **Cleanrooms** (Semiconductor/pharma — temperature ±0.1°C, RH ±1%) - **Hospital OTs** (Positive pressure, HEPA-filtered air with backup pressure sensors) - **Data Centers** (ASHRAE TC 9.9 — precision cooling with multi-sensor redundancy) # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bimetallic Thermostat Deflection ### Given: - Bimetallic strip length $L$ = **100 mm = 0.1 m** - Thickness $t$ = **1 mm = 0.001 m** - High-expansion metal: Brass, $\alpha_1$ = **19 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** - Low-expansion metal: Invar, $\alpha_2$ = **1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** - Temperature change $\Delta T$ = **30°C** - Geometry constant $K$ = **0.75** (cantilever, end-free) ### Find: Free-end deflection $\delta$ in mm. ### Step 1 — Write the governing equation $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute differential expansion coefficient $$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} = 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute all values $$\delta = 0.75 \cdot \frac{(17.8 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (30) \cdot (0.1)^2}{0.001}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerator evaluation $$\text{Numerator} = 0.75 \cdot 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 30 \cdot 0.01$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.3$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 5.34 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 4.005 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ ### Step 5 — Divide by $t$ $$\delta = \frac{4.005 \times 10^{-6}}{0.001} = 4.005 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ ### Step 6 — Convert to mm $$\boxed{\delta = 4.005 \text{ mm}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 4 mm deflection is sufficient to actuate a microswitch with a typical trip travel of 1–2 mm, confirming the design is viable. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Humidity from Wet-Bulb/Dry-Bulb Readings ### Given: - Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$ = **30°C** - Wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$ = **22°C** - Atmospheric pressure $P$ = **101.325 kPa** ### Find: Relative humidity $\phi$ and dew-point temperature $T_{dp}$. ### Step 1 — Saturation pressure at $T_{db}$ = 30°C (from steam tables) $$p_{vs}(30°C) = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 2 — Saturation pressure at $T_{wb}$ = 22°C $$p_{vs}(22°C) = 2.645 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Carrier's equation (approximate) for psychrometric calculation The actual vapour pressure $p_v$ is approximately: $$p_v = p_{vs}(T_{wb}) - (P - p_{vs}(T_{wb})) \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \cdot 0.000367$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $$p_v = 2.645 - (101.325 - 2.645) \cdot (30 - 22) \cdot 0.000367$$ $$p_v = 2.645 - (98.68) \cdot (8) \cdot 0.000367$$ $$p_v = 2.645 - 0.2897$$ $$p_v = 2.355 \text{ kPa}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Relative Humidity $$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\% = \frac{2.355}{4.246} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 55.5\%}$$ ### Step 6 — Dew-Point Temperature Since $p_v = p_{vs}(T_{dp})$, look up which temperature gives $p_{vs} = 2.355$ kPa. From steam tables, this corresponds to approximately: $$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 19.7°C}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Damper Flow Rate & Fan Power Saving ### Given: - Circular duct diameter $D$ = **0.4 m** - Damper fully open area $A_{max} = \pi D^2/4$ = **0.1257 m²** - Discharge coefficient $C_d$ = **0.62** - Pressure drop $\Delta P$ = **250 Pa** (full open) - Air density $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³** ### Find: (a) Maximum volumetric flow rate. (b) When damper closes to 50% area, how does $\Delta P$ change for same flow? (c) Compare fan power. ### Part (a): Maximum Flow $$Q_{max} = C_d \cdot A_{max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ $$Q_{max} = 0.62 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 250}{1.2}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{500}{1.2}} = \sqrt{416.67} = 20.41 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q_{max} = 0.62 \cdot 0.1257 \cdot 20.41 = 1.591 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ ### Part (b): Partial Closing at Same Flow If effective area becomes $A_{eff} = 0.5 \cdot A_{max}$, then for the **same $Q$**, the new pressure drop $\Delta P_2$ must satisfy: $$Q = C_d \cdot (0.5 \cdot A_{max}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho}} = \frac{Q}{0.5 \cdot C_d \cdot A_{max}} = 2 \cdot \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot A_{max}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$\frac{2 \Delta P_2}{\rho} = 4 \cdot \frac{2 \Delta P_1}{\rho}$$ $$\Delta P_2 = 4 \cdot \Delta P_1 = 4 \cdot 250 = 1000 \text{ Pa}$$ ### Part (c): Fan Power Comparison Assuming fan affinity law: $P_{fan} \propto \Delta P$ for the same flow rate: $$\frac{P_{50\%}}{P_{100\%}} = \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = 4$$ **Important Note:** Although pressure drop rises 4×, real fan systems show that **part-load fan power actually decreases** because the system curve flattens. Damper control is inefficient compared to **VFD control** — a classic KTU comparison point. > [!NOTE] > **Fan Affinity Laws (Quick Reference):** > - $\dot{V}_2/\dot{V}_1 = N_2/N_1$ (flow ∝ speed) > - $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ > - $P_{fan,2} / P_{fan,1} = (N_2/N_1)^3$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Cut-Out Trip Threshold ### Given: - Refrigerant: R-410A - Diaphragm effective area $A$ = **5 cm² = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m²** - Spring constant $k$ = **500 N/m** - Spring pre-compression $x_0$ = **2 cm = 0.02 m** - Safety factor $n$ = **1.2** (over-pressure trip margin) ### Find: Cut-out trip pressure. ### Step 1 — Spring pre-load force $$F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0 = 500 \cdot 0.02 = 10 \text{ N}$$ ### Step 2 — Required pressure force (with safety factor) $$F_{trip} = n \cdot F_{spring} = 1.2 \cdot 10 = 12 \text{ N}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for trip pressure $$P_{trip} = \frac{F_{trip}}{A} = \frac{12}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\boxed{P_{trip} = 24{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 2.4 \text{ bar (gauge)}}$$ This falls within the typical R-410A high-pressure cut-out range of **24 – 28 bar**, confirming a realistic design. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Control Devices | Application | Recommended Sensor | Cut-Out Setting | Relay Type | |---|---|---|---| | Window AC | Bimetallic thermostat | — | Mechanical relay | | Split AC (residential) | Electronic thermistor | HPCO: 28 bar | PCB-mounted SSR | | Chilled water plant | RTD (Pt100) + PI controller | LPCO: 2 bar | Contactor + overload | | Cleanroom | Thermistor + capacitive RH | Duct ΔP: 25 mm w.g. | Solid-state relay | | Cold storage | NTC thermistor | LPCO: 0.5 bar | Latching step relay | | AHU (Hospital) | Multi-sensor DDC | HEPA ΔP: 25 → 50 mm w.g. | Time-delay relay | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — HVAC Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conditioned Space
Temperature and Humidity] -->|Measured value| B[Sensor Layer
Thermostat, Humidistat] B -->|Low-power signal| C[Controller
PID Logic, DDC] C -->|Control pulse| D[Relay Bank
EMR, SSR, Contactor] D -->|High-power switching| E[Actuator Layer
Compressor, Heater, Damper Motor] E -->|Modulates| F[Process Variable
Refrigerant flow, Air flow] F -->|Affects| A P[Pressure Cut-Out
Safety Loop] -.->|Trip signal interrupts| D S[Setpoint Input
User or BMS] --> C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The solid arrows depict the **forward control loop** (Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process → Space). - The dotted line from the **Pressure Cut-Out** represents the **safety override**, which can disable the relay bank regardless of controller command. --- ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Control Device Selection for an AC Unit ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Choose Control Device] --> B{Control Parameter?} B -->|Temperature| C[Thermostat] B -->|Humidity| D[Humidistat] B -->|Airflow| E[Control Damper] B -->|Safety Pressure| F[Pressure Cut-Out] B -->|Electrical Switching| G[Relay] C --> C1{Application Scale?} C1 -->|Residential| C2[Bimetallic or Electronic] C1 -->|Industrial| C3[RTD or Thermistor with PID] D --> D1{Sensor Medium?} D1 -->|Hair/Nylon| D2[Mechanical Humidistat] D1 -->|Polymer Capacitor| D3[Electronic Humidistat] E --> E1{Flow Modulation Type?} E1 -->|Linear| E2[Parallel Blade Damper] E1 -->|Equal Percentage| E3[Opposed Blade Damper] F --> F1{Position in Circuit?} F1 -->|Discharge Line| F2[High Pressure Cut-Out] F1 -->|Suction Line| F3[Low Pressure Cut-Out] G --> G1{Switching Current?} G1 -->|< 5 A| G2[Solid State Relay] G1 -->|5 to 50 A| G3[Contactor Relay] ``` --- ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Sequential Starting of Chilled-Water Plant ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T as Thermostat participant R1 as Time-Delay Relay 1 participant C1 as Chiller Contactor participant PC as Pressure Cut-Out participant R2 as Time-Delay Relay 2 participant CP as Chilled Water Pump participant R3 as Time-Delay Relay 3 participant AHU as AHU Fan Note over T: Space temperature rises above setpoint T->>R1: Energize (after 30s delay) R1->>C1: Close chiller contactor C1->>PC: Chiller starts, pressure builds PC-->>C1: Pressure OK signal confirmed Note over C1: Compressor RUNNING C1->>R2: Trigger pump start (after 60s) R2->>CP: Energize chilled water pump CP->>R3: Trigger after 30s flow proven R3->>AHU: Energize AHU fan Note over AHU: Full system ON ``` This sequence prevents simultaneous high-inrush starting and protects against water-hammer. --- ## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Pressure Cut-Out Logic in Refrigerant Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Refrigerant Circuit C[Compressor] -->|Discharge| D1[High Pressure Switch HPCO] D1 -->|Trip at 28 bar| S1[Cut-Out Coil Trip] D1 -->|Pass through| Cd[Condenser] Cd --> R1[Receiver] R1 --> Ev[Expansion Valve] Ev -->|Suction| D2[Low Pressure Switch LPCO] D2 -->|Trip at 2 bar| S1 D2 -->|Pass through| Evap[Evaporator] Evap -->|Suction returns| C end S1 -.->|Open circuit| C Set[HPCO Cut-in 21 bar, LPCO Cut-in 4 bar] -.-> D1 Set -.-> D2 ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the working principle of a bimetallic thermostat with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** A bimetallic thermostat consists of two strips of metals having **different coefficients of thermal expansion** (e.g., brass and invar) bonded together throughout their length. When the temperature of the surroundings changes, the high-expansion metal (brass) expands more than the low-expansion metal (invar). This differential expansion causes the strip to **bend towards the invar side**. The free end of the strip is mechanically linked to an **electrical contact**. When the strip bends sufficiently due to temperature rise, it opens (or closes) the contacts, thereby switching the cooling/heating equipment ON or OFF. **Key Features:** - Simple, robust, and inexpensive - Used in window ACs and residential thermostats - Differential expansion coefficient governs sensitivity ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between a humidistat and a thermostat. State two applications of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Thermostat | Humidistat | |---|---|---| | **Sensing quantity** | Temperature | Relative humidity | | **Sensing element** | Bimetallic strip / thermistor | Hair / nylon / polymer | | **Output action** | Controls heating/cooling | Controls humidifier/dehumidifier | | **Setpoint range** | 15–30°C typical | 20–80% RH typical | **Applications of Thermostat:** (i) Window AC temperature control, (ii) Cold storage temperature regulation. **Applications of Humidistat:** (i) Hospital OT humidity control, (ii) Textile mill humidification. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Module Coverage **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** — *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of:** (i) Bimetallic thermostat (ii) Opposed-blade control damper **Model Solution:** **(i) Bimetallic Thermostat (3.5 marks):** Already covered in detail under Section 2.1. Key points to write: - Two metals with different $\alpha$ bonded together - Deflection $\delta = K \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2/t$ - Mechanical amplification → mercury switch or microswitch - Setpoint adjustment via screw changing spring pre-load - Differential gap prevents short-cycling **Sketch description (for board exam):** Show two horizontal layers labelled "Brass" and "Invar", fixed at one wall end, with the free end connected to a switch lever. Indicate bending direction when heated. **(ii) Opposed-Blade Control Damper (3.5 marks):** - Multiple rectangular blades mounted on a common shaft - **Adjacent blades rotate in opposite directions** (key feature) - Driven by a **damper actuator** (electric or pneumatic) - When closed: blades seal against each other and frame - When partially open: air is forced through tortuous path - Provides **equal-percentage flow characteristic** — ideal for mixing dampers in AHUs **Sketch description:** Show a duct cross-section with 4–5 parallel blades rotating in opposite directions, arrows indicating airflow. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A refrigerated cold storage has R-410A refrigerant. The high-pressure cut-out is set to trip at 28 bar. The diaphragm area is 6 cm². Calculate the spring force required for the trip mechanism. Also, explain the role of differential pressure setting.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units** $$P = 28 \text{ bar} = 28 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Convert area to SI units** $$A = 6 \text{ cm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute trip force** $$F_{trip} = P \cdot A = 2.8 \times 10^6 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{F_{trip} = 1680 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 4 — Spring design** To ensure reliability, the spring pre-load should be marginally less than $F_{trip}$. Choosing a safety factor of 0.85: $$F_{spring} = 0.85 \times 1680 = 1428 \text{ N}$$ **Role of differential pressure setting (3 marks for theory):** - **Differential** = (Cut-out pressure) − (Cut-in pressure) - Prevents **short-cycling** of compressor - Typical differential for R-410A: **3 – 5 bar** - Example: HPCO trips at 28 bar; cuts back in at 24 bar - Provides time for system to stabilize after a trip event --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternate Question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of an electromagnetic relay as used in air-conditioning control circuits. State the role of relays in starting a compressor.** **Model Solution:** **Construction (2 marks):** An electromagnetic relay consists of: 1. **Coil** (copper wire wound on a former) — energized by low-power control signal 2. **Iron core** — concentrates magnetic flux 3. **Armature** — movable iron part attracted when coil is energized 4. **Spring** — returns armature to rest position when coil de-energized 5. **Contacts** — Normally Open (NO), Normally Closed (NC), and Common (COM) **Operation (3 marks):** When the thermostat calls for cooling, it energizes the relay coil (typically 24 V AC). Current $I$ flows through the coil, producing magnetic flux $\Phi$: $$\Phi = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}}$$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is magnetic reluctance. The flux creates a force on the armature $F \propto \Phi^2$. When $F$ exceeds the spring force, the armature moves, closing the NO contacts. This closes a separate high-current circuit (e.g., 230 V AC) that energizes the compressor contactor. The compressor starts. **Role in compressor starting (2 marks):** - **Electrical isolation** between low-power control and high-power load - **Amplification** — milliamps of thermostat current → amperes of compressor current - **Multiple contact handling** — single relay can switch compressor + condenser fan + crankcase heater - **Safety interlock** — relay can be wired to trip on safety conditions **Sketch:** Show 24 V control loop (thermostat → relay coil) on the left, and 230 V power loop (contactor coil → compressor) on the right, with the relay's NO contacts bridging them. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A thermostat uses a bimetallic strip of length 80 mm and thickness 1.5 mm. The expansion coefficients are $\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$/°C and $\alpha_2 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/°C. Calculate the deflection for a 25°C temperature rise. Take K = 0.75.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ $t = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$ $\Delta T = 25°C$ $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (23 - 2) \times 10^{-6} = 21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$ $K = 0.75$ **Step 1 — Apply formula** $$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $$\delta = 0.75 \cdot \frac{(21 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (25) \cdot (0.08)^2}{0.0015}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator** $$\text{Numerator} = 0.75 \cdot 21 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25 \cdot 0.0064$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 21 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.16$$ $$= 0.75 \cdot 3.36 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Divide by $t$** $$\delta = \frac{2.52 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0015} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mm** $$\boxed{\delta = 1.68 \text{ mm}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** A 1.68 mm deflection is sufficient to actuate a typical microswitch. If a larger deflection is required, the designer may increase $L$, increase $\Delta \alpha$ (use better material pair), or use a longer cantilever. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Module 3 — Common Controls)** > > 1. **Confusing thermostat types:** Many students write "mercury thermostat" when the question asks for "bimetallic." Always name the **specific type** (bimetallic / gas-filled / electronic). > > 2. **Units inconsistency:** Pressure cut-out problems must use **SI units (Pa, m²)** throughout. Marks deducted if bar is mixed with cm² without conversion. > > 3. **Forgetting differential setting:** In pressure cut-out numerical problems, examiners specifically look for the **differential (hysteresis)** explanation. A correct trip pressure alone may fetch only partial marks. > > 4. **Relay direction wrong:** Writing "thermostat directly switches the compressor" loses **3–4 marks**. Always emphasize the relay's role as an **amplifier/isolator**. > > 5. **Damper flow equation:** Students forget that $C_d$ is **empirical** (not theoretical).
