Don't derive $C_d$ — accept it as given. > > 6. **No sketch:** Even a rough block diagram with labelled components earns **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Common Controls (Module 3)** > > **Thermostats:** > - [ ] Bimetallic strip uses differential expansion ($\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$) > - [ ] Deflection formula: $\delta = K \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^2/t$ > - [ ] Electronic versions use thermistors/RTDs with PID control > - [ ] Modern ACs use **inverter-driven** thermostats with continuous modulation > > **Humidistats:** > - [ ] Sensing element: hair, nylon, polymer > - [ ] Definition: $\phi = (p_v / p_{vs}) \times 100\%$ > - [ ] Dew-point temperature is where condensation begins > - [ ] Chilled-mirror sensors are the most accurate (used in labs) > > **Control Dampers:** > - [ ] Opposed-blade → equal-percentage (good for mixing) > - [ ] Parallel-blade → linear (good for throttling) > - [ ] Flow: $Q = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ > - [ ] VAV boxes are pressure-independent (constant $\Delta P$ across box) > > **Pressure Cut-Outs:** > - [ ] HPCO on discharge line (R-410A ≈ 24–28 bar) > - [ ] LPCO on suction line (R-410A ≈ 2–3 bar) > - [ ] Differential setting prevents short-cycling > - [ ] Trip force: $F = P \cdot A_{diaphragm}$ > > **Relays:** > - [ ] Low-power control → high-power load switching > - [ ] Magnetic force: $F \propto (NI)^2$ > - [ ] Types: EMR, SSR, Contactor, Time-delay, Step > - [ ] Essential for compressor starting and multi-stage control > > **Cross-Cutting Concepts:** > - [ ] All controls operate on closed-loop feedback principle > - [ ] Safety controls (pressure cut-outs) **override** comfort controls (thermostat) > - [ ] KTU board exams expect **numerical + descriptive mix** > - [ ] Always carry **3 significant figures** in numerical answers > - [ ] Sketch + Label = Guaranteed 2–3 extra marks in 14-mark questions

topics.title: Heating Systems in Automotives: Automotive Heaters, Types, and Engine Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT742 - HEATING VENTILATION AND AIR CONDITIONING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heating Systems in Automotives: Automotive Heaters, Types, and Engine Heating

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Automotive Heating Systems

An **Automotive Heating System** is an integrated thermal management subsystem within a vehicle that conditions the passenger cabin air and, in certain configurations, also warms up the engine and its vital fluids. In the context of KTU syllabus (PEMUT742), it specifically refers to the assembly of components — primarily the **heater core**, **blower fan**, **coolant circulation loop**, **thermostatic control valves**, and the **air-distribution ducting** — that utilize waste heat from the internal combustion (IC) engine coolant to deliver warm air to the cabin for occupant comfort and windshield defogging.

In modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), the same functional objective is achieved through **Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) heaters**, **heat pumps**, or **fuel-operated auxiliary heaters (FOAHs)** since there is no IC engine to provide waste heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 outcome expects students to *identify, classify, and evaluate* heating systems for automotive applications, including the heat-transfer physics of the heater core and the thermodynamic justification for engine warm-up strategies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radiator Inside Your Dashboard"

Imagine you are sitting in your living room on a chilly morning. To warm up, you have a small radiator hidden behind the sofa. Hot water from a central boiler (think of it as the car engine) circulates through that radiator, and a fan (the blower) pushes room air across the radiator's fins. The air heats up and gently flows out through grilles near your feet and the windshield.

That is exactly what an automotive heater does:

| Component | Home Analogy | Automotive Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Boiler | Furnace | Internal Combustion Engine |
| Hot water pipes | Plumbing | Coolant hoses |
| Small radiator | Heater | **Heater Core** (aluminium core with fins) |
| Fan | Ceiling fan | **Blower Motor + Squirrel-cage fan** |
| Thermostat knob | Wall thermostat | **HVAC control head / blend door actuator** |

The key insight is that **the heater core is a small secondary heat exchanger** that "steals" a fraction of the engine's waste heat (which would otherwise be dumped to the environment by the main radiator) and transfers it into the cabin.

## 1.3 Why is Heating Required in a Vehicle?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Objectives of an Automotive Heater:**
> 1. **Occupant Thermal Comfort:** Maintain cabin air temperature between **20 °C and 26 °C** (ASHRAE 55 adaptive comfort range).
> 2. **Defogging / Defrosting:** Prevent water-vapour condensation on the inner surface of the windshield (critical for driver visibility — a **safety** requirement mandated by ECE R21 / FMVSS 103).
> 3. **Engine Warm-up Assistance:** In cold climates, pre-heating coolant and lubricant reduces mechanical wear, lowers hydrocarbon (HC) emissions during cold start, and shortens the time to reach the catalytic-light-off temperature of ~**250 °C – 300 °C**.

## 1.4 The Physics of "Waste Heat Recovery"

A typical gasoline engine converts only about **25 % – 30 %** of the fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work at the crankshaft. The remaining **70 % – 75 %** is rejected as waste heat:

$$
Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{brake}} + Q_{\text{coolant}} + Q_{\text{exhaust}} + Q_{\text{radiation}}
$$

- Roughly **30 %** leaves via the **exhaust gas**.
- Roughly **30 % – 35 %** is absorbed by the **engine coolant** (jacket water).
- Roughly **5 % – 10 %** is lost as **radiation and convection** from the engine block surface.

The heater core taps into the **coolant stream** — the second-largest waste-heat sink — and recovers a portion of it for cabin heating. This makes the automotive heater an elegant example of **passive heat recovery with zero additional fuel penalty**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Industry Bench):** A mid-size passenger car typically requires a **heater core thermal capacity between 3 kW and 7 kW** to raise cabin air from −10 °C ambient to 25 °C within 5 – 10 minutes.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Coolant-Based Automotive Heater

The heater core is fundamentally a **liquid-to-air cross-flow heat exchanger** of compact construction. The hot engine coolant (typically **85 °C – 105 °C** in normal operation) flows through tubes, while cabin air is forced across the external finned surface by the blower.

### 2.1.1 The Two Heat-Transfer Loops

1. **Hot-Side (Coolant Loop):** Coolant enters the heater core at temperature $T_{h,in}$, gives up sensible heat, and exits at $T_{h,out}$. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}_h$.
2. **Cold-Side (Air Loop):** Cabin air enters at $T_{c,in}$ (which may be ambient or partially recirculated), gains sensible heat, and exits as supply air at $T_{c,out}$. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$.

The heat-transfer rate $Q$ is governed by:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (typically **100 – 300 W/m²·K** for compact automotive heater cores)
- $A$ = effective heat-transfer surface area of the core (typically **0.05 m² – 0.20 m²**)
- $c_{p,h}$ = specific heat of ethylene-glycol coolant mix ≈ **3.6 kJ/kg·K** (50:50 mix)
- $c_{p,a}$ = specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**

## 2.2 Classification of Automotive Heaters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification — Memorize This Table:**

| S.No. | Heater Type | Energy Source | Typical Application | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coolant-Based Heater Core** | Engine coolant waste heat | All IC-engine passenger cars | 60 – 80 % |
| 2 | **Fuel-Operated Auxiliary Heater (FOAH)** | Diesel / Petrol burner | Diesel trucks, RVs, cold-start aid | 85 – 95 % |
| 3 | **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Electric Heater** | High-voltage battery (400 V / 800 V) | Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) | 95 – 99 % |
| 4 | **Heat Pump (Reversed AC Cycle)** | Refrigerant cycle | Modern EVs (Tesla, Nissan Leaf) | 200 – 300 % COP |
| 5 | **Catalytic Heater** | Catalytic oxidation of fuel | Military vehicles, ambulances | 90 % |
| 6 | **Exhaust Gas Heater** | Engine exhaust | Some heavy trucks | Variable |

## 2.3 Engine Heating Systems (Cold-Start Aids)

Engine heating is the complementary problem: when the ambient temperature is very low, the engine oil becomes viscous, battery cranking power drops, and fuel atomization worsens. **Engine heaters** pre-warm the engine block, oil, or coolant before start.

### 2.3.1 Types of Engine Heaters

| Heater | Mounting Location | Power Rating | Warm-up Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block heater (immersion type)** | Freeze-plug in engine block | **400 W – 1500 W** | 2 – 4 hours |
| **Oil pan heater (silicone pad)** | Bonded to oil pan | **50 W – 200 W** | 4 – 6 hours |
| **Coolant heater (circulating type)** | Inline in lower radiator hose | **1500 W – 3000 W** | 30 – 60 min |
| **Intake air heater (grid/thermistor)** | Air intake manifold | **800 W – 2000 W** (diesel) | < 30 s |
| **Glow plug (diesel)** | Cylinder head per cylinder | **6 V / 12 V / 24 V**, 5 – 10 s | Pre-start only |

## 2.4 Thermodynamic Analysis — The "Why" Behind the Heat Transfer

### 2.4.1 Sensible Heat Load of the Cabin

The cabin loses heat to the outside through the windshield, side glass, body panels, and due to ventilation/infiltration. The heater must supply this loss to maintain set-point temperature.

$$
Q_{\text{loss}} = (U_{\text{glass}} A_{\text{glass}} + U_{\text{body}} A_{\text{body}} + \dot{m}_{\text{inf}} c_{p,a}) \cdot (T_{\text{cabin}} - T_{\text{ambient}})
$$

### 2.4.2 Heat-Transfer Rate Through Composite Body

For the curved windshield (laminated glass with PVB interlayer) the U-value is approximately **5.5 W/m²·K**, while for the steel body panels it is roughly **3.0 – 4.0 W/m²·K** after including convective surface resistances.

### 2.4.3 Why Heater Cores Use Aluminium, Not Copper

Aluminium has:
- Thermal conductivity $k_{Al} \approx 237 \text{ W/m·K}$ (vs. copper's 401 W/m·K) — lower, but…
- Density $\rho_{Al} \approx 2700 \text{ kg/m³}$ (vs. copper's 8960 kg/m³) — **3.3× lighter**
- Lower cost, and forms a corrosion-resistant oxide layer that suits ethylene-glycol coolant.

This trade-off favours aluminium for mass-sensitive automotive applications.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these eight equations — they cover > 90 % of numerical questions.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} c_{p} \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate (any fluid) |
| 2 | $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Heat exchanger sizing (NTU / LMTD method) |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference (counter-flow) |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{loss}} = U A \, \Delta T$ | Cabin heat loss through walls / glass |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_a c_{p,a} \Delta T}$ | Required blower air volume flow rate |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}$ | Required coolant mass flow through heater core |
| 7 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{\min}}$ | Effectiveness-NTU method parameter |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1+C_r)}$ | Effectiveness of single-pass cross-flow heater |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive OEM design** | Sizing heater core for cold-start cabin bring-up in ≤ 5 min |
| **EV thermal management** | Replacing PTC with heat pumps to extend driving range by 10 – 20 % |
| **Cold-climate logistics** | Engine block heaters in fleet trucks (reduce DPF regeneration cycles) |
| **Defence / Aerospace** | Catalytic heaters in armoured vehicles (silent operation, no flame) |
| **Emissions control** | Faster catalyst light-off → lower HC and CO cold-start emissions |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Blower Air-Flow Rate

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car cabin requires **Q = 4.5 kW** of heating power. The blower supplies air at $T_{c,in} = 5 \, °C$ (ambient), and the heater core must deliver supply air at $T_{c,out} = 45 \, °C$. Calculate the required mass-flow rate and volume-flow rate of air.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known numerical values.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \times 1005 \, \frac{J}{kg \cdot K} \times (45 - 5) \, K
$$

$$
4500 \, W = \dot{m}_a \times 1005 \times 40
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mass-flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{4500}{1005 \times 40} = \dfrac{4500}{40200} = 0.1119 \, \frac{kg}{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass-flow to volume-flow using air density at ~25 °C, $\rho_a = 1.184$ kg/m³.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{0.1119}{1.184} = 0.0945 \, \frac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in CFM (cubic feet per minute) for industry reference.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = 0.0945 \times 2118.88 = 200.2 \, \text{CFM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A ~200 CFM blower is the typical rating for a mid-size sedan. This corresponds to a complete cabin air change roughly every **30 – 60 seconds**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Coolant Mass Flow Through Heater Core

**Problem Statement:** The same heater core receives coolant at $T_{h,in} = 90 \, °C$ and must supply Q = 4.5 kW. The coolant leaves at $T_{h,out} = 75 \, °C$. The coolant is 50:50 ethylene-glycol-water with $c_{p,h} = 3.6 \, kJ/kg\cdot K$. Find $\dot{m}_h$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the energy balance on the coolant side.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values.**

$$
4500 \, W = \dot{m}_h \times 3600 \, \frac{J}{kg \cdot K} \times (90 - 75) \, K
$$

$$
4500 = \dot{m}_h \times 3600 \times 15 = 54000 \, \dot{m}_h
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\dot{m}_h$.**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{4500}{54000} = 0.0833 \, \frac{kg}{s} = 5.0 \, \frac{kg}{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/min using coolant density $\rho_h \approx 1.07$ kg/L.**

$$
\dot{V}_h = \dfrac{0.0833}{1.07} = 0.0779 \, \frac{L}{s} \approx 4.68 \, \frac{L}{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-Balance Cross-Check (Reynolds Analogy):** The ratio of air-side to coolant-side mass flow is $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_h = 0.1119 / 0.0833 \approx 1.34$. Since $c_{p,h}/c_{p,a} = 3600/1005 \approx 3.58$, the heat-capacity rate ratio $C_{\min}/C_{\max} = (1005 \times 0.1119)/(3600 \times 0.0833) = 0.374$ — indicating the **air-side is the limiting (minimum) stream**, a classic design insight.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LMTD for the Heater Core (Counter-Flow Approximation)

For a compact heater core with **crossed streams** (coolant flows through tubes perpendicular to air flow across fins), the **counter-flow LMTD** is used as an upper-bound estimate and corrected with a configuration factor $F$ (typically 0.75 – 0.90).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the four terminal temperatures.**

$$
T_{h,in}, \, T_{h,out}, \, T_{c,in}, \, T_{c,out}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the two end-differences (counter-flow orientation).**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the LMTD integral.** Starting from a differential element $dA$:

$$
dQ = U \, dA \, (T_h - T_c)
$$

Assuming linear temperature profiles (a valid approximation for fluids with constant $c_p$ and negligible phase change):

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{T_h - T_c} = \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \, dA \, \left( \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_a c_{p,a}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between inlet and outlet.**

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_a} \right) \int_0^{A} dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U A \left( \dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_a} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the standard LMTD expression.**

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example using previous data.**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 45 = 45 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 75 - 5 = 70 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{45 - 70}{\ln(45 / 70)} = \dfrac{-25}{-0.4418} = 56.59 \, °C
$$

**Step 7 — Apply cross-flow correction factor $F = 0.85$.**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{actual}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 0.85 \times 56.59 = 48.10 \, °C
$$

**Step 8 — Required heat-transfer area (assume $U = 180$ W/m²·K).**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{4500}{180 \times 48.10} = 0.520 \, m^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A heater core of **~0.52 m² effective surface area** (typical for a sedan) is required. This corresponds to a physically compact core of dimensions roughly **200 mm × 130 mm × 30 mm** with high-density louvered fins.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effectiveness-NTU Verification

Using the data above, validate the heat-exchanger effectiveness.

**Step 1 — Compute heat-capacity rates.**

$$
C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 0.0833 \times 3600 = 299.9 \, W/K
$$

$$
C_a = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a} = 0.1119 \times 1005 = 112.5 \, W/K
$$

**Step 2 — Identify $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$.**

$$
C_{\min} = C_a = 112.5 \, W/K \quad ; \quad C_{\max} = C_h = 299.9 \, W/K
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity ratio.**

$$
C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} = \dfrac{112.5}{299.9} = 0.375
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum possible heat transfer.**

$$
Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 112.5 \times (90 - 5) = 9562.5 \, W
$$

**Step 5 — Effectiveness.**

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \dfrac{4500}{9562.5} = 0.471 \; (\text{or } 47.1 \, \%)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute NTU.**

$$
\text{NTU} = -\dfrac{\ln[1 - \varepsilon(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r} = -\dfrac{\ln[1 - 0.471 \times 1.375]}{1.375}
$$

$$
\text{NTU} = -\dfrac{\ln(0.352)}{1.375} = \dfrac{1.044}{1.375} = 0.759
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity-check $UA$ product.**

$$
UA = \text{NTU} \times C_{\min} = 0.759 \times 112.5 = 85.4 \, W/K
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{UA}{U} = \dfrac{85.4}{180} = 0.474 \, m^2
$$

The small discrepancy with the LMTD-derived value (0.520 m²) arises from the configuration factor $F = 0.85$ — both methods are internally consistent.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Summary

| Variable | Change | Effect on Required Heater Core Area | Design Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ambient $T_{amb} \downarrow$ | Cabin $\Delta T$ rises | Area must increase | Add PTC booster in EVs |
| Coolant inlet $T_{h,in} \uparrow$ | Larger $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Area decreases | Use coolant control valve for hotter flow |
| Air flow $\dot{m}_a \uparrow$ | $C_{\min}$ rises → $C_r$ falls | Area decreases but noise rises | Cap blower at ~250 CFM |
| Coolant flow $\dot{m}_h \downarrow$ | $C_r$ rises → area increases | More area needed | Specify minimum coolant circulation |
| Glass area $A_{\text{glass}} \uparrow$ | Cabin heat loss rises | Larger Q → larger core | Apply low-e coating to windshield |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"With a neat sketch, explain the working of an automotive coolant-based heater."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Component identification (1 mark):** The system consists of an IC engine, a heater core (a small liquid-to-air heat exchanger), a blower motor, hoses, and a thermostatic blend door.

2. **Working principle (1.5 marks):** Hot coolant (~**90 °C**) leaving the engine block is routed through the heater core via a control valve. Simultaneously, the blower pushes cabin air across the external fins of the core. Heat transfers from the coolant (through tubes and fins) to the air, raising its temperature from ambient (~**5 °C**) to about **45 °C**. The blend door mixes this heated air with cold fresh air to achieve the desired outlet temperature.

3. **Flow continuity (0.5 mark):** The cooled coolant returns to the water pump inlet, completing the loop. The system uses **waste heat** from the engine, so there is **no additional fuel penalty**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"List and briefly explain any three types of engine heaters used for cold-start assistance."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Block Heater (1 mark):** An electrical resistance heater (typically **750 W – 1500 W**) screwed into a freeze-plug hole on the engine block. Plugged into mains power 2 – 4 hours before start; warms the coolant jacket and cylinder walls to ~**30 – 40 °C**.

2. **Oil Pan Heater (1 mark):** A silicone-rubber resistive pad bonded to the underside of the oil pan. Power rating **50 – 200 W**. It lowers oil viscosity, reducing cranking torque and wear during start.

3. **Intake Air Heater (1 mark):** A grid or thermistor element in the intake manifold of diesel engines. Energised for **5 – 10 seconds** before cranking, it heats the inducted air to **50 – 80 °C**, improving atomisation and reducing white-smoke emissions during cold start.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 — Internal Choice (Choose either Question A or Question B).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

> **(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) heater** used in battery electric vehicles. Why is it preferred over a resistive coil heater? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A heater core in a passenger car receives coolant at $T_{h,in} = 95 \, °C$ and discharges it at $T_{h,out} = 78 \, °C$. The blower delivers air at the rate of **220 CFM** at **8 °C** and **101.325 kPa**. The air leaves the heater at **48 °C**. Compute:
> (i) the heat-transfer rate $Q$,
> (ii) the air-side mass flow rate,
> (iii) the coolant mass flow rate (take $c_{p,h} = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of PTC Heater (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A PTC heater consists of a **ceramic element** (typically barium titanate, $BaTiO_3$, doped with rare-earth elements like lanthanum or yttrium).
- The ceramic pellets are arranged in **parallel-flow fins** between two metallised electrodes.
- A **blower fan** is integrated downstream to drive cabin air across the fins.
- The unit operates from the **traction battery** (typically **400 V** or **800 V DC** in modern EVs).
- Total power per module: **2 kW – 7 kW**, often multiple modules stacked for higher capacity.

**Working Principle (2.5 marks):**
- Below the **Curie temperature** ($T_c \approx 120 \, °C$ for automotive PTC), the ceramic has low resistance → high current → rapid self-heating.
- As temperature rises above $T_c$, the resistance **increases exponentially** (by **3 – 5 orders of magnitude**), automatically limiting the current and stabilising the temperature.
- This **self-regulating** behaviour eliminates the need for a separate thermal cut-out or thermostat — a critical safety advantage at high voltages.

**Why PTC over Resistive Coil? (1.5 marks):**
- **Inherent safety:** Self-limiting temperature prevents thermal runaway and fire risk.
- **No red-hot filament:** Reduced combustion risk in case of blower failure.
- **Compact and lightweight:** Higher power density (~**10 W/cm²** vs. 5 W/cm²).
- **Long life:** No filament burnout; > **10 000 hours** typical.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\dot{V}_a = 220$ CFM; $T_{c,in} = 8 \, °C$; $T_{c,out} = 48 \, °C$; $T_{h,in} = 95 \, °C$; $T_{h,out} = 78 \, °C$; $c_{p,h} = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Convert CFM to SI volume-flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_a = 220 \, \dfrac{ft^3}{min} \times \dfrac{0.02832 \, m^3}{ft^3} \times \dfrac{1}{60} \, \dfrac{min}{s} = 0.1038 \, \dfrac{m^3}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute air density at inlet conditions (~8 °C, 1 atm).**

$$
\rho_a = \dfrac{P}{R T} = \dfrac{101325}{287 \times 281} = 1.256 \, \dfrac{kg}{m^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Air-side mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_a = 1.256 \times 0.1038 = 0.1304 \, \dfrac{kg}{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat-transfer rate Q.**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 0.1304 \times 1005 \times (48 - 8)
$$

$$
Q = 0.1304 \times 1005 \times 40 = 5242 \, W \approx 5.24 \, kW
$$

**Step 5 — Coolant mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})} = \dfrac{5242}{3600 \times (95 - 78)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{5242}{61200} = 0.0856 \, \dfrac{kg}{s} \approx 5.14 \, \dfrac{kg}{min}
$$

**Step 6 — Coolant volume flow (density ≈ 1.07 kg/L).**

$$
\dot{V}_h = \dfrac{0.0856}{1.07} = 0.0800 \, \dfrac{L}{s} \approx 4.80 \, \dfrac{L}{min}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

> **(a)** Compare **fuel-operated auxiliary heaters (FOAH)** and **heat pump systems** as cabin-heating solutions for modern electric vehicles. Discuss their respective Coefficient of Performance (COP), range impact, and packaging. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A heater core has an overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 200 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$ and an effective area $A = 0.45 \, m^2$. Using the LMTD method, compute the heat-transfer rate for counter-flow operation with the following terminal temperatures:
> $T_{h,in} = 92 \, °C$, $T_{h,out} = 76 \, °C$, $T_{c,in} = 6 \, °C$, $T_{c,out} = 50 \, °C$.
> State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — FOAH vs Heat Pump Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | FOAH (Fuel-Operated Auxiliary Heater) | Heat Pump (Reversed AC Cycle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Diesel / petrol from tank | Traction battery + refrigerant |
| **COP** | **0.85 – 0.95** (thermal / fuel) | **2.0 – 3.0** at 0 °C, drops to **1.2 – 1.5** at −20 °C |
| **Range impact on EV** | **Zero** (uses fuel), but adds mass **+5 – 8 kg** | Reduces EV range by **10 – 20 %** in winter |
| **Packaging** | Burner + combustion chamber + exhaust pipe | Same hardware as AC + reversible valve + accumulator |
| **Warm-up time** | **2 – 4 minutes** to full heat | **30 – 60 seconds** |
| **Noise / vibration** | Combustion roar (~**55 dBA**) | Quiet (~**40 dBA**) |
| **Maintenance** | Spark plug, fuel pump, exhaust | Same as AC system |
| **Use case** | Cold-climate EVs, range-extender EVs | Mainstream EVs (Tesla Model Y, Hyundai Ioniq 5) |

**Key Insights (1 mark):**
- Modern OEMs increasingly prefer **heat pumps** for environmental reasons (no on-board combustion) and because the **multiplied COP** (delivering 2 – 3 kWh of heat per 1 kWh of electricity) is more efficient than resistive PTC heating.
- However, at ambient temperatures below **−10 °C**, heat pump performance degrades sharply, and OEMs (e.g., Nissan, BMW) revert to PTC boost.

**Part (b) — LMTD Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. Constant $c_p$ for both fluids.
3. No heat loss to surroundings (well-insulated core).
4. No phase change on either side.
5. Counter-flow arrangement (true LMTD formula applies).

**Step 1 — Calculate end-temperature differences (counter-flow).**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 92 - 50 = 42 \, °C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 76 - 6 = 70 \, °C
$$

**Step 2 — Apply LMTD formula.**

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{42 - 70}{\ln(42 / 70)} = \dfrac{-28}{-0.5108} = 54.82 \, °C
$$

**Step 3 — Apply correction factor for cross-flow.**

For a cross-flow heat exchanger with both fluids unmixed, use $F \approx 0.88$.

$$
\Delta T_{\text{actual}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 0.88 \times 54.82 = 48.24 \, °C
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat-transfer rate.**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{actual}} = 200 \times 0.45 \times 48.24
$$

$$
Q = 4341.6 \, W \approx 4.34 \, kW
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion (1 mark).**

This heat-transfer rate is sufficient for a compact passenger car cabin under moderate winter conditions (−5 °C to +5 °C ambient). For harsher climates, either a larger area or higher coolant inlet temperature (e.g., from a supplementary PTC) would be required.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks Every Semester:**
> 1. **Confusing air-side and coolant-side mass flow** — students often swap $\dot{m}_a$ and $\dot{m}_h$. Always identify which fluid is the **minimum** capacity stream ($C_{\min}$) first.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert CFM to SI units** — 1 CFM ≠ 1 m³/s. Conversion factor: **1 ft³/min = 4.719 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s**.
> 3. **Using dry-air $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K** instead of $c_p \approx 1010$ J/kg·K at higher temperatures — a 0.5 % error, often acceptable, but check question context.
> 4. **Missing the cross-flow correction factor F** — pure LMTD gives an *ideal* value; for real automotive cores use $F = 0.75 - 0.90$.
> 5. **Ignoring the heat-capacity ratio $C_r$** in effectiveness-NTU derivations — many textbook solutions assume $C_r = 0$ (single-stream dominance), which is **not** valid for low coolant flow conditions.
> 6. **Confusing PTC with resistance wire** — PTC ceramic is **self-regulating** by its ferroelectric Curie transition; it is NOT a simple resistor.
> 7. **Mixing up heat pump and AC cycle directions** — in heating mode the refrigerant condenses **inside** the cabin coil (releases heat indoors) and evaporates outside.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Automotive heater = passive waste-heat recovery device** that uses ~30 % of fuel energy rejected via coolant.
- **Heater core** = compact **liquid-to-air cross-flow heat exchanger**, typically aluminium, $A = 0.05 - 0.20$ m².
- **Typical thermal capacity:** **3 – 7 kW** for a mid-size sedan.
- **Standard coolant temperatures:** inlet **85 – 95 °C**, outlet **70 – 80 °C**.
- **Air supply temperature:** **40 – 50 °C** at the plenum under full-heater conditions.
- **Blower rating:** **150 – 250 CFM** typical for passenger cars.
- **Master formula:** $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.
- **Six types of automotive heaters:** Coolant-based, FOAH, PTC, Heat Pump, Catalytic, Exhaust-gas.
- **Five types of engine heaters:** Block, Oil-pad, Coolant-circulating, Intake-air, Glow-plug.
- **PTC heater advantage:** Self-regulating via Curie transition ($T_c \approx 120 °C$); no thermal runaway.
- **Heat pump COP:** **2.0 – 3.0** at 0 °C ambient; drops to ~**1.2** at −20 °C.
- **Effectiveness of typical heater core:** **40 – 60 %**.
- **Cabin comfort set-point:** **20 – 26 °C** as per ASHRAE 55.
- **Windshield U-value:** ~**5.5 W/m²·K** for laminated glass.
- **Defogging is a safety-critical function** governed by ECE R21 / FMVSS 103.
- **EVs have no engine waste heat** → require PTC, heat pump, or FOAH.
- **Always include the cross-flow correction factor $F$** in LMTD-based heater-core sizing.
- **Standard ambient test conditions:** **−10 °C to +5 °C** for cold-start performance rating.
